\frontmatter
\chapter*{Abstract}
\addcontentsline{toc}{chapter}{Abstract}

Logic admits a single irreducible distinction: true or false. From this binary structure, a strict constraint follows: any system that produces a contradiction under its own requirements cannot persist.

This work applies that constraint formally and mechanically. Each step is verified in Agda‚ÄĒno external axioms, no postulates, no assumptions beyond what logical consistency itself demands. Every structure is justified solely by the elimination of alternatives that fail.

The result is not constructive in the traditional sense. It is reductive: a progressive filtration of everything that does not survive its own internal requirements.

What remains‚ÄĒ$K_4$‚ÄĒis not introduced as a model or hypothesis. It is the minimal structure that elimination cannot touch.

The combinatorial invariants of this structure---vertex count, edge topology, spectral decomposition, Euler characteristic---were not adjusted to match any prior measurement. They are necessities of the derivation. Their numerical values can then be compared with independently measured constants of physics, including the fine-structure constant $\alpha^{-1} \approx 137$. That comparison is not part of the derivation; it is the interpretive question this work places before the reader.

A derivation so compressed touches many disciplines at once‚ÄĒlogic, combinatorics, group theory, spectral analysis, physics‚ÄĒand every one of them was built by someone else. The debts are large because the chain is long.

\section*{Debts}
\addcontentsline{toc}{chapter}{Debts}

None of the ideas in this work are new. The derivation is a single thread; what follows names the hands that spun it.

The starting point‚ÄĒthat a distinction, not a thing, is the irreducible atom of form‚ÄĒbelongs to George Spencer-Brown. The conviction that this is not metaphor but physics, that reality reduces to yes-or-no at every point, is John Archibald Wheeler's \textit{it from bit}.

That such a claim can be made rigorous at all is owed to the constructivists. Brouwer insisted that existence demands a witness. Heyting gave that demand algebraic form. Curry and Howard revealed that proofs and programs are the same act. Per Martin-L√∂f forged these insights into a type theory strong enough to carry mathematics, and Voevodsky showed what must be given up‚ÄĒaxiom K, uniqueness of identity proofs‚ÄĒif homotopy is to be taken seriously. Every line of this work executes inside those constraints.

The combinatorial backbone has older roots. Euler first counted vertices, edges, and faces as a single invariant. Cayley classified graphs by their symmetries. Birkhoff established when a representation is canonical‚ÄĒwhen structure admits exactly one normal form. These are the tools that force $K_4$, and only $K_4$, out of the filtration.

That the survivor carries physical content is owed to the tradition of spectral analysis: Fourier decomposed signals into modes, Laplace gave the operator its name, Hilbert made the space precise. In this work, the Laplacian of $K_4$ yields a spectrum‚ÄĒ$\{0,4,4,4\}$‚ÄĒthat was not chosen but inherited.

What connects spectrum to measurement is symmetry, and symmetry has its own lineage. Galois revealed that solvability is a property of the group, not the equation. Noether proved that every continuous symmetry produces a conserved quantity. Curie showed that what breaks the symmetry is as real as what preserves it. Here, the automorphism group $\mathrm{Aut}(K_4) \cong S_4$ is the arbiter: only quantities invariant under it qualify as observables.

The physical endgame was prepared by those who first extracted constants from constraint. Sommerfeld isolated $\alpha$. Dirac demanded that the electron obey logic so strict it predicted the positron. Feynman showed that all paths contribute‚ÄĒand that consistency among them is the only filter. Planck proved that nature herself quantizes. The claim that $\alpha^{-1} = 137$ falls out of combinatorial necessity, not measurement, is placed before a tradition that earned the right to test it.

The categorical perspective‚ÄĒthat structure is characterized not by what it \emph{is} but by the maps it \emph{admits}‚ÄĒdescends from Lawvere and the tradition of Tarski, where fixpoints are not found but forced by the architecture of the logic itself.

What this work contributes is not a new insight. It is a tool applied: the Agda type checker, which enforces logical consistency without exception, turned loose on the question these people raised from different directions. If the result has any value, it is because the filter is trustworthy‚ÄĒnot because the author is clever.

\mainmatter

\chapter{Introduction}
\label{ch:introduction}

\section{Three Questions, One Filter}
\label{sec:introduction:three-questions-one-filter}

Mathematics asks: which structures are consistent?
Physics asks: which laws survive observation?
Logic asks: which statements can be decided at all?

These appear to be fundamentally different questions. They use different languages. They live in different departments. Their practitioners rarely read each other's journals. And yet, when pushed far enough, all three arrive at the same boundary: each discipline possesses an internal filter that it did not choose. Mathematics eliminates structures that contradict themselves. Physics eliminates theories that violate observed invariants. Logic eliminates propositions that cannot be assigned a truth value without inconsistency.

The mechanism is the same everywhere. What cannot stand under its own requirements, falls. The stages are different. The verdict is identical.

This raises an uncomfortable question: if the filter is the same, could the \textit{survivor} also be the same?

\section{Elimination as Method}
\label{sec:introduction:elimination-as-method}

The standard approach in formal work is constructive. One posits axioms, defines structures, proves theorems, and builds upward. The direction of reasoning is from assumption to consequence.

This work reverses that direction entirely.

Nothing is assumed. Nothing is postulated. Instead, a single starting point is taken‚ÄĒthe most primitive act of logic: drawing a distinction, separating what \textit{is} from what \textit{is not}‚ÄĒand then a relentless filter is applied. At every stage, we ask: which structures survive their own consistency requirements? Those that do not are eliminated. Those that do, persist‚ÄĒnot by our choice, but because elimination could not reach them.

This is not a new idea. It is how logic has always worked. But the full consequences of applying this filter \textit{mechanically}, from the ground up, without exception, have not been traced before.

\section{The Tool: Agda as Executioner}
\label{sec:introduction:agda-as-executioner}

A filter is only as trustworthy as its enforcement. Human reasoning is fallible: we overlook edge cases, confuse sufficiency with necessity, and mistake intuition for proof. This is why every step in this work is formalized and verified in Agda, a dependently typed proof assistant.

Agda does not care about elegance or plausibility. It checks one thing: does this term have the type it claims to have? If yes, the step stands. If no, it is rejected. There is no override, no escape hatch.

The compiler flags used in this work‚ÄĒ\AgdaFlag{safe} and \AgdaFlag{without-K}‚ÄĒfurther restrict the environment. No external axioms can be assumed. No postulates can be introduced. No appeal to the uniqueness of identity proofs is permitted. The system is locked into the narrowest possible corridor of logical consistency, and then forced to walk.

\section{What Happens Here}
\label{sec:introduction:what-happens-here}

When the proofs become detailed, the global line of force is easy to lose from view. The argument itself has a single irreversible spine:

\[
\begin{aligned}
  & D_0
  \longrightarrow
  \text{minimal logical infrastructure}
  \longrightarrow
  \text{classification}
  \longrightarrow
  \text{drift and reachability} \\
  & \longrightarrow
  \text{presentation and fixpoint}
  \longrightarrow
  K_4
  \longrightarrow
  \text{arithmetic and spectral invariants}
  \longrightarrow
  \text{physics record.}
\end{aligned}
\]

\subsection{The Elimination Protocol}
\label{sec:introduction:elimination-protocol}

Before entering the proofs, the reader deserves a compact ledger of the entire eliminative chain. At every step below, the column on the right names the alternatives that were tested and destroyed. $K_4$ is what remains after every row has been executed. Nothing in this list was chosen; everything was forced by the failure of its rivals.

\begin{center}
\small
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.35}
\begin{tabular}{@{}r l p{5.2cm} p{5.2cm}@{}}
\toprule
\textbf{\#} & \textbf{Survivor} & \textbf{Why it survives} & \textbf{What was eliminated} \\
\midrule
1 & Distinction ($D_0$)
  & The bare act of separating $\ell$ from $r$. Any formal system that cannot distinguish true from false is vacuous.
  & Vacuous carriers, carriers without boundary witnesses, carriers where $\ell = r$. \\
2 & Two-class coverage
  & Every element of the carrier equals $\ell$ or $r$. No unclassified residue is possible.
  & Carriers with hidden elements, partial classifications, carriers with $>2$ equivalence classes. \\
3 & Four endomorphisms
  & Coverage forces $|\mathrm{End}(S)| = 2 \times 2 = 4$. Each case is mutually distinct.
  & $K_1$ (1 vertex --- cannot host 4 distinct maps), $K_2$ (2 vertices --- collapses constant maps), $K_3$ (3 vertices --- one case always orphaned). \\
4 & Drift acyclicity
  & Applying classification to classification produces a level tower. The reachability relation on that tower is well-founded.
  & Cyclic level structures, infinite descending chains, non-well-founded hierarchies. \\
5 & Presentation fixpoint
  & Every reachability witness reduces to a canonical finite form. The observable that measures proofs factors through a unique base.
  & Non-canonical representations, observables that depend on proof-internal choices, redundant witnesses. \\
6 & $K_4$
  & The complete graph on four vertices is the unique fixpoint of the filter. No proper subgraph survives all five constraints above.
  & Every proper subgraph of $K_4$, every supergraph, every non-complete topology on four vertices. \\
7 & Derived arithmetic
  & $K_4$ forces counting (naturals), orientation (integers), spectral ratios (rationals), limit closure (reals).
  & Number systems not grounded in $K_4$ structure, algebraic laws that contradict the spectral data. \\
8 & Physics record
  & The combinatorial invariants of $K_4$ ($V=4$, $E=6$, $\chi=7$, $\mathrm{Spec}=\{0,4,4,4\}$) yield $\alpha^{-1}=137$ by evaluation. No free parameter.
  & Any alternative constant that would require a different graph or a tunable parameter. \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

The protocol is the backbone of the argument. If any single row could be replaced by a genuine alternative---if, at any step, the eliminated rival could survive instead---then the derivation would branch and the uniqueness claim would fail. The remainder of the book is the proof that no such branch exists.

This book proceeds in six parts. The first four derive $K_4$ through elimination. The fifth builds the arithmetic its structure makes necessary. The sixth computes the physical constants its invariants determine.

\textbf{Part I (Foundation)} establishes the minimal infrastructure that any consistent formal system must carry: equality, pairing, negation, decidability, and the natural numbers. None is introduced by fiat. Each is shown to be the unique survivor when the alternative is tested for internal consistency and fails.

\textbf{Part II (Drift and Reachability)} discovers what happens when classification is applied to classification itself. Each level of the type hierarchy is forced by the unclassified residue of the level below it. The reach proofs in this part track that ascent through the tower.

\textbf{Part III (Heteromorphisms and Presentation)} extends the classification to maps between two distinct carriers---not just within one. The same four cases survive. The presentation theorems then reduce every reachability fact to a finite canonical witness.

\textbf{Part IV (Observables and Fixpoint)} identifies which quantities that functionals can measure on proofs are actually determined by structure rather than proof-choice. The answer is a fixpoint: every admissible observable factors through a unique base computation. The K$_4$ graph---four vertices, six edges, complete---is the result of applying this filter to everything derived so far.

\textbf{Part V (Derived Arithmetic)} builds the number systems that K$_4$'s structure demands. Integers arise from orientation. Rationals arise from spectral ratios. Reals close the Cauchy gaps. Measurement, scaling, and distance laws follow---all without any postulate beyond what Parts I through IV established.

\textbf{Part VI (Physics from $K_4$)} assembles the invariants into a verified computation record. The vertex count, edge structure, Euler characteristic, and spectral decomposition of $K_4$ produce numerical values that can then be compared with independently measured constants of physics. The computation is theorem; the comparison is interpretation. The former is proved, machine-verified, and offered first. The latter is left open to the reader's evaluation.

The chapters do not wander away from this chain. They only slow down at different points in order to verify that no weaker alternative survives. The reader is invited to verify every claim. The source of this book is itself the proof: a literate Agda file where prose and machine-checked code are one and the same document.

\section{How To Read This Book}
\label{sec:introduction:how-to-read}

This document can be entered from more than one side.

\textbf{For the reader following the main argument:} read the Abstract, this Introduction, the opening paragraphs of each Part, \emph{Graph Presentation}, \emph{An Unexpected Correspondence}, \emph{K\textsubscript{4} Physics Constants Layer}, and the Conclusion. That path carries the global claim from distinction to $K_4$ to the physics record without demanding that every intermediate proof be read in full on the first pass.

\textbf{For the reader testing the formal necessity:} follow the code blocks and their immediate narrative wrappers. The prose states what pressure is being applied; the Agda code shows exactly which constructions survive it.

\textbf{For the reader testing the physics claim first:} begin with \emph{Graph Presentation}, then \emph{An Unexpected Correspondence}, then the openings of \emph{Derived Arithmetic} and \emph{Physics from $K_4$}, and only then descend into the intermediate derivations as needed. The point is not to delay the claim, but to make clear which earlier chapters each later computation depends on.

What begins now is the first irreversible pressure test. The question is no longer whether distinction can be described, but whether it can survive without borrowing hidden structure from outside itself. If that survival fails here, everything later collapses into rhetoric. If it holds, the path to $K_4$ is no longer a vision. It is a sentence already being executed.

\part{Foundation}

\chapter{The Foundation of Necessity}
\label{ch:foundation-necessity}

Before a single distinction can be drawn, the environment in which it is drawn must itself be constrained. This is the first and perhaps most counterintuitive step: we do not begin by building. We begin by closing doors.

Consider what happens if we do not. A formal system that permits unproven axioms‚ÄĒpostulates accepted on faith‚ÄĒgains arbitrary degrees of freedom. Any structure derived from such a system inherits those degrees of freedom. The results would depend on which postulates a human happened to choose, not on what logic demands. So the first door closes: the Agda flag \AgdaFlag{safe} forbids all postulates. Nothing enters this system that is not constructed from within.

The second door concerns identity. In many formal systems, it is taken for granted that all proofs of the same equality are themselves identical‚ÄĒa principle known as Axiom K. This sounds harmless. But it imposes a hidden constraint: it flattens the space of proofs into a single layer. Higher-dimensional structure‚ÄĒwhere the \textit{way} two things are equal matters, not just \textit{that} they are‚ÄĒis silently collapsed before it can be examined. Since our investigation must remain open to relational complexity at every level, this premature flattening is unacceptable. The flag \AgdaFlag{without-K} prevents it.

The third door concerns self-reference. Without universe stratification, a type could contain itself as a member‚ÄĒan act that immediately produces paradox (Russell's paradox being the most famous instance). Agda's hierarchy of universe levels (\texttt{Level}, $\mathit{Set}_\omega$) prevents this by ensuring that every type lives strictly below the level that quantifies over it. No type can swallow itself.

These three constraints‚ÄĒno postulates, no identity collapse, no self-reference‚ÄĒare not design choices. They are the minimal conditions under which a formal system can reason about its own distinctions without contradiction. The module therefore opens under the same two restrictions just named. The code block that follows is the direct translation of these constraints into Agda.

\begin{code}[hide]
{-# OPTIONS --safe --without-K #-}
\end{code}

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Strict Environment Constraint: No axioms, no postulates, no Axiom K.
module FirstDistinction where

-- ¬ß Stratification primitives are forced to prevent self-reference paradoxes.
open import Agda.Primitive using (Level; lzero; lsuc; _‚äĒ_; SetŌČ)
\end{code}

\section{Propositional Equality}
\label{sec:foundation:propositional-equality}

With the environment locked down, the first question is immediate: when are two things the same?

This is not a philosophical musing. Without a stable notion of equality, no proof can refer to a previous result. No substitution can be performed. No structure can be recognized across contexts. A system without equality is a system where nothing follows from anything‚ÄĒwhere every statement is an island. That is not logic. That is noise.

So equality must exist. But what is the minimal form it can take? The answer is surprisingly tight. A single constructor suffices: \texttt{refl}, which states that a thing is equal to itself. Nothing more is assumed. From this one fact, the entire machinery of equality‚ÄĒsymmetry, transitivity, congruence, substitution‚ÄĒfollows by elimination. Not as additional axioms, but as the only functions the type checker will accept given the structure of \texttt{refl}. Try to define symmetry any other way, and Agda rejects it. There is exactly one implementation that typechecks.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Propositional equality: the identity type with unique constructor refl.
infix 4 _‚Č°_

data _‚Č°_ {‚Ąď : Level} {A : Set ‚Ąď} (x : A) : A ‚Üí Set ‚Ąď where
  refl : x ‚Č° x

-- ¬ß Symmetry is forced by elimination on refl.
sym : {‚Ąď : Level} {A : Set ‚Ąď} {x y : A} ‚Üí x ‚Č° y ‚Üí y ‚Č° x
sym refl = refl

-- ¬ß Transitivity is forced by elimination on the first proof.
trans : {‚Ąď : Level} {A : Set ‚Ąď} {x y z : A} ‚Üí x ‚Č° y ‚Üí y ‚Č° z ‚Üí x ‚Č° z
trans refl q = q

-- ¬ß Congruence: equality is invariant under function application.
cong : {‚Ąď‚āĀ ‚Ąď‚āā : Level} {A : Set ‚Ąď‚āĀ} {B : Set ‚Ąď‚āā} (f : A ‚Üí B) {x y : A} ‚Üí x ‚Č° y ‚Üí f x ‚Č° f y
cong f refl = refl

-- ¬ß Substitution: transport along an equality proof.
subst : {‚Ąď‚āĀ ‚Ąď‚āā : Level} {A : Set ‚Ąď‚āĀ} (P : A ‚Üí Set ‚Ąď‚āā) {x y : A} ‚Üí x ‚Č° y ‚Üí P x ‚Üí P y
subst P refl px = px
\end{code}

\section{Universe Lifting}
\label{sec:foundation:universe-lifting}

The stratification we imposed in the previous section‚ÄĒuniverse levels that prevent self-reference‚ÄĒcreates an immediate practical problem.
A proof living at one level cannot directly interact with a proof living at another.
The levels are sealed compartments. Without a controlled passage between them, the system would fragment into isolated strata that cannot communicate.

The passage must satisfy a strict requirement: moving a witness up to a higher level and back down again must return exactly the original witness, unchanged. If the round trip alters anything‚ÄĒintroduces noise, loses information, or shifts meaning‚ÄĒthen cross-level reasoning becomes unreliable.

\texttt{Lift} is the unique structure satisfying this constraint. It wraps a value and places it one level higher. \texttt{lower} unwraps it. The injectivity lemma that follows immediately proves that no information is lost: if two lifted values are equal, the originals were already equal. This is not a convenience‚ÄĒit is the proof that the elevator is faithful.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Lift embeds a type into the next universe level.
data Lift {‚Ąď : Level} (A : Set ‚Ąď) : Set (lsuc ‚Ąď) where
  lift : A ‚Üí Lift A

-- ¬ß Lower extracts the embedded value.
lower : {‚Ąď : Level} {A : Set ‚Ąď} ‚Üí Lift A ‚Üí A
lower (lift x) = x

-- ¬ß Lift is injective by construction.
lift-injective : {‚Ąď : Level} {A : Set ‚Ąď} {x y : A} ‚Üí lift x ‚Č° lift y ‚Üí x ‚Č° y
lift-injective refl = refl
\end{code}

\section{Universe-Polymorphic Equality}
\label{sec:foundation:universe-polymorphic-equality}

There is a ceiling in Agda's universe hierarchy: $\mathit{Set}_\omega$, the level that sits above all numbered levels. Statements that quantify over all universe levels‚ÄĒand such statements are unavoidable later‚ÄĒlive here.

But the equality type (\texttt{\_‚Č°\_}) was defined at a specific, bounded level. It cannot operate at $\mathit{Set}_\omega$. This is not a limitation that can be worked around; it is a hard type error. If the gap remains open, then the highest level of the system‚ÄĒthe level where the most general statements live‚ÄĒhas no notion of identity. Proofs at that level could not be compared, composed, or transported. The top would be lawless.

The solution is forced: replicate the identity type at $\mathit{Set}_\omega$. The replica, \texttt{\_‚Č°ŌČ\_}, has the same single constructor (\texttt{reflŌČ}) and the same derived operations. It is not a second identity type by choice‚ÄĒit is the only way to extend the guarantees of the lower levels to the ceiling without breaking the stratification that keeps the system sound.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Universe-polymorphic equality at SetŌČ.
infix 4 _‚Č°ŌČ_

data _‚Č°ŌČ_ {A : SetŌČ} (x : A) : A ‚Üí SetŌČ where
  reflŌČ : x ‚Č°ŌČ x

-- ¬ß Symmetry at SetŌČ.
symŌČ : {A : SetŌČ} {x y : A} ‚Üí x ‚Č°ŌČ y ‚Üí y ‚Č°ŌČ x
symŌČ reflŌČ = reflŌČ

-- ¬ß Transitivity at SetŌČ.
transŌČ : {A : SetŌČ} {x y z : A} ‚Üí x ‚Č°ŌČ y ‚Üí y ‚Č°ŌČ z ‚Üí x ‚Č°ŌČ z
transŌČ reflŌČ q = q

-- ¬ß Congruence at SetŌČ.
congŌČ : {A B : SetŌČ} (f : A ‚Üí B) {x y : A} ‚Üí x ‚Č°ŌČ y ‚Üí f x ‚Č°ŌČ f y
congŌČ f reflŌČ = reflŌČ

-- ¬ß Substitution at SetŌČ.
substŌČ : {A : SetŌČ} (P : A ‚Üí SetŌČ) {x y : A} ‚Üí x ‚Č°ŌČ y ‚Üí P x ‚Üí P y
substŌČ P reflŌČ px = px
\end{code}

\section{Products and Dependent Pairs}
\label{sec:foundation:products-dependent-pairs}

So far, every structure holds a single piece of information. But the moment a statement like ``there exists an element with a certain property'' must be expressed, two things must be held at once: the element and the proof of its property. Without a way to bundle these together, existential statements are inexpressible. The system can talk about individual objects but cannot witness claims about them.

The product type (\texttt{A √ó B}) is the simplest response: it holds two independent values side by side. But the more consequential structure is the dependent pair (\texttt{ő£ A B}), where the type of the second component depends on the value of the first. This is what makes it possible to say ``there exists an $a$ in $A$ such that $B(a)$ holds''‚ÄĒthe $a$ and its proof travel together as a single witness. Without dependent pairs, the gap between ``for all'' and ``there exists'' cannot be bridged, and half of logic collapses.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Non-dependent product of two types.
infixr 3 _√ó_

record _√ó_ (A B : Set) : Set where
  constructor _,_
  field
    fst : A
    snd : B

open _√ó_ public

-- ¬ß Dependent pair: the type of witnesses.
record ő£ (A : Set) (B : A ‚Üí Set) : Set where
  constructor _,_
  field
    fst : A
    snd : B fst

open ő£ public
\end{code}

\section{Absurdity and Negation}
\label{sec:foundation:absurdity-negation}

If the filter we described in the introduction is to have any teeth, it must be possible to express that something \textit{cannot} exist. A system that can only affirm‚ÄĒnever deny‚ÄĒhas no elimination power. It accepts everything. And a system that accepts everything proves nothing.

The empty type (\texttt{‚ä•}) is the formal expression of impossibility: a type with no constructors, and therefore no inhabitants. If you somehow hold a value of type \texttt{‚ä•}, you have a contradiction‚ÄĒand from a contradiction, anything follows. This is \texttt{‚ä•-elim}, the principle of \textit{ex falso quodlibet}: from the impossible, derive whatever you wish.

Negation falls out immediately. To say ``$A$ is false'' is to say ``$A$ leads to a contradiction''‚ÄĒformally, $\neg A$ is the function type $A \to \bot$. No additional negation primitive is needed. Double negation and inequality are then derived forms, not new axioms.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß The empty type: no constructors, no inhabitants.
data ‚ä• : Set where

-- ¬ß Ex falso: the empty type eliminates into any type.
‚ä•-elim : {‚Ąď : Level} {A : Set ‚Ąď} ‚Üí ‚ä• ‚Üí A
‚ä•-elim ()

-- ¬ß Negation: the unique map into absurdity.
¬¨_ : {‚Ąď : Level} ‚Üí Set ‚Ąď ‚Üí Set ‚Ąď
¬¨ A = A ‚Üí ‚ä•

-- ¬ß Double negation.
¬¨¬¨_ : {‚Ąď : Level} ‚Üí Set ‚Ąď ‚Üí Set ‚Ąď
¬¨¬¨ A = ¬¨ (¬¨ A)

-- ¬ß Inequality: negation of propositional equality.
infix 2 _‚Č†_
_‚Č†_ : {‚Ąď : Level} {A : Set ‚Ąď} ‚Üí A ‚Üí A ‚Üí Set ‚Ąď
x ‚Č† y = ¬¨ (x ‚Č° y)
\end{code}

\section{Disjoint Union}
\label{sec:foundation:disjoint-union}

Pairs express ``both A and B.'' But logic also requires ``either A or B''‚ÄĒand it requires knowing \textit{which one}. An untagged alternative‚ÄĒwhere the two cases are merged without a label‚ÄĒdestroys the ability to distinguish them. Case analysis becomes impossible.

The disjoint union (\texttt{A ‚äé B}) solves this by tagging each side: \texttt{inj‚āĀ} marks a witness from the left, \texttt{inj‚āā} from the right. Pattern matching can then always determine which case holds. The $\mathit{Set}_\omega$ variant (\texttt{\_‚äéŌČ\_}) extends this to the top universe level, for the same reason we extended equality: every level must carry the full logical vocabulary, or the system has blind spots.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Disjoint union (coproduct) of two types.
infixr 2 _‚äé_

data _‚äé_ {‚Ąď : Level} (A B : Set ‚Ąď) : Set ‚Ąď where
  inj‚āĀ : A ‚Üí A ‚äé B
  inj‚āā : B ‚Üí A ‚äé B

-- ¬ß Disjoint union at SetŌČ.
infixr 2 _‚äéŌČ_

data _‚äéŌČ_ (A B : SetŌČ) : SetŌČ where
  inj‚āĀŌČ : A ‚Üí A ‚äéŌČ B
  inj‚āāŌČ : B ‚Üí A ‚äéŌČ B
\end{code}

\section{Ontological Primitives}
\label{sec:foundation:ontological-primitives}

Before the first distinction can be drawn‚ÄĒthe primordial cut between ``is'' and ``is not''‚ÄĒthe system must be precise about what it means for a type to be interesting enough to cut at all.

A type that contains only one element, where every inhabitant is provably equal to every other, is \textit{contractible}. It has no internal structure. Drawing a distinction within it is meaningless‚ÄĒthere is nothing to separate.

At the other extreme, a type that contains at least two provably \textit{different} inhabitants has a genuine internal boundary. This is the minimal precondition for distinction: you cannot cut what has no parts.

These three predicates‚ÄĒcontractibility, the existence of a distinct pair, and non-triviality‚ÄĒare the vocabulary the system needs before the first distinction can even be \textit{stated}. They are not deep theorems. They are the definitions without which the question ``can this type be cut?'' has no formal meaning.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Contractibility: a single center absorbs all inhabitants.
isContr : Set ‚Üí Set
isContr A = ő£ A (őĽ c ‚Üí (x : A) ‚Üí c ‚Č° x)

-- ¬ß A type has a distinct pair if two inhabitants are provably unequal.
HasDistinctPair : Set ‚Üí Set
HasDistinctPair A = ő£ A (őĽ a ‚Üí ő£ A (őĽ b ‚Üí a ‚Č† b))

-- ¬ß Non-triviality: the type is not contractible.
NonTrivial : Set ‚Üí Set
NonTrivial A = ¬¨ (isContr A)
\end{code}

\section{The Distinction Record}
\label{sec:foundation:distinction-record}

What, then, is a distinction? Not philosophically‚ÄĒformally. If we strip away every detail that is not logically essential, what survives?

A carrier type. Two witnesses in that carrier. A proof that the witnesses are not equal. And a coverage guarantee that every element of the carrier falls into one of these two classes. Nothing less will do: remove any one of these four components and the structure either collapses to triviality or loses the ability to separate. Nothing more is needed: any additional field would be a free parameter‚ÄĒeliminable by the filter we have committed to.

The record that follows is not a chosen definition. It is the minimal data that the word ``distinction'' requires in order to mean anything at all.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß The first distinction: carrier, boundary points, separation, and coverage.
record Distinction : Set1 where
  field
    S     : Set
    ‚Ąď     : S
    r     : S
    ‚Ąď‚Č†r   : ‚Ąď ‚Č† r
    cover : (x : S) ‚Üí (x ‚Č° ‚Ąď) ‚äé (x ‚Č° r)

open Distinction public
\end{code}

\chapter{Non-Vacuity}
\label{ch:non-vacuity}

The record \texttt{Distinction} now exists as a type. But types can be empty. If no inhabitant of this type can be constructed‚ÄĒif no actual distinction exists‚ÄĒthen every law we derive from it holds vacuously. ``For all distinctions $d$, $P(d)$'' is trivially true when there are no distinctions. The entire formal apparatus would prove everything and say nothing.

This is the empty-ontology loophole, and it must be closed before any subsequent reasoning has force.

Under \AgdaFlag{safe}, we cannot simply postulate that a distinction exists. But we can demand something weaker and logically sufficient: that the assumption of \textit{no} distinction leads to contradiction. This is double-negation stability‚ÄĒ$\neg\neg\,\mathit{Distinction}$‚ÄĒthe weakest assertion that still blocks the vacuous path. It does not hand us a concrete distinction. It forbids us from assuming there is none.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Non-vacuity demands that Distinction cannot be refuted.
record NonVacuityLaw : Set1 where
  field
    nonvacuity : ¬¨¬¨ Distinction

open NonVacuityLaw public
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 0.0: Distinction Is Irrefutable}
\label{law:nonvacuity:irrefutable}

The first law is the simplest and the most important. If $\neg\,\mathit{Distinction}$ were admissible‚ÄĒif we could consistently deny the existence of any distinction‚ÄĒthen every law of the form ``for all distinctions $d$\ldots'' would be vacuously true. No elimination would occur. No structure would be forced. The entire investigation would be an elaborate tautology about nothing. Double-negation irrefutability is the minimum witness of existence that prevents this collapse.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 0.0: Distinction is irrefutable.
law0-0-nonvacuity : (nv : NonVacuityLaw) ‚Üí ¬¨¬¨ Distinction
law0-0-nonvacuity = nonvacuity
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 0.1: Non-Vacuity Is Non-Eliminability}
\label{law:nonvacuity:non-eliminability}

The non-vacuity law says exactly the same thing in its sharpest form: distinction cannot be eliminated. The negation of distinction is itself refutable. This is not a constructive existence claim‚ÄĒwe do not hold a distinction in our hands‚ÄĒbut it is enough to guarantee that every subsequent argument has a non-trivial domain.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 0.1: NonVacuityLaw is non-eliminability of Distinction.
law0-1-nonvacuity-is-non-eliminability :
  NonVacuityLaw ‚Üí ¬¨ (¬¨ Distinction)
law0-1-nonvacuity-is-non-eliminability = nonvacuity
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 0.2: Distinction Carrier Is Inhabited}
\label{law:nonvacuity:carrier-inhabited}

Once a distinction exists, its carrier cannot be empty. The boundary witness $\ell$ is already part of the record‚ÄĒit \textit{is} a member of the carrier by construction. This is not a theorem that requires proof; it is an immediate consequence of what ``distinction'' means. An empty carrier would have no witnesses, and a structure with no witnesses is no distinction at all.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 0.2: Distinction carrier is inhabited.
law0-2-inhabited : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí S d
law0-2-inhabited d = ‚Ąď d
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 0.3: Distinction Carrier Has A Distinct Pair}
\label{law:nonvacuity:distinct-pair}

The carrier does not merely contain an element‚ÄĒit contains two elements that are provably not equal. The boundary witnesses $\ell$ and $r$, together with the separation proof $\ell \neq r$, hand us a distinct pair directly from the record. No carrier with fewer than two distinguishable elements can support a distinction.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 0.3: Distinction carrier has a distinct pair.
law0-3-has-distinct-pair : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí HasDistinctPair (S d)
law0-3-has-distinct-pair d = (‚Ąď d , (r d , ‚Ąď‚Č†r d))
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 0.4: Distinction Carrier Is Non-Contractible}
\label{law:nonvacuity:non-contractible}

A contractible type is one where every element is provably equal to a single center point. If the carrier were contractible, then $\ell$ and $r$ would both collapse to that center‚ÄĒand therefore to each other. But $\ell \neq r$. Contradiction. Contractibility is dead on arrival.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 0.4: Distinction carrier is non-contractible.
law0-4-not-contractible : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí NonTrivial (S d)
law0-4-not-contractible d (c , collapse) =
  ‚Ąď‚Č†r d (trans (sym (collapse (‚Ąď d))) (collapse (r d)))
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 0.5: Distinct Pair Forces Non-Contractibility}
\label{law:nonvacuity:distinct-pair-forces-noncontractible}

This is the general principle behind the previous law, freed from the specific context of distinction. Whenever \textit{any} type carries a pair of elements known to be unequal, that type cannot be contractible. The center of contraction would identify the pair, contradicting their separation. The proof is identical in structure: transport through the center, arrive at equality, invoke the separation witness.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 0.5: Distinct pair forces non-contractibility.
law0-5-distinct-pair-forces-nontrivial :
  {A : Set} ‚Üí HasDistinctPair A ‚Üí NonTrivial A
law0-5-distinct-pair-forces-nontrivial (a , (b , a‚Č†b)) (c , collapse) =
  a‚Č†b (trans (sym (collapse a)) (collapse b))
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 0.6: Distinction Is Reconstructible From Its Data}
\label{law:nonvacuity:reconstruction}

If you have a type, two elements of that type, a proof of their inequality, and a coverage function‚ÄĒthen you have a distinction. There is no fifth ingredient, no hidden requirement, no gap between ``having all the data'' and ``having the record.'' The record is exactly its fields, nothing more. This is what makes the formalization honest: the definition conceals no implicit structure.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 0.6: Distinction is reconstructible from its data.
fromDistinctCovered :
  {S‚āÄ : Set} (a b : S‚āÄ) ‚Üí
  a ‚Č† b ‚Üí
  ((x : S‚āÄ) ‚Üí (x ‚Č° a) ‚äé (x ‚Č° b)) ‚Üí
  Distinction
fromDistinctCovered {S‚āÄ} a b a‚Č†b cov = record
  { S = S‚āÄ ; ‚Ąď = a ; r = b ; ‚Ąď‚Č†r = a‚Č†b ; cover = cov }

law0-6-reconstruction :
  {S‚āÄ : Set} (a b : S‚āÄ) ‚Üí
  (a‚Č†b : a ‚Č† b) ‚Üí
  (cov : (x : S‚āÄ) ‚Üí (x ‚Č° a) ‚äé (x ‚Č° b)) ‚Üí
  Distinction
law0-6-reconstruction = fromDistinctCovered
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 0.7: Reconstruction Is Exact}
\label{law:nonvacuity:reconstruction-exact}

The round trip is lossless. Take a distinction, extract its four components, feed them back into the constructor: the result is definitionally equal to the original. Not propositionally equal‚ÄĒnot ``there exists a proof of equality''‚ÄĒbut definitionally equal, meaning the type checker treats them as the same term without any proof at all. The proof is \texttt{refl}. This is the strongest form of exactness a reconstruction can have.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 0.7: Reconstruction is exact by definitional equality.
law0-7-reconstruction-exact :
  (d : Distinction) ‚Üí
  fromDistinctCovered (‚Ąď d) (r d) (‚Ąď‚Č†r d) (cover d) ‚Č° d
law0-7-reconstruction-exact d = refl
\end{code}

The distinction record is now stable under reconstruction: it can be decomposed into its four components and reassembled without loss. This is the sharpest form of structural identity the type system can express. The object is not merely described---it is locked.

\chapter{Elimination and Classification}
\label{ch:elimination-classification}

The distinction record is now established. What can be done with it?

The answer is surprisingly constrained. Two-class coverage‚ÄĒthe guarantee that every element of the carrier equals $\ell$ or $r$‚ÄĒdoes not merely partition the carrier. It forces a radical compression of the entire function space. Any endomorphism $f : S \to S$ is completely determined by two values: $f(\ell)$ and $f(r)$. Each of these values is itself forced into one of two classes. So the total number of endomorphisms is at most $2 \times 2 = 4$. Not approximately. Exactly.

This chapter traces that compression from its source (coverage) to its consequence (the complete four-case classification that constitutes $K_4$).

\section{Two-Class Coverage}
\label{sec:classification:two-class-coverage}

\subsection{Law 1.1: Carrier Coverage Is Two-Class}
\label{law:classification:two-class-coverage}

The coverage field is not a bonus feature of the distinction record‚ÄĒit is the engine that drives everything that follows. Without it, the carrier might contain elements that are neither $\ell$ nor $r$: unclassified witnesses that escape the boundary structure. With it, every element is nailed down. There is nowhere to hide.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 1.1: Carrier coverage is two-class.
law1-1-cover : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí (x : S d) ‚Üí (x ‚Č° ‚Ąď d) ‚äé (x ‚Č° r d)
law1-1-cover = cover
\end{code}

Two-class coverage forces the first structural milestone: every surviving witness lies on one of two boundary classes. Figure~\ref{fig:first-distinction} fixes this collapse as a two-node distinction diagram.

\begin{figure}[h]
\centering
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=Latex, node distance=3.2cm]
  \node[circle, draw=fdBlue, thick, minimum size=10mm, fill=fdLight] (l) {$\ell$};
  \node[circle, draw=fdRed, thick, minimum size=10mm, fill=fdLight, right of=l] (r) {$r$};
  \draw[->, thick] (l) -- node[above] {distinction} (r);
\end{tikzpicture}
\caption{First distinction: two boundary witnesses forced by two-class coverage.}
\label{fig:first-distinction}
\end{figure}

\subsection{Law 1.2: Elimination Is Forced By Coverage}
\label{law:classification:elimination-from-coverage}

Coverage has a powerful consequence: it gives us a canonical eliminator. For any predicate $P$ on the carrier, if we know $P(\ell)$ and $P(r)$, then we know $P(x)$ for every $x$. The proof is mechanical: ask \texttt{cover} which class $x$ belongs to, then substitute the appropriate witness. This is not a convenience‚ÄĒit is the only way to discharge a universal statement over the carrier. The two boundary cases are all there is.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß The canonical eliminator for a distinction carrier.
Distinction-elim :
  (d : Distinction) ‚Üí
  {P : S d ‚Üí Set} ‚Üí
  P (‚Ąď d) ‚Üí
  P (r d) ‚Üí
  (x : S d) ‚Üí
  P x
Distinction-elim d {P} p‚Ąď pr x with cover d x
... | inj‚āĀ x‚Č°‚Ąď = subst P (sym x‚Č°‚Ąď) p‚Ąď
... | inj‚āā x‚Č°r = subst P (sym x‚Č°r) pr
\end{code}

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 1.2: Elimination is forced by coverage.
law1-2-elim :
  (d : Distinction) ‚Üí
  {P : S d ‚Üí Set} ‚Üí
  P (‚Ąď d) ‚Üí
  P (r d) ‚Üí
  (x : S d) ‚Üí
  P x
law1-2-elim = Distinction-elim
\end{code}

\section{Duality}
\label{sec:classification:duality}

Once the carrier has exactly two classes, there is exactly one nontrivial thing you can do to it: swap the two sides. Send $\ell$ to $r$ and $r$ to $\ell$. This is the dual map. It is not one permutation among many‚ÄĒit is the \textit{only} non-identity permutation that a two-element carrier admits.

The critical property is involutivity: applying the swap twice returns to the starting point. This is not merely expected‚ÄĒit is forced. If the double application landed anywhere other than the original, it would produce a third element or violate coverage. Neither is possible. So duality is an involution, and this fact is proved by exhaustive case analysis on both boundary witnesses.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß The dual map: swap boundary cases.
Distinction-dual : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí S d ‚Üí S d
Distinction-dual d x with cover d x
... | inj‚āĀ _ = r d
... | inj‚āā _ = ‚Ąď d

-- ¬ß Duality is involutive by exhaustive case analysis.
Distinction-dual-involutive :
  (d : Distinction) ‚Üí
  (x : S d) ‚Üí
  Distinction-dual d (Distinction-dual d x) ‚Č° x
Distinction-dual-involutive d =
  Distinction-elim d proof-‚Ąď proof-r
  where
    proof-‚Ąď : Distinction-dual d (Distinction-dual d (‚Ąď d)) ‚Č° ‚Ąď d
    proof-‚Ąď with cover d (‚Ąď d)
    ... | inj‚āā ‚Ąď‚Č°r = ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) ‚Ąď‚Č°r)
    ... | inj‚āĀ _ with cover d (r d)
    ... | inj‚āĀ r‚Č°‚Ąď = ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) (sym r‚Č°‚Ąď))
    ... | inj‚āā _   = refl

    proof-r : Distinction-dual d (Distinction-dual d (r d)) ‚Č° r d
    proof-r with cover d (r d)
    ... | inj‚āĀ r‚Č°‚Ąď = ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) (sym r‚Č°‚Ąď))
    ... | inj‚āā _ with cover d (‚Ąď d)
    ... | inj‚āā ‚Ąď‚Č°r = ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) ‚Ąď‚Č°r)
    ... | inj‚āĀ _   = refl
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 1.3: Duality Is An Involution}
\label{law:classification:duality-involution}

If the dual map were not involutive, it would generate an orbit of length three or more‚ÄĒbut the carrier has only two elements. There is no room. The double application must return to the original, and that is exactly what the type checker confirms.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 1.3: Duality is an involution.
law1-3-dual-involutive :
  (d : Distinction) ‚Üí
  (x : S d) ‚Üí
  Distinction-dual d (Distinction-dual d x) ‚Č° x
law1-3-dual-involutive = Distinction-dual-involutive
\end{code}

\section{Pointwise Equality}
\label{sec:classification:pointwise-equality}

When are two endomorphisms the same? The answer is forced by the elimination structure we already have. Since every carrier element is determined by its boundary class, and every endomorphism is determined by what it does to the boundary, two endomorphisms are equal precisely when they agree on every input. This pointwise equality is the only comparison that respects the boundary structure. Intensional distinctions‚ÄĒcases where two maps compute differently but produce identical results‚ÄĒare irrelevant. What matters is the output at every point, nothing else.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Pointwise equality of functions.
infix 4 _‚Čó_
_‚Čó_ : {‚Ąď‚āĀ ‚Ąď‚āā : Level} {A : Set ‚Ąď‚āĀ} {B : Set ‚Ąď‚āā} ‚Üí (A ‚Üí B) ‚Üí (A ‚Üí B) ‚Üí Set (‚Ąď‚āĀ ‚äĒ ‚Ąď‚āā)
_‚Čó_ {A = A} f g = (x : A) ‚Üí f x ‚Č° g x

-- ¬ß The identity function.
id : {A : Set} ‚Üí A ‚Üí A
id x = x
\end{code}

\section{Four-Case Endomorphism Classification}
\label{sec:classification:four-case-endomorphism-classification}

Now the compression becomes visible. An endomorphism $f : S \to S$ sends each boundary witness to some element of the carrier. But every element of the carrier is either $\ell$ or $r$. So $f(\ell)$ is either $\ell$ or $r$, and $f(r)$ is either $\ell$ or $r$. That gives exactly four possibilities:

\begin{itemize}
\item $f(\ell) = \ell$ and $f(r) = \ell$: the constant-left map.
\item $f(\ell) = r$ and $f(r) = r$: the constant-right map.
\item $f(\ell) = \ell$ and $f(r) = r$: the identity.
\item $f(\ell) = r$ and $f(r) = \ell$: the dual.
\end{itemize}

There is no fifth case. The four labels below name these survivors.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß The four-case classification of endomorphisms.
data EndoCase : Set where
  case-constL : EndoCase
  case-constR : EndoCase
  case-id     : EndoCase
  case-dual   : EndoCase
\end{code}

Figure~\ref{fig:k3-failure} makes the pressure visible in the negative direction. A three-vertex closure cannot survive this classification, because one of the four mutually distinct cases is always left outside. The triangle is therefore not a weaker rival to $K_4$; it is the first unstable alternative that the four-case verdict destroys.

\begin{figure}[h]
\centering
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=Latex, every node/.style={circle, draw=fdBlue, thick, minimum size=9mm, fill=fdLight}]
  \node (a) at (90:1.8) {$c_L$};
  \node (b) at (210:1.8) {$c_R$};
  \node (c) at (330:1.8) {$id$};
  \node[draw=fdRed, text=fdRed, right=2.8cm of c] (d) {$dual$};
  \draw[thick] (a) -- (b) -- (c) -- cycle;
  \draw[dashed, thick, fdRed, ->] (d) -- ($(b)!0.5!(c)$);
  \node[rectangle, rounded corners=1mm, draw=fdRed, text=fdRed, fill=white, below=0.2cm of d] {no room in $K_3$};
\end{tikzpicture}
\caption{Why $K_3$ fails: four mutually distinct endomorphism cases cannot close on a three-vertex graph. One case is always forced outside.}
\label{fig:k3-failure}
\end{figure}

\section{The \texorpdfstring{$K_4$}{K4} Classification Module}
\label{sec:classification:k4-module}

The four cases are not yet a classification‚ÄĒthey are just labels. A proper classification requires three things: an \textit{interpretation} that turns each label into an actual endomorphism, a \textit{classifier} that assigns a label to any given endomorphism, and proofs that these two operations are inverses of each other. Soundness (classify then interpret recovers the original) and injectivity (distinct labels produce distinct behavior) together force uniqueness: every endomorphism receives exactly one label, and no two labels describe the same map.

The module \texttt{K‚āĄ} assembles all of this in one place. It is parameterized by a distinction $d$, because the classification depends on the specific boundary witnesses.

\paragraph{Step 1: Endo type and pointwise equivalence.}
The endomorphism type and its equivalence relation are forced by the carrier.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß K‚āĄ endomorphism algebra: classify, interpret, and verify.
module K‚āĄ (d : Distinction) where
  Endo : Set
  Endo = S d ‚Üí S d

  ‚Čó-refl : {f : Endo} ‚Üí f ‚Čó f
  ‚Čó-refl x = refl

  ‚Čó-sym : {f g : Endo} ‚Üí f ‚Čó g ‚Üí g ‚Čó f
  ‚Čó-sym p x = sym (p x)

  ‚Čó-trans : {f g h : Endo} ‚Üí f ‚Čó g ‚Üí g ‚Čó h ‚Üí f ‚Čó h
  ‚Čó-trans p q x = trans (p x) (q x)
\end{code}

\paragraph{Step 2: The four canonical endomorphisms.}
Two-class coverage forces exactly four surviving endomorphisms: constant-left, constant-right, identity, and dual.
The dual exchanges the two boundary witnesses; its boundary behavior is verified by case analysis against \texttt{cover}.

\begin{code}
  -- ¬ß Constant-left endomorphism.
  constL : Endo
  constL _ = ‚Ąď d

  -- ¬ß Constant-right endomorphism.
  constR : Endo
  constR _ = r d

  -- ¬ß The dual endomorphism.
  dual : Endo
  dual = Distinction-dual d

  -- ¬ß Dual sends ‚Ąď to r.
  dual-‚Ąď : dual (‚Ąď d) ‚Č° r d
  dual-‚Ąď with cover d (‚Ąď d)
  ... | inj‚āĀ _   = refl
  ... | inj‚āā ‚Ąď‚Č°r = ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) ‚Ąď‚Č°r)

  -- ¬ß Dual sends r to ‚Ąď.
  dual-r : dual (r d) ‚Č° ‚Ąď d
  dual-r with cover d (r d)
  ... | inj‚āĀ r‚Č°‚Ąď = ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) (sym r‚Č°‚Ąď))
  ... | inj‚āā _   = refl
\end{code}

\paragraph{Step 3: Interpretation and classification.}
Each case label is interpreted as one of the four canonical endomorphisms.
Conversely, every endofunction is classified by its boundary outputs into one of the four cases.

\begin{code}
  -- ¬ß Interpret a case label as an endofunction.
  interpret : EndoCase ‚Üí Endo
  interpret case-constL = constL
  interpret case-constR = constR
  interpret case-id     = id
  interpret case-dual   = dual

  -- ¬ß Classify an endofunction by its boundary outputs.
  classify : Endo ‚Üí EndoCase
  classify f with cover d (f (‚Ąď d)) | cover d (f (r d))
  ... | inj‚āĀ _ | inj‚āĀ _ = case-constL
  ... | inj‚āā _ | inj‚āā _ = case-constR
  ... | inj‚āĀ _ | inj‚āā _ = case-id
  ... | inj‚āā _ | inj‚āĀ _ = case-dual
\end{code}

\paragraph{Step 4: Soundness of classification.}
Classification followed by interpretation recovers the original endofunction at both boundary witnesses.
The pointwise proof is assembled from the two boundary cases via the carrier eliminator.

\begin{code}
  -- ¬ß Soundness at ‚Ąď: interpretation recovers the value at ‚Ąď.
  sound-at-‚Ąď : (f : Endo) ‚Üí interpret (classify f) (‚Ąď d) ‚Č° f (‚Ąď d)
  sound-at-‚Ąď f with cover d (f (‚Ąď d)) | cover d (f (r d))
  ... | inj‚āĀ fl‚Č°‚Ąď | inj‚āĀ _     = sym fl‚Č°‚Ąď
  ... | inj‚āā fl‚Č°r | inj‚āā _     = sym fl‚Č°r
  ... | inj‚āĀ fl‚Č°‚Ąď | inj‚āā _     = sym fl‚Č°‚Ąď
  ... | inj‚āā fl‚Č°r | inj‚āĀ _     = trans dual-‚Ąď (sym fl‚Č°r)

  -- ¬ß Soundness at r: interpretation recovers the value at r.
  sound-at-r : (f : Endo) ‚Üí interpret (classify f) (r d) ‚Č° f (r d)
  sound-at-r f with cover d (f (‚Ąď d)) | cover d (f (r d))
  ... | inj‚āĀ _     | inj‚āĀ fr‚Č°‚Ąď = sym fr‚Č°‚Ąď
  ... | inj‚āā _     | inj‚āā fr‚Č°r = sym fr‚Č°r
  ... | inj‚āĀ _     | inj‚āā fr‚Č°r = sym fr‚Č°r
  ... | inj‚āā _     | inj‚āĀ fr‚Č°‚Ąď = trans dual-r (sym fr‚Č°‚Ąď)

  -- ¬ß Soundness: classification followed by interpretation recovers behavior.
  classify-sound : (f : Endo) ‚Üí interpret (classify f) ‚Čó f
  classify-sound f x = Distinction-elim d (sound-at-‚Ąď f) (sound-at-r f) x
\end{code}

\paragraph{Step 5: Boundary determination and injectivity.}
Endofunctions are determined by their boundary values alone (via the eliminator).
Interpretation is injective: distinct case labels force distinct boundary behavior, so the $4 \times 4$ case matrix either produces \texttt{refl} or derives absurdity from $\ell \neq r$.

\begin{code}
  -- ¬ß Endofunctions are determined by their boundary values.
  endo-determined :
    (f g : Endo) ‚Üí
    f (‚Ąď d) ‚Č° g (‚Ąď d) ‚Üí
    f (r d) ‚Č° g (r d) ‚Üí
    f ‚Čó g
  endo-determined f g eq‚Ąď eqr x = Distinction-elim d eq‚Ąď eqr x

  -- ¬ß Interpretation is injective: distinct cases produce distinct behavior.
  interpret-injective :
    (c c' : EndoCase) ‚Üí
    interpret c ‚Čó interpret c' ‚Üí
    c ‚Č° c'
  interpret-injective case-constL case-constL _ = refl
  interpret-injective case-constL case-constR p = ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) (p (‚Ąď d)))
  interpret-injective case-constL case-id     p = ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) (p (r d)))
  interpret-injective case-constL case-dual   p =
    ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) (trans (p (‚Ąď d)) dual-‚Ąď))

  interpret-injective case-constR case-constL p =
    ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) (sym (p (‚Ąď d))))
  interpret-injective case-constR case-constR _ = refl
  interpret-injective case-constR case-id     p =
    ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) (sym (p (‚Ąď d))))
  interpret-injective case-constR case-dual   p =
    ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) (sym (trans (p (r d)) dual-r)))

  interpret-injective case-id     case-constL p =
    ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) (sym (p (r d))))
  interpret-injective case-id     case-constR p = ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) (p (‚Ąď d)))
  interpret-injective case-id     case-id     _ = refl
  interpret-injective case-id     case-dual   p =
    ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) (trans (p (‚Ąď d)) dual-‚Ąď))

  interpret-injective case-dual   case-constL p =
    ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) (sym (trans (sym (dual-‚Ąď)) (p (‚Ąď d)))))
  interpret-injective case-dual   case-constR p =
    ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) (trans (sym (dual-r)) (p (r d))))
  interpret-injective case-dual   case-id     p =
    ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) (sym (trans (sym (dual-‚Ąď)) (p (‚Ąď d)))))
  interpret-injective case-dual   case-dual   _ = refl
\end{code}

\paragraph{Step 6: Classification uniqueness.}
Soundness and injectivity together force that the case label assigned by \texttt{classify} is unique.

\begin{code}
  -- ¬ß Classification is unique: the label is forced by soundness + injectivity.
  classify-unique : (f : Endo) ‚Üí (c : EndoCase) ‚Üí interpret c ‚Čó f ‚Üí c ‚Č° classify f
  classify-unique f c c‚Čóf =
    interpret-injective c (classify f) (‚Čó-trans c‚Čóf (‚Čó-sym (classify-sound f)))
\end{code}

\section{Mutual Distinctness of Cases}
\label{sec:classification:mutual-distinctness}

The four case labels must remain genuinely different‚ÄĒno two of them may be identified. This is not something that requires deep reasoning. The labels are constructors of a data type, and Agda's pattern matching confirms that no constructor equals any other. The six pairwise inequalities are all proved by the absurd pattern \texttt{()}: there is simply no way to build a proof that, say, \texttt{case-constL} equals \texttt{case-id}. The type is empty.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß All four EndoCase constructors are pairwise distinct.
EndoCase-distinct :
  (case-constL ‚Č° case-constR ‚Üí ‚ä•) √ó
  (case-constL ‚Č° case-id     ‚Üí ‚ä•) √ó
  (case-constL ‚Č° case-dual   ‚Üí ‚ä•) √ó
  (case-constR ‚Č° case-id     ‚Üí ‚ä•) √ó
  (case-constR ‚Č° case-dual   ‚Üí ‚ä•) √ó
  (case-id     ‚Č° case-dual   ‚Üí ‚ä•)
EndoCase-distinct =
  (őĽ ()) ,
  ((őĽ ()) ,
   ((őĽ ()) ,
    ((őĽ ()) ,
     ((őĽ ()) ,
      (őĽ ())))))
\end{code}

\section{Top-Level Classification Witnesses}
\label{sec:classification:top-level-witnesses}

The local module laws force exported witnesses of soundness and uniqueness.
These witnesses are thin wrappers‚ÄĒthey add no new content, only accessibility.
The classification lives inside the parameterized module \texttt{K‚āĄ}; to use it in later chapters, we export soundness and uniqueness at the top level.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Top-level K‚āĄ soundness witness.
k4-classification-sound :
  (d : Distinction) ‚Üí
  (f : S d ‚Üí S d) ‚Üí
  ő£ EndoCase (őĽ c ‚Üí K‚āĄ.interpret d c ‚Čó f)
k4-classification-sound d f = K‚āĄ.classify d f , K‚āĄ.classify-sound d f

-- ¬ß Top-level K‚āĄ uniqueness witness.
k4-classification-unique :
  (d : Distinction) ‚Üí
  (f : S d ‚Üí S d) ‚Üí
  (c‚āĀ c‚āā : EndoCase) ‚Üí
  K‚āĄ.interpret d c‚āĀ ‚Čó f ‚Üí
  K‚āĄ.interpret d c‚āā ‚Čó f ‚Üí
  c‚āĀ ‚Č° c‚āā
k4-classification-unique d f c‚āĀ c‚āā p‚āĀ p‚āā =
  K‚āĄ.interpret-injective d c‚āĀ c‚āā (K‚āĄ.‚Čó-trans d p‚āĀ (K‚āĄ.‚Čó-sym d p‚āā))
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 1.4: Endo(S) Classifies Into Exactly Four Cases}
\label{law:k4:endo-four-case-classification}

Every endomorphism on a distinction carrier receives a label from the four-case type. The classifier inspects the two boundary outputs and assigns the unique matching case. No endomorphism escapes classification.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 1.4: Endo(S) classifies into exactly four cases.
law1-4-classify : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí (S d ‚Üí S d) ‚Üí EndoCase
law1-4-classify d = K‚āĄ.classify d
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 1.5: Classification Is Sound}
\label{law:k4:classification-sound}

Soundness means that the round trip works: classify an endomorphism, interpret the resulting label, and you get back a function that agrees with the original at every point. This is not a design goal‚ÄĒit is forced by the fact that classification reads off the boundary values and interpretation plugs them back in.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 1.5: Classification is sound.
law1-5-classify-sound : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí (f : S d ‚Üí S d) ‚Üí K‚āĄ.interpret d (K‚āĄ.classify d f) ‚Čó f
law1-5-classify-sound d f = snd (k4-classification-sound d f)
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 1.6: Endo Is Determined By Boundary Values}
\label{law:k4:boundary-determination}

If two endomorphisms agree on $\ell$ and agree on $r$, they agree everywhere. This is a direct consequence of coverage: every carrier element is either $\ell$ or $r$, so agreement on those two points leaves no room for disagreement anywhere else. The interior of the carrier is empty‚ÄĒthere are no hidden witnesses where the two maps could diverge.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 1.6: Endo is determined by boundary values.
law1-6-endo-determined :
  (d : Distinction) ‚Üí
  (f g : S d ‚Üí S d) ‚Üí
  f (‚Ąď d) ‚Č° g (‚Ąď d) ‚Üí
  f (r d) ‚Č° g (r d) ‚Üí
  f ‚Čó g
law1-6-endo-determined d = K‚āĄ.endo-determined d
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 1.7: Classification Is Unique}
\label{law:k4:classification-unique}

If a case label $c$ interprets to a function that agrees pointwise with $f$, then $c$ must be exactly the label that the classifier assigns to $f$. The proof chains soundness (the classifier's label also interprets correctly) with injectivity (two labels interpreting to the same function must be equal). There is no ambiguity, no alternative labeling. The classification is canonical.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 1.7: Classification is unique.
law1-7-classify-unique :
  (d : Distinction) ‚Üí
  (f : S d ‚Üí S d) ‚Üí
  (c : EndoCase) ‚Üí
  K‚āĄ.interpret d c ‚Čó f ‚Üí
  c ‚Č° K‚āĄ.classify d f
law1-7-classify-unique d f c p =
  k4-classification-unique d f c (fst (k4-classification-sound d f)) p (snd (k4-classification-sound d f))
\end{code}

Exhaustive classification reaches the second structural milestone here. Figure~\ref{fig:k4-classification} fixes the unique four-case closure as the complete graph $K_4$.

\begin{figure}[h]
\centering
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=Latex, every node/.style={circle, draw=fdBlue, thick, minimum size=9mm, fill=fdLight}]
  \node (a) at (90:2.2) {$c_L$};
  \node (b) at (0:2.2) {$c_R$};
  \node (c) at (270:2.2) {$id$};
  \node (d) at (180:2.2) {$dual$};
  \draw[thick] (a) -- (b);
  \draw[thick] (a) -- (c);
  \draw[thick] (a) -- (d);
  \draw[thick] (b) -- (c);
  \draw[thick] (b) -- (d);
  \draw[thick] (c) -- (d);
\end{tikzpicture}
\caption{Exhaustive four-case endomorphism classification closes as the complete graph $K_4$.}
\label{fig:k4-classification}
\end{figure}

\chapter{Two Normal Form}
\label{ch:two-normal-form}

The classification is complete: every endomorphism falls into one of four cases. But the carrier itself‚ÄĒthe type $S$ in the distinction record‚ÄĒis still abstract. We know it has exactly two elements, because coverage says so. But ``having exactly two elements'' and ``being the canonical two-element type'' are different statements. This chapter closes the gap.

The key insight is that coverage forces a canonical isomorphism between any distinction carrier and a concrete two-element type \texttt{Two}. The maps in both directions are uniquely determined by the boundary witnesses, the round trips are identity, and the only remaining freedom is a binary choice: do we send $\ell$ to $L$ or to $R$? That choice is the orientation, and there are exactly two of them. The same compression applies to automorphisms: the only self-equivalences of a distinction are the identity and the dual.

\section{The Two Type}
\label{sec:two-normal-form:two-type}

The canonical two-element type has two constructors‚ÄĒ\texttt{L} and \texttt{R}‚ÄĒthat are provably distinct and exhaustively cover the type. This is the simplest possible distinction. It serves as the solved form: the concrete representative to which every abstract distinction carrier is isomorphic.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß The canonical two-element type.
data Two : Set where
  L : Two
  R : Two

-- ¬ß L and R are distinct.
Two-L‚Č†R : L ‚Č† R
Two-L‚Č†R ()

-- ¬ß Exhaustive two-class coverage for Two.
Two-cover : (x : Two) ‚Üí (x ‚Č° L) ‚äé (x ‚Č° R)
Two-cover L = inj‚āĀ refl
Two-cover R = inj‚āā refl

-- ¬ß Two forms a canonical Distinction.
Two-distinction : Distinction
Two-distinction = record
  { S     = Two
  ; ‚Ąď     = L
  ; r     = R
  ; ‚Ąď‚Č†r   = Two-L‚Č†R
  ; cover = Two-cover
  }
\end{code}

\section{Distinction Isomorphism}
\label{sec:two-normal-form:distinction-isomorphism}

Comparing two distinctions requires a notion of structural equivalence. A \textit{distinction isomorphism} is a pair of maps between the carriers that are mutual inverses and that preserve the boundary witnesses: $\ell$ maps to $\ell$ and $r$ maps to $r$. A weaker notion‚ÄĒ\textit{distinction equivalence}‚ÄĒdrops the boundary requirement and retains only the inverse laws. The weaker notion is what orientation and automorphism classification will use, since those questions ask precisely whether the boundary is preserved or swapped.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Boundary-preserving isomorphism between distinctions.
record DistinctionIso (d‚āĀ d‚āā : Distinction) : Set1 where
  field
    to      : S d‚āĀ ‚Üí S d‚āā
    from    : S d‚āā ‚Üí S d‚āĀ
    to-from : (y : S d‚āā) ‚Üí to (from y) ‚Č° y
    from-to : (x : S d‚āĀ) ‚Üí from (to x) ‚Č° x
    to-‚Ąď    : to (‚Ąď d‚āĀ) ‚Č° ‚Ąď d‚āā
    to-r    : to (r d‚āĀ) ‚Č° r d‚āā

open DistinctionIso public

-- ¬ß Equivalence without boundary constraints.
record DistinctionEquiv (d‚āĀ d‚āā : Distinction) : Set1 where
  field
    to      : S d‚āĀ ‚Üí S d‚āā
    from    : S d‚āā ‚Üí S d‚āĀ
    to-from : (y : S d‚āā) ‚Üí to (from y) ‚Č° y
    from-to : (x : S d‚āĀ) ‚Üí from (to x) ‚Č° x

open DistinctionEquiv public

-- ¬ß Forgetting boundary data from an isomorphism.
forgetIso : {d‚āĀ d‚āā : Distinction} ‚Üí DistinctionIso d‚āĀ d‚āā ‚Üí DistinctionEquiv d‚āĀ d‚āā
forgetIso i = record
  { to      = DistinctionIso.to i
  ; from    = DistinctionIso.from i
  ; to-from = DistinctionIso.to-from i
  ; from-to = DistinctionIso.from-to i
  }
\end{code}

\section{Canonical Two Maps}
\label{sec:two-normal-form:canonical-two-maps}

The isomorphism between any distinction carrier and \texttt{Two} writes itself. The forward map \texttt{toTwo} sends $\ell$ to $L$ and $r$ to $R$‚ÄĒdetermined by coverage. The backward map \texttt{fromTwo} reverses this. The round-trip proofs are forced by the eliminator: there is exactly one way to verify that the composition is the identity, and the type checker accepts it.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß The canonical boundary-preserving map to Two.
toTwo : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí S d ‚Üí Two
toTwo d x with cover d x
... | inj‚āĀ _ = L
... | inj‚āā _ = R

-- ¬ß The canonical embedding from Two.
fromTwo : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí Two ‚Üí S d
fromTwo d L = ‚Ąď d
fromTwo d R = r d

-- ¬ß toTwo sends ‚Ąď to L.
toTwo-‚Ąď : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí toTwo d (‚Ąď d) ‚Č° L
toTwo-‚Ąď d with cover d (‚Ąď d)
... | inj‚āĀ _   = refl
... | inj‚āā ‚Ąď‚Č°r = ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) ‚Ąď‚Č°r)

-- ¬ß toTwo sends r to R.
toTwo-r : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí toTwo d (r d) ‚Č° R
toTwo-r d with cover d (r d)
... | inj‚āā _   = refl
... | inj‚āĀ r‚Č°‚Ąď = ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) (sym r‚Č°‚Ąď))

-- ¬ß fromTwo ‚ąė toTwo is the identity.
fromTwo-toTwo : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí (x : S d) ‚Üí fromTwo d (toTwo d x) ‚Č° x
fromTwo-toTwo d =
  Distinction-elim d
    (trans (cong (fromTwo d) (toTwo-‚Ąď d)) refl)
    (trans (cong (fromTwo d) (toTwo-r d)) refl)

-- ¬ß toTwo ‚ąė fromTwo is the identity.
toTwo-fromTwo : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí (t : Two) ‚Üí toTwo d (fromTwo d t) ‚Č° t
toTwo-fromTwo d L = toTwo-‚Ąď d
toTwo-fromTwo d R = toTwo-r d
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 1.8: Every Distinction Is Isomorphic To Two}
\label{law:two-normal-form:isomorphic-to-two}

This is the normal form theorem. Every distinction‚ÄĒregardless of how its carrier was originally defined‚ÄĒis boundary-preserving isomorphic to the canonical \texttt{Two} distinction. The proof assembles the maps and round-trip verifications from the previous section into a single record. There is nothing to choose; every field is forced.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 1.8: Every distinction is isomorphic to Two.
two-normal-form : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí DistinctionIso d Two-distinction
two-normal-form d = record
  { to      = toTwo d
  ; from    = fromTwo d
  ; to-from = toTwo-fromTwo d
  ; from-to = fromTwo-toTwo d
  ; to-‚Ąď    = toTwo-‚Ąď d
  ; to-r    = toTwo-r d
  }

law1-8-two-normal-form : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí DistinctionIso d Two-distinction
law1-8-two-normal-form = two-normal-form
\end{code}

\section{Orientation}
\label{sec:two-normal-form:orientation}

An equivalence between a distinction and \texttt{Two} must send the two boundary witnesses to distinct images‚ÄĒotherwise the inverse map would identify $\ell$ and $r$, contradicting their separation. The images are elements of \texttt{Two}, which has only two inhabitants. So there are exactly two possibilities: send $\ell$ to $L$ (preserve) or send $\ell$ to $R$ (swap). No third option exists.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß The swap map on Two.
swapTwo : Two ‚Üí Two
swapTwo L = R
swapTwo R = L

-- ¬ß Swap is involutive.
swapTwo-involutive : (t : Two) ‚Üí swapTwo (swapTwo t) ‚Č° t
swapTwo-involutive L = refl
swapTwo-involutive R = refl

-- ¬ß The swap-oriented map to Two.
toTwo-swap : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí S d ‚Üí Two
toTwo-swap d x = swapTwo (toTwo d x)

-- ¬ß Swap-oriented map sends ‚Ąď to R.
toTwo-swap-‚Ąď : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí toTwo-swap d (‚Ąď d) ‚Č° R
toTwo-swap-‚Ąď d = cong swapTwo (toTwo-‚Ąď d)

-- ¬ß Swap-oriented map sends r to L.
toTwo-swap-r : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí toTwo-swap d (r d) ‚Č° L
toTwo-swap-r d = cong swapTwo (toTwo-r d)

-- ¬ß The canonical equivalence (boundary-preserving).
two-normal-form-equiv : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí DistinctionEquiv d Two-distinction
two-normal-form-equiv d = forgetIso (two-normal-form d)

-- ¬ß The swap equivalence.
two-normal-form-equiv-swap : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí DistinctionEquiv d Two-distinction
two-normal-form-equiv-swap d = record
  { to      = toTwo-swap d
  ; from    = fromTwo d ‚ąė swapTwo
  ; to-from = őĽ t ‚Üí trans (cong (toTwo-swap d) (refl)) (trans (cong swapTwo (toTwo-fromTwo d (swapTwo t))) (swapTwo-involutive t))
  ; from-to = őĽ x ‚Üí trans (cong (fromTwo d) (swapTwo-involutive (toTwo d x))) (fromTwo-toTwo d x)
  }
  where
    _‚ąė_ : {A B C : Set} ‚Üí (B ‚Üí C) ‚Üí (A ‚Üí B) ‚Üí A ‚Üí C
    (f ‚ąė g) x = f (g x)

-- ¬ß The two orientations of a Two normal form.
data TwoOrientation : Set where
  orient-preserve : TwoOrientation
  orient-swap     : TwoOrientation

-- ¬ß The two orientations are distinct.
preserve‚Č†swap : orient-preserve ‚Č° orient-swap ‚Üí ‚ä•
preserve‚Č†swap ()

swap‚Č†preserve : orient-swap ‚Č° orient-preserve ‚Üí ‚ä•
swap‚Č†preserve ()
\end{code}

Boundary images of an equivalence are forced to remain distinct by the inverse law.
The orientation classifier is the unique surviving witness that selects between preserve and swap.
This eliminates every ambiguous orientation assignment.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Equivalence images at boundary are distinct.
to-distinct-on-boundary :
  (d : Distinction) ‚Üí
  (e : DistinctionEquiv d Two-distinction) ‚Üí
  to e (‚Ąď d) ‚Č° to e (r d) ‚Üí ‚ä•
to-distinct-on-boundary d e eq =
  ‚Ąď‚Č†r d (trans (sym (from-to e (‚Ąď d))) (trans (cong (from e) eq) (from-to e (r d))))

-- ¬ß Classify an equivalence by its orientation.
orientation-classify :
  (d : Distinction) ‚Üí
  (e : DistinctionEquiv d Two-distinction) ‚Üí
  TwoOrientation
orientation-classify d e with Two-cover (to e (‚Ąď d)) | Two-cover (to e (r d))
... | inj‚āĀ t‚Ąď‚Č°L | inj‚āĀ tr‚Č°L = ‚ä•-elim (to-distinct-on-boundary d e (trans t‚Ąď‚Č°L (sym tr‚Č°L)))
... | inj‚āā t‚Ąď‚Č°R | inj‚āā tr‚Č°R = ‚ä•-elim (to-distinct-on-boundary d e (trans t‚Ąď‚Č°R (sym tr‚Č°R)))
... | inj‚āĀ _ | inj‚āā _ = orient-preserve
... | inj‚āā _ | inj‚āĀ _ = orient-swap
\end{code}

\section{Automorphisms}
\label{sec:two-normal-form:automorphisms}

An automorphism of a distinction is a self-equivalence: a pair of maps from the carrier to itself that are mutual inverses. The inverse law forces injectivity, and injectivity forces the boundary images to remain distinct (since $\ell \neq r$ would be violated otherwise). With only two elements in the carrier, the only injective endomorphisms are the identity and the swap. That is the complete automorphism group of any distinction: exactly two elements, \texttt{id} and \texttt{dual}.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Automorphism of a distinction.
Aut : Distinction ‚Üí Set1
Aut d = DistinctionEquiv d d

-- ¬ß Automorphism transport is injective.
to-injective : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí (a : Aut d) ‚Üí {x y : S d} ‚Üí to a x ‚Č° to a y ‚Üí x ‚Č° y
to-injective d a {x} {y} eq =
  trans (sym (from-to a x)) (trans (cong (from a) eq) (from-to a y))

-- ¬ß Automorphism images at boundary points are distinct.
to-‚Ąď‚Č†to-r : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí (a : Aut d) ‚Üí to a (‚Ąď d) ‚Č† to a (r d)
to-‚Ąď‚Č†to-r d a eq = ‚Ąď‚Č†r d (to-injective d a eq)

-- ¬ß Dual boundary lemma: dual sends ‚Ąď to r.
Distinction-dual-‚Ąď : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí Distinction-dual d (‚Ąď d) ‚Č° r d
Distinction-dual-‚Ąď d with cover d (‚Ąď d)
... | inj‚āĀ _      = refl
... | inj‚āā ‚Ąď‚Č°r    = ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) ‚Ąď‚Č°r)

-- ¬ß Dual boundary lemma: dual sends r to ‚Ąď.
Distinction-dual-r : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí Distinction-dual d (r d) ‚Č° ‚Ąď d
Distinction-dual-r d with cover d (r d)
... | inj‚āā _      = refl
... | inj‚āĀ r‚Č°‚Ąď    = ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) (sym r‚Č°‚Ąď))

-- ¬ß Automorphism classification: exactly id or dual.
Aut-sound :
  (d : Distinction) ‚Üí
  (a : Aut d) ‚Üí
  (to a ‚Čó id) ‚äé (to a ‚Čó Distinction-dual d)
Aut-sound d a with cover d (to a (‚Ąď d)) | cover d (to a (r d))
... | inj‚āĀ t‚Ąď‚Č°‚Ąď | inj‚āĀ tr‚Č°‚Ąď = ‚ä•-elim (to-‚Ąď‚Č†to-r d a (trans t‚Ąď‚Č°‚Ąď (sym tr‚Č°‚Ąď)))
... | inj‚āā t‚Ąď‚Č°r | inj‚āā tr‚Č°r = ‚ä•-elim (to-‚Ąď‚Č†to-r d a (trans t‚Ąď‚Č°r (sym tr‚Č°r)))
... | inj‚āĀ t‚Ąď‚Č°‚Ąď | inj‚āā tr‚Č°r =
  inj‚āĀ (Distinction-elim d t‚Ąď‚Č°‚Ąď tr‚Č°r)
... | inj‚āā t‚Ąď‚Č°r | inj‚āĀ tr‚Č°‚Ąď =
  inj‚āā (Distinction-elim d
    (trans t‚Ąď‚Č°r (sym (Distinction-dual-‚Ąď d)))
    (trans tr‚Č°‚Ąď (sym (Distinction-dual-r d))))
\end{code}

Automorphism classification reaches the third structural milestone here. Figure~\ref{fig:k4-orbits} fixes the symmetry action as a permutation of the four case witnesses.

\begin{figure}[h]
\centering
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=Latex, every node/.style={circle, draw=fdBlue, thick, minimum size=9mm, fill=fdLight}]
  \node (a) at (90:2.2) {$c_L$};
  \node (b) at (0:2.2) {$c_R$};
  \node (c) at (270:2.2) {$id$};
  \node (d) at (180:2.2) {$dual$};
  \draw[thick] (a) -- (b) -- (c) -- (d) -- (a);
  \draw[dashed, thick] (a) -- (c);
  \draw[dashed, thick] (b) -- (d);
  \draw[->, very thick, fdRed] ($(a)+(0.0,0.35)$) arc[start angle=90,end angle=0,radius=2.2];
  \draw[->, very thick, fdRed] ($(c)+(0.0,-0.35)$) arc[start angle=270,end angle=180,radius=2.2];
\end{tikzpicture}
\caption{Automorphism action on the four-case witness space: preserve and swap survive as the only orbit structure.}
\label{fig:k4-orbits}
\end{figure}

\subsection{Law 1.9: The Two Isomorphism Is Unique}
\label{law:two-normal-form:isomorphism-unique}

Any boundary-preserving map from a distinction carrier to \texttt{Two} must agree with the canonical map \texttt{toTwo}. The proof is immediate from the eliminator: since the map is determined by its values at $\ell$ and $r$, and those values are fixed by the boundary-preservation requirement, there is exactly one such map. The same holds for the backward direction.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Uniqueness of the to-direction.
toTwo-unique :
  (d : Distinction) ‚Üí
  (f : S d ‚Üí Two) ‚Üí
  f (‚Ąď d) ‚Č° L ‚Üí
  f (r d) ‚Č° R ‚Üí
  f ‚Čó toTwo d
toTwo-unique d f f‚Ąď fr =
  Distinction-elim d
    (trans f‚Ąď (sym (toTwo-‚Ąď d)))
    (trans fr (sym (toTwo-r d)))

-- ¬ß Uniqueness of the swap-direction.
toTwo-swap-unique :
  (d : Distinction) ‚Üí
  (f : S d ‚Üí Two) ‚Üí
  f (‚Ąď d) ‚Č° R ‚Üí
  f (r d) ‚Č° L ‚Üí
  f ‚Čó toTwo-swap d
toTwo-swap-unique d f f‚Ąď fr =
  Distinction-elim d
    (trans f‚Ąď (sym (toTwo-swap-‚Ąď d)))
    (trans fr (sym (toTwo-swap-r d)))

-- ¬ß Law 1.9: iso-to is unique.
law1-9-iso-to-unique : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí (i : DistinctionIso d Two-distinction) ‚Üí to i ‚Čó toTwo d
law1-9-iso-to-unique d i =
  toTwo-unique d (to i)
    (trans (to-‚Ąď i) refl)
    (trans (to-r i) refl)

-- ¬ß Uniqueness of the from-direction.
fromTwo-unique :
  (d : Distinction) ‚Üí
  (g : Two ‚Üí S d) ‚Üí
  g L ‚Č° ‚Ąď d ‚Üí
  g R ‚Č° r d ‚Üí
  g ‚Čó fromTwo d
fromTwo-unique d g gL gR L = trans gL refl
fromTwo-unique d g gL gR R = trans gR refl

-- ¬ß Law 1.9: iso-from is unique.
law1-9-iso-from-unique : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí (i : DistinctionIso d Two-distinction) ‚Üí from i ‚Čó fromTwo d
law1-9-iso-from-unique d i =
  fromTwo-unique d (from i)
    (trans (sym (cong (from i) (to-‚Ąď i))) (from-to i (‚Ąď d)))
    (trans (sym (cong (from i) (to-r i))) (from-to i (r d)))
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 1.10: Two Normal Form Has Exactly Two Orientations}
\label{law:two-normal-form:two-orientations}

Every equivalence to \texttt{Two} is either the canonical boundary-preserving map or the swap. The proof checks the image of $\ell$: it is either $L$ or $R$. If $L$, the map agrees with \texttt{toTwo}. If $R$, it agrees with \texttt{toTwo-swap}. There is no third possibility, because \texttt{Two} has no third element.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 1.10: orientation is exhaustive.
orientation-exhaustive :
  (d : Distinction) ‚Üí
  (e : DistinctionEquiv d Two-distinction) ‚Üí
  (to e ‚Čó toTwo d) ‚äé (to e ‚Čó toTwo-swap d)
orientation-exhaustive d e with Two-cover (to e (‚Ąď d)) | Two-cover (to e (r d))
... | inj‚āĀ t‚Ąď‚Č°L | inj‚āĀ tr‚Č°L = ‚ä•-elim (to-distinct-on-boundary d e (trans t‚Ąď‚Č°L (sym tr‚Č°L)))
... | inj‚āā t‚Ąď‚Č°R | inj‚āā tr‚Č°R = ‚ä•-elim (to-distinct-on-boundary d e (trans t‚Ąď‚Č°R (sym tr‚Č°R)))
... | inj‚āĀ t‚Ąď‚Č°L | inj‚āā tr‚Č°R = inj‚āĀ (toTwo-unique d (to e) t‚Ąď‚Č°L tr‚Č°R)
... | inj‚āā t‚Ąď‚Č°R | inj‚āĀ tr‚Č°L = inj‚āā (toTwo-swap-unique d (to e) t‚Ąď‚Č°R tr‚Č°L)

law1-10-orientation-exhaustive :
  (d : Distinction) ‚Üí
  (e : DistinctionEquiv d Two-distinction) ‚Üí
  (to e ‚Čó toTwo d) ‚äé (to e ‚Čó toTwo-swap d)
law1-10-orientation-exhaustive = orientation-exhaustive
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 1.11: Automorphisms Are Exactly id Or dual}
\label{law:two-normal-form:automorphisms-id-or-dual}

The boundary behavior of any automorphism is forced into one of two patterns: fix both boundaries (identity) or swap both (dual). Any other combination‚ÄĒfixing one and moving the other‚ÄĒwould produce a non-injective map, which contradicts the inverse law.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 1.11: Automorphisms are exactly id or dual.
law1-11-Aut-sound :
  (d : Distinction) ‚Üí
  (a : Aut d) ‚Üí
  (to a ‚Čó id) ‚äé (to a ‚Čó Distinction-dual d)
law1-11-Aut-sound = Aut-sound
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 1.12: Two-Orientation Classification Is Sound And Unique}
\label{law:two-normal-form:orientation-classification}

The orientation classifier assigns every equivalence to \texttt{Two} one of two labels: \texttt{case-preserve} or \texttt{case-swap}. Soundness: the assigned label interprets back to a map that agrees pointwise with the original equivalence. Uniqueness: no equivalence can receive two different labels, because that would force $L = R$ in \texttt{Two}.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Orientation case type.
data OrientationCase : Set where
  case-preserve : OrientationCase
  case-swap     : OrientationCase

-- ¬ß Interpret an orientation case as a map.
orientationInterpret : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí OrientationCase ‚Üí S d ‚Üí Two
orientationInterpret d case-preserve = toTwo d
orientationInterpret d case-swap     = toTwo-swap d

-- ¬ß Law 1.12: orientation classification is sound.
orientationCase-sound :
  (d : Distinction) ‚Üí
  (e : DistinctionEquiv d Two-distinction) ‚Üí
  ő£ OrientationCase (őĽ c ‚Üí to e ‚Čó orientationInterpret d c)
orientationCase-sound d e with orientation-exhaustive d e
... | inj‚āĀ p = case-preserve , p
... | inj‚āā p = case-swap     , p

-- ¬ß Law 1.12: orientation classification is unique.
orientationCase-unique :
  (d : Distinction) ‚Üí
  (e : DistinctionEquiv d Two-distinction) ‚Üí
  (c‚āĀ c‚āā : OrientationCase) ‚Üí
  to e ‚Čó orientationInterpret d c‚āĀ ‚Üí
  to e ‚Čó orientationInterpret d c‚āā ‚Üí
  c‚āĀ ‚Č° c‚āā
orientationCase-unique d e case-preserve case-preserve _ _ = refl
orientationCase-unique d e case-swap     case-swap     _ _ = refl
orientationCase-unique d e case-preserve case-swap p q =
  ‚ä•-elim (Two-L‚Č†R (sym toR‚Č°L))
  where
    toR‚Č°L : R ‚Č° L
    toR‚Č°L =
      trans (sym (toTwo-swap-‚Ąď d))
        (trans (sym (q (‚Ąď d)))
          (trans (p (‚Ąď d)) (toTwo-‚Ąď d)))
orientationCase-unique d e case-swap case-preserve p q =
  ‚ä•-elim (Two-L‚Č†R (sym toR‚Č°L))
  where
    toR‚Č°L : R ‚Č° L
    toR‚Č°L =
      trans (sym (toTwo-swap-‚Ąď d))
        (trans (sym (p (‚Ąď d)))
          (trans (q (‚Ąď d)) (toTwo-‚Ąď d)))

law1-12-orientationCase-sound :
  (d : Distinction) ‚Üí
  (e : DistinctionEquiv d Two-distinction) ‚Üí
  ő£ OrientationCase (őĽ c ‚Üí to e ‚Čó orientationInterpret d c)
law1-12-orientationCase-sound = orientationCase-sound

law1-12-orientationCase-unique :
  (d : Distinction) ‚Üí
  (e : DistinctionEquiv d Two-distinction) ‚Üí
  (c‚āĀ c‚āā : OrientationCase) ‚Üí
  to e ‚Čó orientationInterpret d c‚āĀ ‚Üí
  to e ‚Čó orientationInterpret d c‚āā ‚Üí
  c‚āĀ ‚Č° c‚āā
law1-12-orientationCase-unique = orientationCase-unique
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 1.13: Automorphism Classification Is Sound And Unique}
\label{law:two-normal-form:automorphism-classification}

The same structure holds for automorphisms. Every self-equivalence receives exactly one label‚ÄĒ\texttt{case-id} or \texttt{case-dual}‚ÄĒand that label is forced by the boundary behavior. If two different labels could describe the same automorphism, their interpretations would agree pointwise, which would force $\ell = r$ via the boundary lemmas. Contradiction eliminates every unsound or non-unique automorphism witness.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Automorphism case type.
data AutCase : Set where
  case-id   : AutCase
  case-dual : AutCase

-- ¬ß Interpret an automorphism case.
autInterpret : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí AutCase ‚Üí S d ‚Üí S d
autInterpret d case-id   = id
autInterpret d case-dual = Distinction-dual d

-- ¬ß Automorphism classification is sound.
autCase-sound :
  (d : Distinction) ‚Üí
  (a : Aut d) ‚Üí
  ő£ AutCase (őĽ c ‚Üí to a ‚Čó autInterpret d c)
autCase-sound d a with Aut-sound d a
... | inj‚āĀ p = case-id   , p
... | inj‚āā p = case-dual , p

-- ¬ß Automorphism classification is unique.
autCase-unique :
  (d : Distinction) ‚Üí
  (a : Aut d) ‚Üí
  (c‚āĀ c‚āā : AutCase) ‚Üí
  to a ‚Čó autInterpret d c‚āĀ ‚Üí
  to a ‚Čó autInterpret d c‚āā ‚Üí
  c‚āĀ ‚Č° c‚āā
autCase-unique d a case-id case-id _ _ = refl
autCase-unique d a case-dual case-dual _ _ = refl
autCase-unique d a case-id case-dual p q =
  ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) ‚Ąď‚Č°r)
  where
    ‚Ąď‚Č°r : ‚Ąď d ‚Č° r d
    ‚Ąď‚Č°r =
      trans (sym (p (‚Ąď d)))
        (trans (q (‚Ąď d)) (Distinction-dual-‚Ąď d))
autCase-unique d a case-dual case-id p q =
  ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) ‚Ąď‚Č°r)
  where
    ‚Ąď‚Č°r : ‚Ąď d ‚Č° r d
    ‚Ąď‚Č°r =
      trans (sym (q (‚Ąď d)))
        (trans (p (‚Ąď d)) (Distinction-dual-‚Ąď d))

-- ¬ß Law 1.13: Automorphism classification is sound and unique.
law1-13-autCase-sound :
  (d : Distinction) ‚Üí
  (a : Aut d) ‚Üí
  ő£ AutCase (őĽ c ‚Üí to a ‚Čó autInterpret d c)
law1-13-autCase-sound = autCase-sound

law1-13-autCase-unique :
  (d : Distinction) ‚Üí
  (a : Aut d) ‚Üí
  (c‚āĀ c‚āā : AutCase) ‚Üí
  to a ‚Čó autInterpret d c‚āĀ ‚Üí
  to a ‚Čó autInterpret d c‚āā ‚Üí
  c‚āĀ ‚Č° c‚āā
law1-13-autCase-unique = autCase-unique
\end{code}

\section{K\texorpdfstring{$_4$}{4} Witness Interface}
\label{sec:classification:k4-witness-interface}

The dependent pair that bundles a case label with a proof that its interpretation matches a given endomorphism is the canonical witness of classification.
