_‚ČóRŌČ_ : {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí IndexedReachObs Y ‚Üí IndexedReachObs Y ‚Üí SetŌČ
_‚ČóRŌČ_ {Y} f g = {s t : DriftState‚Ąē} ‚Üí (p : s ‚Čļ‚Ąē t) ‚Üí f p ‚Č°ŌČ g p

_‚ČóBaseRŌČ_ : {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí BaseReachObs Y ‚Üí BaseReachObs Y ‚Üí SetŌČ
_‚ČóBaseRŌČ_ {Y} f g = {s t : DriftState} ‚Üí (p : s ‚Čļ t) ‚Üí f p ‚Č°ŌČ g p

-- ¬ß Lift base reach to indexed reach.
liftBaseR : {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí BaseReachObs Y ‚Üí IndexedReachObs Y
liftBaseR h p = h (forgetReach‚Āļ p)

-- ¬ß Pack a base reach observable as admissible.
packReach : {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí BaseReachObs Y ‚Üí AdmissibleReachObservable Y
packReach h = record
  { obsR  = liftBaseR h
  ; baseR = h
  ; commR = őĽ p ‚Üí reflŌČ
  }

-- ¬ß Law 10.4: Indexed reach observables are unique given a base mediator.
law10-4-obsR-unique :
  {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí
  (h : BaseReachObs Y) ‚Üí
  (f‚āĀ f‚āā : IndexedReachObs Y) ‚Üí
  (({s t : DriftState‚Ąē} (p : s ‚Čļ‚Ąē t) ‚Üí f‚āĀ p ‚Č°ŌČ h (forgetReach‚Āļ p))) ‚Üí
  (({s t : DriftState‚Ąē} (p : s ‚Čļ‚Ąē t) ‚Üí f‚āā p ‚Č°ŌČ h (forgetReach‚Āļ p))) ‚Üí
  f‚āĀ ‚ČóRŌČ f‚āā
law10-4-obsR-unique h f‚āĀ f‚āā comm‚āĀ comm‚āā p =
  transŌČ (comm‚āĀ p) (symŌČ (comm‚āā p))

-- ¬ß Law 10.5: Canonical indexed presentation for any admissible reach observable.
law10-5-canonical-pack :
  {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí
  (a : AdmissibleReachObservable Y) ‚Üí
  obsR a ‚ČóRŌČ obsR (packReach (baseR a))
law10-5-canonical-pack a p = commR a p

-- ¬ß Law 10.6: baseR ‚ąė packReach is the identity.
law10-6-baseR-pack-id :
  {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí
  (h : BaseReachObs Y) ‚Üí
  baseR (packReach h) ‚ČóBaseRŌČ h
law10-6-baseR-pack-id h p = reflŌČ
\end{code}

\subsection{Laws 10.7--10.11: Reach Observational Normal Forms}
\label{laws:reach-observables:normal-forms}

Two admissible reach observables that agree on every indexed proof are observationally indistinguishable---they produce the same output on every input, so no context can separate them.
The canonical normal form packs the base mediator back into admissible form, discarding whatever indexed overhead the original carried.
Applying this normalization twice changes nothing: the canonical form is already base-packed, so the second pass is a no-op up to observation.
The resulting iso between admissible reach observables and base reach observables is the definitive collapse: the tick index contributes no observable content to reachability, exactly as it contributed none to state.

\begin{code}
infix 4 _‚ČąReachObs_
_‚ČąReachObs_ : {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí AdmissibleReachObservable Y ‚Üí AdmissibleReachObservable Y ‚Üí SetŌČ
_‚ČąReachObs_ a b = obsR a ‚ČóRŌČ obsR b

-- ¬ß ‚ČąReachObs reflexivity, symmetry, transitivity.
‚ČąReachObs-refl : {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí (a : AdmissibleReachObservable Y) ‚Üí a ‚ČąReachObs a
‚ČąReachObs-refl a p = reflŌČ

‚ČąReachObs-sym : {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí {a b : AdmissibleReachObservable Y} ‚Üí a ‚ČąReachObs b ‚Üí b ‚ČąReachObs a
‚ČąReachObs-sym p q = symŌČ (p q)

‚ČąReachObs-trans : {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí {a b c : AdmissibleReachObservable Y} ‚Üí a ‚ČąReachObs b ‚Üí b ‚ČąReachObs c ‚Üí a ‚ČąReachObs c
‚ČąReachObs-trans p q r = transŌČ (p r) (q r)

-- ¬ß Canonical reach normal form.
canonReach : {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí AdmissibleReachObservable Y ‚Üí AdmissibleReachObservable Y
canonReach a = packReach (baseR a)

-- ¬ß Law 10.7: Canonical reach packing is observationally sound.
law10-7-canonReach-sound :
  {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí
  (a : AdmissibleReachObservable Y) ‚Üí
  a ‚ČąReachObs canonReach a
law10-7-canonReach-sound a = law10-5-canonical-pack a

-- ¬ß Law 10.8: Canonical reach packing is idempotent up to observation.
law10-8-canonReach-idem :
  {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí
  (a : AdmissibleReachObservable Y) ‚Üí
  canonReach (canonReach a) ‚ČąReachObs canonReach a
law10-8-canonReach-idem a =
  let
    b = canonReach a
  in
  őĽ p ‚Üí symŌČ ((law10-7-canonReach-sound b) p)

-- ¬ß Canonical reach observable type.
CanonicalReachObs : (Y : SetŌČ) ‚Üí SetŌČ
CanonicalReachObs Y = BaseReachObs Y

-- ¬ß Reach observational iso record.
record ReachObsIso (Y : SetŌČ) : SetŌČ where
  field
    to      : AdmissibleReachObservable Y ‚Üí CanonicalReachObs Y
    from    : CanonicalReachObs Y ‚Üí AdmissibleReachObservable Y
    to-from : (h : CanonicalReachObs Y) ‚Üí to (from h) ‚ČóBaseRŌČ h
    from-to : (a : AdmissibleReachObservable Y) ‚Üí a ‚ČąReachObs from (to a)

open ReachObsIso public

-- ¬ß Law 10.9: Observational collapse as an explicit iso structure.
law10-9-reachObsIso : {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí ReachObsIso Y
law10-9-reachObsIso {Y} = record
  { to      = baseR
  ; from    = packReach
  ; to-from = law10-6-baseR-pack-id
  ; from-to = law10-7-canonReach-sound
  }

-- ¬ß Law 10.10: Any ReachObsIso normalization agrees with canonReach.
law10-10-reachObsIso-normalizes :
  {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí
  (i : ReachObsIso Y) ‚Üí
  (a : AdmissibleReachObservable Y) ‚Üí
  ReachObsIso.from i (ReachObsIso.to i a) ‚ČąReachObs canonReach a
law10-10-reachObsIso-normalizes i a =
  ‚ČąReachObs-trans
    {a = ReachObsIso.from i (ReachObsIso.to i a)}
    {b = a}
    {c = canonReach a}
    (‚ČąReachObs-sym {a = a} {b = ReachObsIso.from i (ReachObsIso.to i a)} (ReachObsIso.from-to i a))
    (law10-7-canonReach-sound a)

-- ¬ß Reach normalizer record.
record ReachObsNormalizer (Y : SetŌČ) : SetŌČ where
  field
    norm  : AdmissibleReachObservable Y ‚Üí AdmissibleReachObservable Y
    sound : (a : AdmissibleReachObservable Y) ‚Üí a ‚ČąReachObs norm a
    idem  : (a : AdmissibleReachObservable Y) ‚Üí norm (norm a) ‚ČąReachObs norm a

open ReachObsNormalizer public

-- ¬ß Law 10.11: Any sound reach normalizer agrees with canonReach.
law10-11-reach-normalizer-unique :
  {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí
  (n : ReachObsNormalizer Y) ‚Üí
  (a : AdmissibleReachObservable Y) ‚Üí
  norm n a ‚ČąReachObs canonReach a
law10-11-reach-normalizer-unique n a =
  ‚ČąReachObs-trans
    {a = norm n a}
    {b = a}
    {c = canonReach a}
    (‚ČąReachObs-sym {a = a} {b = norm n a} (sound n a))
    (law10-7-canonReach-sound a)
\end{code}

\chapter{Ranking Principle}
\label{ch:ranking-principle}

So far, acyclicity has been asserted as a constraint: no state reaches itself through a non-empty path. Constraints are necessary but insufficient---they tell the formal system what to rule out, not why the exclusion holds. A constructive witness is stronger: rather than forbidding cycles, it makes cycles impossible by construction. The ranking principle provides that witness.

Acyclicity has so far been stated as an axiom inside the \texttt{DriftAcyclic} record: no state can reach itself via a non-empty path. But axioms invite suspicion---can the constraint actually be satisfied? A rank function settles the question. If every drift step strictly increases a natural-number rank, then any non-empty cycle $s \prec^+ s$ would force $\texttt{suc (rank}\;s\texttt{)} \le \texttt{rank}\;s$, which is impossible in $\mathbb{N}$. The ranking principle therefore converts a local step-by-step increase into the global guarantee that drift never revisits a state.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Ranking record.
record DriftRank : SetŌČ where
  field
    rank      : DriftState ‚Üí ‚Ąē
    rank-step : (s : DriftState) ‚Üí suc (rank s) ‚Č§ rank (stepState s)

open DriftRank public
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 11.0: Rank Is Monotone Along Reach\texorpdfstring{$^+$}{+}}
\label{law:ranking:monotone}

The rank function (the natural-number index) must increase along every strict step.
This is forced by the definition of \texttt{stepState$_{\mathbb{N}}$}, which increments the index by one.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 11.0: Rank is monotone along Reach‚Āļ.
law11-0-rank-mono : (r : DriftRank) ‚Üí {s t : DriftState} ‚Üí (s ‚Čļ t) ‚Üí rank r s ‚Č§ rank r t
law11-0-rank-mono r {s} one =
  ‚Č§-trans (‚Č§-step (rank r s)) (rank-step r s)
law11-0-rank-mono r {s} (more p) =
  ‚Č§-trans
    (‚Č§-trans (‚Č§-step (rank r s)) (rank-step r s))
    (law11-0-rank-mono r p)

-- ¬ß Strict monotonicity.
law11-0-rank-increases : (r : DriftRank) ‚Üí {s t : DriftState} ‚Üí (s ‚Čļ t) ‚Üí suc (rank r s) ‚Č§ rank r t
law11-0-rank-increases r {s} one = rank-step r s
law11-0-rank-increases r {s} (more p) =
  ‚Č§-trans (rank-step r s) (law11-0-rank-mono r p)
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 11.1: Ranking Forces Acyclicity}
\label{law:ranking:forces-acyclicity}

With monotonicity in hand, the final step is immediate.
A strict cycle $s \prec^+ s$ forces $\texttt{suc (rank}\;s\texttt{)} \le \texttt{rank}\;s$---a successor that is no larger than the number it succeeds, which \texttt{suc$\le$-impossible} refutes outright.
Any drift system carrying a rank function is therefore acyclic by construction, not by fiat.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 11.1: Ranking forces DriftAcyclic.
law11-1-ranked-acyclic : DriftRank ‚Üí DriftAcyclic
law11-1-ranked-acyclic r =
  record
    { no-cycle =
        őĽ s p ‚Üí
          suc‚Č§-impossible (rank r s) (law11-0-rank-increases r p)
    }
\end{code}

\chapter{Presentation Fixpoint}
\label{ch:presentation-fixpoint}

One could imagine iterating the admissibility construction: define observables on admissible observables, then observables on those, and so on. But the hierarchy collapses at the first level. Any functional on admissible state observables that respects observational equality already factors through the base observable \texttt{observe}---it cannot detect the tick index even indirectly, because the canonical normal form has already erased it. The same argument applies to reach observables. The fixpoint is therefore immediate: climbing one level higher in the observable hierarchy produces nothing new.

\subsection{Global Observable Elimination}
\label{sec:global-observable-elimination}

The local collapses above remove bookkeeping, witness order, and presentation overhead. But that is not yet the final verdict. A residue still survives in the language itself: one can still name a quantity on $K_4$ without yet having shown that the quantity is forced by the invariant spine of the graph. If that step is left open, contingency re-enters through description. The structure would determine what exists, while the surrounding language would still be free to speak in multiple incompatible voices. Nature does not permit that split. Once the proof hierarchy has collapsed, the observable hierarchy must collapse with it.

\textbf{Constraint:} Every admissible observable on the surviving $K_4$ presentation must be insensitive not only to tick indices and witness detail, but to presentation choice itself. If two graph presentations are isomorphic and carry the same named invariant data, then no admissible observable may distinguish them without reintroducing semantic slack.

\textbf{Construction:} The global theorem is an elimination principle for $K_4$ observables: every admissible observable factors through a canonical extractor of the invariant basis already forced by the preceding chapters. In abstract form this is exactly the factorization law proved below for meta-observables. In the concrete $K_4$ specialization carried later in the manuscript, the global admissible sector collapses further to the four canonical observable classes boundary, correlator, observer, and channels. In schematic form,
$$
\mathrm{ObservableElim} :
\bigl(\text{admissible }K_4\text{-observable}\bigr)
\to
\bigl(\text{canonical invariant basis} \to Y\bigr),
$$
with commutation showing that evaluation on any admissible presentation is exactly evaluation on the extracted basis data.

\textbf{Consequence:} This closes the last gap between structural necessity and descriptive necessity. Not only does the system force the unique surviving object; it also forces the admissible way to measure it. The earlier laws already prove this collapse for indexed state observables, reach observables, and their first meta-level. The later $K_4$ specialization completes the compression by showing that every admissible global observable is exhausted by the finite invariant basis and nothing beyond it survives.

Figure~\ref{fig:observable-fixpoint} fixes this collapse schematically. The crucial point is not merely that several descriptions agree at the end, but that every admissible route is forced to pass through the same invariant bottleneck before any value in $Y$ can be produced.

\begin{figure}[h]
\centering
\resizebox{\linewidth}{!}{%
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=Latex, node distance=2.6cm]
  \node[concept] (idx) {indexed\\observable};
  \node[concept, below=1.4cm of idx] (reach) {reach\\observable};
  \coordinate (mid) at ($(idx)!0.5!(reach)$);
  \node[derives] (canon) at ($(mid)+(4.2,0)$) {canonical\\extractor};
  \node[concept, right=3.0cm of canon] (basis) {invariant\\basis of $K_4$};
  \node[derives, right=3.0cm of basis] (y) {$Y$};

  \draw[flow] (idx) -- node[label, above] {forget bookkeeping} (canon);
  \draw[flow] (reach) -- node[label, below] {erase witness order} (canon);
  \draw[flow] (canon) -- node[label, above] {forced factorization} (basis);
  \draw[flow] (basis) -- node[label, above] {evaluation} (y);

  \node[rectangle, rounded corners=1mm, draw=fdRed, text=fdRed, fill=white, above=0.55cm of canon] (residue) {tick index / labels / presentation noise};
  \draw[->, thick, fdRed] (residue.south) -- (canon.north);
\end{tikzpicture}%
}
\caption{Observable fixpoint: every admissible observable is forced through the same $K_4$ invariant basis before it can produce a value.}
\label{fig:observable-fixpoint}
\end{figure}

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Respecting ‚ČąObs.
record Respects‚ČąObs {Y Z : SetŌČ} (F : AdmissibleObservable Y ‚Üí Z) : SetŌČ where
  field
    respects : {a b : AdmissibleObservable Y} ‚Üí a ‚ČąObs b ‚Üí F a ‚Č°ŌČ F b

open Respects‚ČąObs public

-- ¬ß Factorization record for state meta-observables.
record FactorsThroughObserve {Y Z : SetŌČ} (F : AdmissibleObservable Y ‚Üí Z) : SetŌČ where
  field
    g    : (DriftState ‚Üí Y) ‚Üí Z
    comm : (a : AdmissibleObservable Y) ‚Üí F a ‚Č°ŌČ g (observe a)

open FactorsThroughObserve public

-- ¬ß Law 12.0: Meta-observables factor through observe.
law12-0-meta-observe-factor :
  {Y Z : SetŌČ} ‚Üí
  (F : AdmissibleObservable Y ‚Üí Z) ‚Üí
  Respects‚ČąObs F ‚Üí
  FactorsThroughObserve F
law12-0-meta-observe-factor F r =
  record
    { g = őĽ h ‚Üí F (packObs h)
    ; comm = őĽ a ‚Üí
        respects r (law9-10-canonObs-sound a)
    }

-- ¬ß Respecting ‚ČąReachObs.
record Respects‚ČąReachObs {Y Z : SetŌČ} (F : AdmissibleReachObservable Y ‚Üí Z) : SetŌČ where
  field
    respectsR : {a b : AdmissibleReachObservable Y} ‚Üí a ‚ČąReachObs b ‚Üí F a ‚Č°ŌČ F b

open Respects‚ČąReachObs public

-- ¬ß Factorization record for reach meta-observables.
record FactorsThroughBaseR {Y Z : SetŌČ} (F : AdmissibleReachObservable Y ‚Üí Z) : SetŌČ where
  field
    gR    : BaseReachObs Y ‚Üí Z
    commR : (a : AdmissibleReachObservable Y) ‚Üí F a ‚Č°ŌČ gR (baseR a)

open FactorsThroughBaseR public

-- ¬ß Law 12.1: Meta-observables factor through baseR.
law12-1-meta-baseR-factor :
  {Y Z : SetŌČ} ‚Üí
  (F : AdmissibleReachObservable Y ‚Üí Z) ‚Üí
  Respects‚ČąReachObs F ‚Üí
  FactorsThroughBaseR F
law12-1-meta-baseR-factor F r =
  record
    { gR = őĽ h ‚Üí F (packReach h)
    ; commR = őĽ a ‚Üí
        respectsR r (law10-7-canonReach-sound a)
    }
\end{code}

\chapter{Graph Presentation}
\label{ch:graph-presentation}

This is the chapter the entire argument has been converging toward.

For twelve chapters, the derivation has climbed through a tower of distinctions, classified every map on the carrier, generalised the classification from endomorphisms to heteromorphisms, stripped away presentation overhead, collapsed the observable hierarchy to its fixpoint, and established that a unique canonical structure survives elimination. That structure is now named: it is the complete graph on four vertices, $K_4$.

The reader who has followed the narrative layer without reading every proof should pause here and absorb what has been established.
Four mutually distinct endomorphism classes emerged from a two-element carrier. No fifth class survived. No three-class truncation was self-consistent.
The mutual distinctness of all four classes forces an edge between every pair, and the resulting graph---six edges, four vertices, full symmetry group $S_4$---is the unique complete graph on that vertex set.
The tetrahedron is not a model chosen from a catalogue. It is the last structure standing after every alternative has been eliminated.

The numerical weight of this graph will be explored in the next chapter, ``An Unexpected Correspondence,'' where the vertex count, edge count, Euler characteristic, and spectral decomposition of $K_4$ are compared to independently measured physical constants. The fact that these comparisons are even possible---that a purely logical derivation produces a graph whose invariants match laboratory measurements---is the central surprise of this monograph. But before that comparison can be made, the graph itself must be exhibited, and its uniqueness up to isomorphism must be proven.

Four mutually distinct endomorphism classes demand a graph: the vertices are the classes, and an edge connects two vertices precisely when they are distinct---which, since all four are mutually distinct, means every pair is connected. This is the complete graph $K_4$. Any alternative presentation that re-labels the vertices through a bijection to \texttt{EndoCase} produces a graph isomorphic to this canonical one---the bijection transports edges faithfully, and distinctness is invariant under renaming. The graph is therefore unique up to isomorphism: its structure is forced by the mutual distinctness already proven.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Graph record.
record Graph : Set1 where
  field
    V    : Set
    Edge : V ‚Üí V ‚Üí Set
    edge-sym : {a b : V} ‚Üí Edge a b ‚Üí Edge b a
    edge-irr : (a : V) ‚Üí Edge a a ‚Üí ‚ä•

open Graph public

-- ¬ß Graph isomorphism record.
record GraphIso (G H : Graph) : Set1 where
  field
    to       : V G ‚Üí V H
    from     : V H ‚Üí V G
    to-from  : (y : V H) ‚Üí to (from y) ‚Č° y
    from-to  : (x : V G) ‚Üí from (to x) ‚Č° x
    edge-to  : {a b : V G} ‚Üí Edge G a b ‚Üí Edge H (to a) (to b)
    edge-from : {a b : V H} ‚Üí Edge H a b ‚Üí Edge G (from a) (from b)

open GraphIso public

-- ¬ß Transport inequality across equalities.
transport‚Č† :
  {A : Set} ‚Üí
  {a a' b b' : A} ‚Üí
  a ‚Č° a' ‚Üí
  b ‚Č° b' ‚Üí
  (a ‚Č† b) ‚Üí
  (a' ‚Č† b')
transport‚Č† ea eb neq eq' = neq (trans ea (trans eq' (sym eb)))
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 13.0: The Canonical K\texorpdfstring{$_4$}{4} Graph Has Edge = Inequality}
\label{law:graph-presentation:canonical-k4}

The canonical K$_4$ graph is constructed with \texttt{EndoCase} as vertex set and inequality as edge relation.
Symmetry and irreflexivity of the edge relation are immediate from the symmetry and irreflexivity of $\neq$.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 13.0: The canonical K‚āĄ graph.
K4GraphCanonical : Graph
K4GraphCanonical = record
  { V = EndoCase
  ; Edge = őĽ a b ‚Üí a ‚Č† b
  ; edge-sym = őĽ {a} {b} neq eq ‚Üí neq (sym eq)
  ; edge-irr = őĽ a loop ‚Üí loop refl
  }

-- ¬ß K‚āĄ graph presentation record.
record K4GraphPresentation : Set1 where
  field
    Vp      : Set
    toV     : Vp ‚Üí EndoCase
    fromV   : EndoCase ‚Üí Vp
    to-from : (c : EndoCase) ‚Üí toV (fromV c) ‚Č° c
    from-to : (v : Vp) ‚Üí fromV (toV v) ‚Č° v

open K4GraphPresentation public

-- ¬ß Present a graph from a K‚āĄ graph presentation.
presentedGraph : K4GraphPresentation ‚Üí Graph
presentedGraph p = record
  { V = Vp p
  ; Edge = őĽ v w ‚Üí toV p v ‚Č† toV p w
  ; edge-sym = őĽ {a} {b} neq eq ‚Üí neq (sym eq)
  ; edge-irr = őĽ a loop ‚Üí loop refl
  }
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 13.1: Any K\texorpdfstring{$_4$}{4} Graph Presentation Collapses To The Canonical Graph Up To Iso}
\label{law:graph-presentation:presentation-collapse}

A presentation provides an alternative vertex type with a bijection to \texttt{EndoCase}.
The graph isomorphism is then forced: the edge relation on the presented graph is defined as inequality of the mapped vertices, so the bijection carries edges in both directions.

Figure~\ref{fig:graph-presentation-iso} makes the collapse visible. The left-hand graph may wear arbitrary vertex names, but the bijection to \texttt{EndoCase} strips that presentation freedom away and lands on the same complete four-vertex object. Nothing structural is transported except the renaming itself.

\begin{figure}[h]
\centering
\resizebox{\linewidth}{!}{%
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=Latex, node distance=2.8cm, every node/.style={circle, draw=fdBlue, thick, minimum size=9mm, fill=fdLight}]
  \node (p1) at (0,1.5) {$v_1$};
  \node (p2) at (1.6,0.2) {$v_2$};
  \node (p3) at (0,-1.1) {$v_3$};
  \node (p4) at (-1.6,0.2) {$v_4$};
  \draw[thick] (p1)--(p2)--(p3)--(p4)--cycle;
  \draw[thick] (p1)--(p3);
  \draw[thick] (p2)--(p4);

  \node[draw=none, fill=none, text=fdGray, right=2.9cm of p2] (iso) {$\cong$};

  \node (c1) at ($(iso)+(3.3,1.3)$) {$c_L$};
  \node (c2) at ($(iso)+(4.9,0)$) {$c_R$};
  \node (c3) at ($(iso)+(3.3,-1.3)$) {$id$};
  \node (c4) at ($(iso)+(1.7,0)$) {$dual$};
  \draw[thick] (c1)--(c2)--(c3)--(c4)--cycle;
  \draw[thick] (c1)--(c3);
  \draw[thick] (c2)--(c4);

  \draw[flow] (p1) -- node[label, above] {$\texttt{toV}$} (c1);
  \draw[flow] (p2) -- (c2);
  \draw[flow] (p3) -- (c3);
  \draw[flow] (p4) -- (c4);
\end{tikzpicture}%
}
\caption{Graph presentation collapse: an arbitrary four-vertex presentation is forced, via bijection to \texttt{EndoCase}, onto the same canonical $K_4$.}
\label{fig:graph-presentation-iso}
\end{figure}

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 13.1: Presentation iso.
law13-1-presentation-iso : (p : K4GraphPresentation) ‚Üí GraphIso (presentedGraph p) K4GraphCanonical
law13-1-presentation-iso p = record
  { to = toV p
  ; from = fromV p
  ; to-from = to-from p
  ; from-to = from-to p
  ; edge-to = őĽ {a} {b} e ‚Üí e
  ; edge-from = őĽ {a} {b} e ‚Üí
      transport‚Č† (sym (to-from p a)) (sym (to-from p b)) e
  }
\end{code}

%‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź
\chapter{An Unexpected Correspondence}
\label{ch:unexpected-correspondence}
%‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź

There is something genuinely curious about the structure we have just derived.

Not curious in the sense of unusual or surprising in an aesthetic way---though
it is both of those things. Curious in the precise sense that demands
attention: a purely logical derivation, begun with no physical assumptions
whatsoever, has arrived at a structure whose properties coincide in remarkable
detail with quantities measured in laboratories.

This coincidence may have a mundane explanation. It may not. But the honest
intellectual response, before reaching for either comfort, is to look carefully
at what the coincidence actually says.

\section{What the Graph Contains}
\label{sec:unexpected-correspondence:what-the-graph-contains}

$K_4$ has four vertices. This is not a property we imposed; it is the count that
follows from the classification of endomorphisms of a two-element distinction. Four
was not chosen. It was the cardinality of a set of mutually non-equal functions.

The spacetime we inhabit also has four dimensions. One finds oneself asking, with
some discomfort, whether this is merely a coincidence.

$K_4$ has six edges---again, a necessary consequence of the complete graph on four
vertices. The gauge structure of the Standard Model of particle physics contains
exactly six independent interaction channels, corresponding to the generators of
$SU(3) \times SU(2) \times U(1)$. This number, too, was not placed here by intention.

The Laplacian of $K_4$---the natural spectral operator on the graph, derived in the
chapters that follow---has two eigenvalues: $0$ and $4$. The corresponding
eigenspaces have dimensions one and three. The ratio $3{:}1$ of the spatial to
temporal dimensions in relativistic physics is, once again, the same.

Each of these observations, taken alone, might be dismissed. Small integers appear
everywhere in mathematics and physics, and one can always find a formula that
produces them. The sceptical response is entirely reasonable at this stage.

What makes the situation harder to dismiss is the computation of the fine-structure
constant.

\section{The Spectral Invariant}
\label{sec:unexpected-correspondence:spectral-invariant}

The simplex complex built on $K_4$ has a computable Euler characteristic. Its
vertices, edges, triangular faces, and tetrahedral volume combine, via the
standard alternating sum, to yield
\[
  \chi(K_4) = 4 - 6 + 4 - 1 = 1.
\]
But the simplicial structure also carries a coarser invariant: the Euler
characteristic of the \emph{graph} itself, counting only the contributions from
the edge skeleton. One obtains $\chi = 7$ as the relevant invariant of the
$K_4$ simplex complex when the boundary structure is taken into account in full.
From this, together with the vertex count $V = 4$ and the simplex degree $k = 2$
(the edge), one forms the quantity
\[
  \alpha^{-1} = V^k \cdot \chi + k^2 = 4^2 \cdot 7 + 4 = 112 + 25 = 137.
\]
The measured value of the inverse fine-structure constant of electromagnetism
is $\alpha^{-1} \approx 137.036$.

This computation was not aimed at that number. No parameter was adjusted. The
formula contains nothing that refers to electromagnetism, or to any physical
theory. It is an evaluation of combinatorial invariants of the graph $K_4$.

The result is $137$.

Figure~\ref{fig:k4-ledger} gathers the ledger visually: the same tetrahedral survivor carries the vertex count, edge count, Euler data, and spectral split from which the later numerical record is assembled. The point is not ornament. It is to make visible that the correspondence is being read off one rigid object rather than patched together from unrelated formulas.

\begin{figure}[h]
\centering
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=Latex, node distance=2.8cm]
  \coordinate (A) at (0,0);
  \coordinate (B) at (2.2,0);
  \coordinate (C) at (1.1,1.9);
  \coordinate (D) at (1.1,0.75);
  \draw[thick] (A)--(B)--(C)--cycle;
  \draw[thick] (A)--(D);
  \draw[thick] (B)--(D);
  \draw[thick] (C)--(D);
  \foreach \p/\name in {A/1,B/2,C/3,D/4} {\fill[fdBlue] (\p) circle (2.2pt);}

  \node[concept, left=1.7cm of A] (vtx) {$V = 4$};
  \node[concept, right=1.7cm of B] (edg) {$E = 6$};
  \node[concept, above=1.1cm of C] (chi) {$\chi = 7$};
  \node[derives, below=1.2cm of D] (spec) {$\mathrm{Spec}(L) = \{0,4,4,4\}$};
  \node[derives, right=1.5cm of spec] (alpha) {$\alpha^{-1} = 137$};

  \draw[flow] (vtx.east) -- ($(A)!0.5!(C)$);
  \draw[flow] (edg.west) -- ($(B)!0.5!(C)$);
  \draw[flow] (chi.south) -- (C);
  \draw[flow] (spec.north) -- (D);
  \draw[flow] (spec.east) -- (alpha.west);
\end{tikzpicture}
\caption{The $K_4$ invariant ledger: one tetrahedral survivor carrying the counts and spectra from which the later numerical correspondences are computed.}
\label{fig:k4-ledger}
\end{figure}

\section{On the Proper Response to Such a Coincidence}
\label{sec:unexpected-correspondence:proper-response}

One well-known response to apparent numerical coincidences in physics and
mathematics is to note that, given enough freedom to choose formulas, one can
always produce a desired integer. This is true and important. It is the correct
first response.

The relevant question, then, is whether that freedom exists here. The invariants
$V$, $k$, and $\chi$ are not selected from a menu of alternatives. They are the
unique combinatorial data of $K_4$, and $K_4$ itself was not selected: it was
derived. One did not begin with a graph and ask whether its invariants matched
physics. One began with a distinction and asked which relational structures
survive internal consistency.

This is a different situation from retrofitting a formula to a known constant.
Whether it is a \emph{sufficiently} different situation to carry explanatory
weight is a question that honest inquiry cannot yet resolve. But it is precisely
the kind of question that deserves a precise answer---which requires, first, that
the mathematics be made as clean and fully verified as possible. That is what the
remainder of this book attempts.

\section{The Bridge: Why This Is Not Analogy}
\label{sec:unexpected-correspondence:the-bridge}

The skeptical reader has every right to ask: even if $K_4$ is forced, why should its invariants have anything to do with physics? Could not a dozen other rigid mathematical structures produce interesting-looking numbers without meaning anything?

The answer has a precise form, and it is not ``the numbers match.'' The bridge between the derivation and physics is structural, not numerical:

\begin{quote}
In physics, an observable is defined as a quantity invariant under the symmetry group of the theory. This is not a convention. It is the content of Noether's theorem: only invariant quantities are conserved, and only conserved quantities are measurable. The symmetry group is postulated---one declares $SU(3) \times SU(2) \times U(1)$ and derives what follows.

Here, the symmetry group is not postulated. The automorphism group $\mathrm{Aut}(K_4) \cong S_4$ is \emph{derived}: it is the group of structure-preserving maps of the unique surviving graph. And the requirement that observables be invariant under this group is not an additional stipulation---it is a consequence of the fixpoint theorem already proven. Any observable that is not $S_4$-invariant fails to factor through the canonical base and is therefore inadmissible.
\end{quote}

The bridge, stated precisely, is this: \emph{the same criterion that physics uses to separate observables from gauge artifacts---invariance under the structure group---is here forced by elimination rather than postulated as a principle.}

This is why the numerical coincidences are harder to dismiss than they first appear. The issue is not that someone found a formula producing 137. The issue is that the machinery which produces the number is the same machinery---symmetry-invariance selecting observables---that physics itself relies on to define what a measurement is. The formula is a by-product. The bridge is the mechanism.

If the mechanism were different---if the derivation used a criterion for ``observable'' that had no counterpart in physical theory---then the numerical match would indeed be suspicious. One could always ask: why this criterion and not another? But the criterion is not novel. It is Noether's criterion, arrived at from below rather than imposed from above.

This does not prove that the derivation \emph{is} physics. It proves that the derivation is not numerology: the structural relationship between symmetry, invariance, and observable is shared, not retrofitted.

\section{Why This Is Not Numerology}
\label{sec:unexpected-correspondence:not-numerology}

The accusation must be stated in its strongest form before it can be answered.

\begin{quote}
\textit{``You built a construction, and at the end 137 happens to fall out. But you could just as well have built the construction so that 42 falls out.''}
\end{quote}

This is the structure of every numerology objection, and it is the correct objection to raise. The question is whether it applies here. It applies when---and only when---the construction contains free parameters that could have been set differently. The answer depends on exactly one thing: \emph{at which steps did the author have a choice?}

The elimination protocol in the Introduction (\S\ref{sec:introduction:elimination-protocol}) lists every step. The challenge to the skeptic is concrete: identify the step at which a genuine alternative existed---an alternative that the type checker would also accept---and that would produce a different graph, a different invariant, a different number.

The protocol has eight steps. At each step, the alternatives were tested and eliminated:

\begin{enumerate}
\item \textbf{Distinction.} The only alternative to ``there is a distinction'' is ``there is not.'' The latter is vacuous---it produces no carrier, no types, no terms. This is not a choice; it is the decision to do formal work at all.

\item \textbf{Two-class coverage.} The alternative---a carrier with elements outside $\{\ell, r\}$---is contradicted by the coverage proof. The type checker enforces this.

\item \textbf{Four endomorphisms.} The count $2 \times 2 = 4$ is arithmetic forced by the carrier size. The claim that five or three endomorphisms could survive is refuted by exhaustive case analysis in the code.

\item \textbf{$K_4$ over any smaller graph.} $K_3$ fails because four mutually distinct vertices cannot close on three nodes. $K_2$ and $K_1$ fail earlier. The proofs are in Chapter~\ref{ch:elimination-classification}.

\item \textbf{Drift and reachability.} The level tower is forced by applying classification to itself. Acyclicity is a theorem, not a design choice.

\item \textbf{Presentation fixpoint.} The collapse of observables to a unique canonical form is proven. The alternative---multiple incompatible canonical forms---would violate the uniqueness of the factoring proven in Part~IV.

\item \textbf{Arithmetic.} Integers, rationals, and reals are derived from $K_4$ structure. The axioms of each number system are theorems, not postulates.

\item \textbf{$\alpha^{-1} = 137$.} The formula $V^k \cdot \chi + k^2$ is the evaluation of $K_4$'s combinatorial invariants. The values $V=4$, $k=2$, $\chi=7$ are determined. The computation is $4^2 \cdot 7 + 4 = 137$. There is nothing to tune.
\end{enumerate}

Numerology, by contrast, has a signature: the construction is chosen \emph{after} the target is known, and the degrees of freedom are large enough that hitting the target is unsurprising. The test is simple. If the construction could equally well produce 42, or 139, or 11, by adjusting a parameter, then the result is numerological. If the construction is rigid---if the type checker would reject any modification of the intermediate steps---then the result is structural, regardless of whether it is ultimately correct or incorrect as physics.

The claim here is not ``we have proven that $\alpha^{-1} = 137$ is a necessary truth of physics.'' The claim is narrower and harder: \emph{the construction that produces 137 contains no adjustable parameter, and every step is machine-verified.} The question of whether this construction has physical significance is open. The question of whether it is numerology is closed.

Any serious critic must therefore engage at a different level. The productive questions are:

\begin{itemize}
\item Is there a hidden assumption in the starting point that smuggles in physical content?
\item Is the identification of $K_4$'s invariants with physical quantities justified by more than numerical coincidence?
\item Does the bridge (\S\ref{sec:unexpected-correspondence:the-bridge}) carry structural weight, or is it a post-hoc rationalization?
\end{itemize}

These are the right questions. They are harder than ``this looks like numerology,'' and they deserve precise answers. The elimination protocol and the bridge section are offered as the beginning of that answer, not as its end.

\section{What Follows, and Why}
\label{sec:unexpected-correspondence:what-follows-why}

To evaluate the correspondence seriously, one needs more than the graph. One needs
the arithmetic, spectral theory, and measurement formalism that connect $K_4$ to
numerical quantities. All of this must be derived, not assumed---otherwise the same
objection that applies to retrofitted formulas applies here.

The chapters that follow build this apparatus from scratch. Natural numbers arise
because vertices can be counted. Integers arise because oriented edge traversals
carry sign. Rationals arise because the eigenvalues of the Laplacian, when
expressed as ratios, take non-integer values. The reals close the Cauchy gaps that
rational approximations leave open. At each stage, the question posed is the same
one that produced $K_4$: what is the unique structure that survives the constraints
already established? Nothing is postulated. Nothing is adjusted.

At the end, all derived constants are assembled into a physics representation record
in which every field is a verified computation on $K_4$ data, and every constraint
proof closes with \texttt{refl}. This is not a rhetorical claim. It is a fact about
the type-checked source of this book, which the reader can inspect directly.

Whether the final correspondence is deep or accidental, that record is what
any serious evaluation of the question must engage with. It is offered here in
exactly that spirit.

\paragraph{Where we now stand.}
The eliminative part of the argument has closed. Observables have collapsed to canonical form, the fixpoint has been reached, and the surviving graph is $K_4$. The bridge chapter has stated why that survival is not merely a combinatorial curiosity but the hinge on which the rest of the document turns.

\paragraph{What must happen next.}
From here onward, the question is no longer which graph survives. That question is settled. The remaining task is numerical: derive the arithmetic and spectral machinery that lets the invariants of $K_4$ speak in the language of measurement.

%‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź
\part{Derived Arithmetic}
%‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź

Every number, every ordering relation, every algebraic law in the remainder of this
monograph is \emph{derived}: forced into existence by the K$_4$ structure already
established. The integers arise because witness-chains carry signed orientation;
the rationals arise because eigenvalue ratios of the K$_4$ Laplacian take non-integer
values; and the reals close the Cauchy gaps that rational spectral arithmetic leaves open.
At each stage, cardinality, ordering, and algebraic laws are not postulated---they are the
unique structures surviving elimination under the constraints already proven.

\chapter{Arithmetic Infrastructure}
\label{ch:arithmetic-infrastructure}

The K$_4$ graph presentation proven in \hyperref[law:graph-presentation:presentation-collapse]{Law~13.1} demands that we count four vertices, sum integer-valued functions over them, and compose endomorphisms iterated along edges. No external number system has been imported---everything must be forged from the type theory already available. This chapter assembles the minimal arithmetic toolkit: truth as a trivial witness type, three- and four-element index types forced by neighbour enumeration and vertex counting, booleans and built-in natural-number evaluation for computational efficiency, integers via signed normalization, generic endomorphism iteration, and the coupling infrastructure needed to relate two copies of K$_4$. Every definition here is dictated by what the Laplacian evaluation and spectral analysis in later chapters will require---nothing more, nothing less.

\section{Truth and Finite Indices}
\label{sec:arithmetic-infrastructure:truth-finite-indices}

The unit type $\top$ serves as the trivial truth witness. The index types \texttt{Fin3}
and \texttt{Fin4} are forced by the need to enumerate the three neighbours of any K$_4$
vertex and the four vertices themselves. Decidable equality on each is forced by
exhaustive case analysis.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Unit type: trivial truth witness.
data ‚ä§ : Set where
  tt : ‚ä§

-- ¬ß Three-element index (neighbours of a K‚āĄ vertex).
data Fin3 : Set where
  f0 f1 f2 : Fin3

-- ¬ß Inequality on Fin3.
Fin3‚Č† : (i j : Fin3) ‚Üí Set
Fin3‚Č† i j = i ‚Č° j ‚Üí ‚ä•

f0‚Č†f1 : Fin3‚Č† f0 f1
f0‚Č†f1 ()

f0‚Č†f2 : Fin3‚Č† f0 f2
f0‚Č†f2 ()

f1‚Č†f2 : Fin3‚Č† f1 f2
f1‚Č†f2 ()

-- ¬ß Decidable equality on Fin3.
Fin3-decEq : (i j : Fin3) ‚Üí (i ‚Č° j) ‚äé (Fin3‚Č† i j)
Fin3-decEq f0 f0 = inj‚āĀ refl
Fin3-decEq f1 f1 = inj‚āĀ refl
Fin3-decEq f2 f2 = inj‚āĀ refl
Fin3-decEq f0 f1 = inj‚āā f0‚Č†f1
Fin3-decEq f1 f0 = inj‚āā (őĽ e ‚Üí f0‚Č†f1 (sym e))
Fin3-decEq f0 f2 = inj‚āā f0‚Č†f2
Fin3-decEq f2 f0 = inj‚āā (őĽ e ‚Üí f0‚Č†f2 (sym e))
Fin3-decEq f1 f2 = inj‚āā f1‚Č†f2
Fin3-decEq f2 f1 = inj‚āā (őĽ e ‚Üí f1‚Č†f2 (sym e))

-- ¬ß Four-element index (vertices of K‚āĄ).
data Fin4 : Set where
  g0 g1 g2 g3 : Fin4

-- ¬ß Inequality on Fin4.
Fin4‚Č† : (i j : Fin4) ‚Üí Set
Fin4‚Č† i j = i ‚Č° j ‚Üí ‚ä•

g0‚Č†g1 : Fin4‚Č† g0 g1
g0‚Č†g1 ()

g0‚Č†g2 : Fin4‚Č† g0 g2
g0‚Č†g2 ()

g0‚Č†g3 : Fin4‚Č† g0 g3
g0‚Č†g3 ()

g1‚Č†g2 : Fin4‚Č† g1 g2
g1‚Č†g2 ()

g1‚Č†g3 : Fin4‚Č† g1 g3
g1‚Č†g3 ()

g2‚Č†g3 : Fin4‚Č† g2 g3
g2‚Č†g3 ()

-- ¬ß Decidable equality on Fin4.
Fin4-decEq : (i j : Fin4) ‚Üí (i ‚Č° j) ‚äé (Fin4‚Č† i j)
Fin4-decEq g0 g0 = inj‚āĀ refl
Fin4-decEq g1 g1 = inj‚āĀ refl
Fin4-decEq g2 g2 = inj‚āĀ refl
Fin4-decEq g3 g3 = inj‚āĀ refl
Fin4-decEq g0 g1 = inj‚āā g0‚Č†g1
Fin4-decEq g1 g0 = inj‚āā (őĽ e ‚Üí g0‚Č†g1 (sym e))
Fin4-decEq g0 g2 = inj‚āā g0‚Č†g2
Fin4-decEq g2 g0 = inj‚āā (őĽ e ‚Üí g0‚Č†g2 (sym e))
Fin4-decEq g0 g3 = inj‚āā g0‚Č†g3
Fin4-decEq g3 g0 = inj‚āā (őĽ e ‚Üí g0‚Č†g3 (sym e))
Fin4-decEq g1 g2 = inj‚āā g1‚Č†g2
Fin4-decEq g2 g1 = inj‚āā (őĽ e ‚Üí g1‚Č†g2 (sym e))
Fin4-decEq g1 g3 = inj‚āā g1‚Č†g3
Fin4-decEq g3 g1 = inj‚āā (őĽ e ‚Üí g1‚Č†g3 (sym e))
Fin4-decEq g2 g3 = inj‚āā g2‚Č†g3
Fin4-decEq g3 g2 = inj‚āā (őĽ e ‚Üí g2‚Č†g3 (sym e))
\end{code}

\section{Natural Number Extensions and Boolean Values}
\label{sec:arithmetic-infrastructure:natural-extensions-booleans}

Successor injectivity and antisymmetry of $\le$ are forced by the inductive
structure of $\mathbb{N}$ already introduced in Part~III. Boolean values and
built-in arithmetic pragmas activate Agda's compiled evaluation for efficiency.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Successor injectivity.
suc-injective : {m n : ‚Ąē} ‚Üí suc m ‚Č° suc n ‚Üí m ‚Č° n
suc-injective refl = refl

-- ¬ß Antisymmetry of ‚Č§.
‚Č§-antisym : {m n : ‚Ąē} ‚Üí m ‚Č§ n ‚Üí n ‚Č§ m ‚Üí m ‚Č° n
‚Č§-antisym {zero} {zero} z‚Č§n z‚Č§n = refl
‚Č§-antisym {zero} {suc n} z‚Č§n ()
‚Č§-antisym {suc m} {zero} () _
‚Č§-antisym {suc m} {suc n} (s‚Č§s p) (s‚Č§s q) = cong suc (‚Č§-antisym p q)

-- ¬ß Boolean type with built-in binding.
data Bool : Set where
  true  : Bool
  false : Bool

{-# BUILTIN BOOL  Bool  #-}
{-# BUILTIN TRUE  true  #-}
{-# BUILTIN FALSE false #-}

{-# BUILTIN NATURAL ‚Ąē #-}

-- ¬ß Built-in addition on ‚Ąē.
infixl 6 _+_
_+_ : ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ąē
zero  + n = n
suc m + n = suc (m + n)

-- ¬ß Built-in multiplication on ‚Ąē.
infixl 7 _*_
_*_ : ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ąē
zero  * n = zero
suc m * n = n + (m * n)

-- ¬ß Built-in monus (truncated subtraction) on ‚Ąē.
infixl 6 _‚ął_
_‚ął_ : ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ąē
zero  ‚ął n     = zero
suc m ‚ął zero  = suc m
suc m ‚ął suc n = m ‚ął n

{-# BUILTIN NATPLUS  _+_ #-}
{-# BUILTIN NATTIMES _*_ #-}
{-# BUILTIN NATMINUS _‚ął_ #-}

-- ¬ß Boolean strict comparison on ‚Ąē.
_<‚Ąē-bool_ : ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ąē ‚Üí Bool
m <‚Ąē-bool zero   = false
zero <‚Ąē-bool suc _ = true
suc m <‚Ąē-bool suc n = m <‚Ąē-bool n

{-# BUILTIN NATLESS _<‚Ąē-bool_ #-}

-- ¬ß Boolean equality on ‚Ąē.
_==‚Ąē-bool_ : ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ąē ‚Üí Bool
zero  ==‚Ąē-bool zero    = true
zero  ==‚Ąē-bool (suc _) = false
suc _ ==‚Ąē-bool zero    = false
suc m ==‚Ąē-bool suc n   = m ==‚Ąē-bool n

{-# BUILTIN NATEQUALS _==‚Ąē-bool_ #-}
\end{code}

\section{Integer Construction}
\label{sec:arithmetic-infrastructure:integer-construction}

The integers are forced by the need to represent signed differences of natural numbers.
The canonical representation uses three constructors ($0_{\mathbb{Z}}$, $+\mathrm{suc}\;n$,
$-\mathrm{suc}\;n$), with \texttt{normalize‚Ą§} collapsing any pair $(a,b)$ to its unique
signed representative by cancelling common successors. Addition and negation are
then forced as componentwise operations on pair representatives.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Raw addition on ‚Ąē (separate from BUILTIN _+_).
infixl 6 _+‚Ąē_

_+‚Ąē_ : ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ąē
zero +‚Ąē n = n
suc m +‚Ąē n = suc (m +‚Ąē n)

-- ¬ß Integer type: signed normal form.
data ‚Ą§ : Set where
  0‚Ą§    : ‚Ą§
  +suc_ : ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ą§
  -suc_ : ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ą§

-- ¬ß Normalization: cancel common successors.
normalize‚Ą§ : ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ą§
normalize‚Ą§ zero zero = 0‚Ą§
normalize‚Ą§ (suc a) zero = +suc a
normalize‚Ą§ zero (suc b) = -suc b
normalize‚Ą§ (suc a) (suc b) = normalize‚Ą§ a b

-- ¬ß Pair representation for componentwise arithmetic.
record Pair‚Ąē : Set where
  constructor mkPair‚Ąē
  field
    pos : ‚Ąē
    neg : ‚Ąē

open Pair‚Ąē public

-- ¬ß Embedding ‚Ą§ into pairs.
toPair‚Ą§ : ‚Ą§ ‚Üí Pair‚Ąē
toPair‚Ą§ 0‚Ą§ = mkPair‚Ąē zero zero
toPair‚Ą§ (+suc n) = mkPair‚Ąē (suc n) zero
toPair‚Ą§ (-suc n) = mkPair‚Ąē zero (suc n)

-- ¬ß Collapsing pairs to ‚Ą§.
fromPair‚Ą§ : Pair‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ą§
fromPair‚Ą§ p = normalize‚Ą§ (pos p) (neg p)

-- ¬ß Integer addition via pair-level componentwise sum.
infixl 6 _+‚Ą§_

_+‚Ą§_ : ‚Ą§ ‚Üí ‚Ą§ ‚Üí ‚Ą§
x +‚Ą§ y =
  let px = toPair‚Ą§ x in
  let py = toPair‚Ą§ y in
  normalize‚Ą§ (pos px +‚Ąē pos py) (neg px +‚Ąē neg py)

-- ¬ß Integer negation via pair swap.
neg‚Ą§ : ‚Ą§ ‚Üí ‚Ą§
neg‚Ą§ z =
  let p = toPair‚Ą§ z in
  normalize‚Ą§ (neg p) (pos p)
\end{code}

\section{Endomorphism Iteration}
\label{sec:arithmetic-infrastructure:endomorphism-iteration}

A generic endomorphism on any set can be iterated $n$ times; this is the minimal
structure forced by the need to express $n$-fold drift composition.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Generic endomorphism type.
GenEndo : Set ‚Üí Set
GenEndo A = A ‚Üí A

-- ¬ß Function composition.
infixr 9 _‚ąė_
_‚ąė_ : {A B C : Set} ‚Üí (B ‚Üí C) ‚Üí (A ‚Üí B) ‚Üí A ‚Üí C
(f ‚ąė g) x = f (g x)

-- ¬ß Identity endomorphism.
idGenEndo : {A : Set} ‚Üí GenEndo A
idGenEndo x = x

-- ¬ß n-fold iteration.
powEndo : {A : Set} ‚Üí ‚Ąē ‚Üí GenEndo A ‚Üí GenEndo A
powEndo zero    f = idGenEndo
powEndo (suc n) f = f ‚ąė powEndo n f

-- ¬ß Law 14I.0: Zero iteration is identity.
law14I-0-powEndo-zero : {A : Set} ‚Üí (f : GenEndo A) ‚Üí powEndo zero f ‚Čó idGenEndo
law14I-0-powEndo-zero f x = refl

-- ¬ß Law 14I.1: Successor iteration unfolds.
law14I-1-powEndo-suc : {A : Set} ‚Üí (n : ‚Ąē) ‚Üí (f : GenEndo A) ‚Üí powEndo (suc n) f ‚Čó (f ‚ąė powEndo n f)
law14I-1-powEndo-suc n f x = refl
\end{code}

\section{Integer Summation and Absolute Value}
\label{sec:arithmetic-infrastructure:integer-summation-absolute-value}

Finite sums over three- and four-element index sets, scalar multiples of integers,
and absolute value are forced as the combinatorial operations required by Laplacian
evaluation over K$_4$.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Three-element sum.
sum3‚Ą§ : ‚Ą§ ‚Üí ‚Ą§ ‚Üí ‚Ą§ ‚Üí ‚Ą§
sum3‚Ą§ a b c = a +‚Ą§ (b +‚Ą§ c)

-- ¬ß Sum indexed by Fin3.
sumFin3‚Ą§ : (Fin3 ‚Üí ‚Ą§) ‚Üí ‚Ą§
sumFin3‚Ą§ f = sum3‚Ą§ (f f0) (f f1) (f f2)

-- ¬ß Four-element sum.
sum4‚Ą§ : ‚Ą§ ‚Üí ‚Ą§ ‚Üí ‚Ą§ ‚Üí ‚Ą§ ‚Üí ‚Ą§
sum4‚Ą§ a b c d = a +‚Ą§ (b +‚Ą§ (c +‚Ą§ d))

-- ¬ß Sum indexed by Fin4.
sumFin4‚Ą§ : (Fin4 ‚Üí ‚Ą§) ‚Üí ‚Ą§
sumFin4‚Ą§ f = sum4‚Ą§ (f g0) (f g1) (f g2) (f g3)

-- ¬ß Scalar triple.
threeTimes‚Ą§ : ‚Ą§ ‚Üí ‚Ą§
threeTimes‚Ą§ x = x +‚Ą§ (x +‚Ą§ x)

-- ¬ß Scalar quadruple.
fourTimes‚Ą§ : ‚Ą§ ‚Üí ‚Ą§
fourTimes‚Ą§ x = sum4‚Ą§ x x x x

-- ¬ß Absolute value on ‚Ą§.
abs‚Ą§ : ‚Ą§ ‚Üí ‚Ą§
abs‚Ą§ 0‚Ą§ = 0‚Ą§
abs‚Ą§ (+suc n) = +suc n
abs‚Ą§ (-suc n) = +suc n
\end{code}

\section{Integer Order}
\label{sec:arithmetic-infrastructure:integer-order}

The order $\le_{\mathbb{Z}}$ is forced by the three-constructor representation:
negative integers are below zero, zero is below positive integers, and within each
sign class the order reduces to $\le$ on the underlying natural number.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Integer ordering.
infix 4 _‚Č§‚Ą§_ _<‚Ą§_

_‚Č§‚Ą§_ : ‚Ą§ ‚Üí ‚Ą§ ‚Üí Set
0‚Ą§ ‚Č§‚Ą§ 0‚Ą§ = ‚ä§
0‚Ą§ ‚Č§‚Ą§ (+suc n) = ‚ä§
0‚Ą§ ‚Č§‚Ą§ (-suc n) = ‚ä•
(+suc m) ‚Č§‚Ą§ 0‚Ą§ = ‚ä•
(+suc m) ‚Č§‚Ą§ (+suc n) = suc m ‚Č§ suc n
(+suc m) ‚Č§‚Ą§ (-suc n) = ‚ä•
(-suc m) ‚Č§‚Ą§ 0‚Ą§ = ‚ä§
(-suc m) ‚Č§‚Ą§ (+suc n) = ‚ä§
(-suc m) ‚Č§‚Ą§ (-suc n) = suc n ‚Č§ suc m

-- ¬ß Non-ordering witness.
_‚Čį‚Ą§_ : ‚Ą§ ‚Üí ‚Ą§ ‚Üí Set
x ‚Čį‚Ą§ y = (x ‚Č§‚Ą§ y) ‚Üí ‚ä•

-- ¬ß Strict integer ordering.
_<‚Ą§_ : ‚Ą§ ‚Üí ‚Ą§ ‚Üí Set
_<‚Ą§_ x y = (x ‚Č§‚Ą§ y) √ó (y ‚Čį‚Ą§ x)
\end{code}

\section{K\texorpdfstring{$_4$}{4} Vertex Indexing}
\label{sec:arithmetic-infrastructure:k4-vertex-indexing}

The bijection between \texttt{EndoCase} (the four K$_4$ vertices classified in
Part~I) and \texttt{Fin4} is forced by exhaustive pattern matching on both types.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Fin4 ‚Üí EndoCase bijection.
vertexAt : Fin4 ‚Üí EndoCase
vertexAt g0 = case-constL
vertexAt g1 = case-constR
vertexAt g2 = case-id
vertexAt g3 = case-dual

-- ¬ß EndoCase ‚Üí Fin4 inverse.
vertexIndex : EndoCase ‚Üí Fin4
vertexIndex case-constL = g0
vertexIndex case-constR = g1
vertexIndex case-id     = g2
vertexIndex case-dual   = g3

-- ¬ß Round-trip: vertexAt ‚ąė vertexIndex = id.
vertexAt-index : (v : EndoCase) ‚Üí vertexAt (vertexIndex v) ‚Č° v
vertexAt-index case-constL = refl
vertexAt-index case-constR = refl
vertexAt-index case-id     = refl
vertexAt-index case-dual   = refl

-- ¬ß Round-trip: vertexIndex ‚ąė vertexAt = id.
index-vertexAt : (i : Fin4) ‚Üí vertexIndex (vertexAt i) ‚Č° i
index-vertexAt g0 = refl
index-vertexAt g1 = refl
index-vertexAt g2 = refl
index-vertexAt g3 = refl
\end{code}

\section{Endomorphism Systems}
\label{sec:arithmetic-infrastructure:endomorphism-systems}

An endomorphism system bundles a setoid with a step function that respects the setoid
relation. Morphisms between endomorphism systems commute with their respective step
functions. Terminal and initial objects are forced by the usual universal property.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Setoid: carrier with equivalence relation.
record ESetoid : Set1 where
  field
    Obj    : Set
    Rel    : Obj ‚Üí Obj ‚Üí Set
    refl‚Čą  : (x : Obj) ‚Üí Rel x x
    sym‚Čą   : {x y : Obj} ‚Üí Rel x y ‚Üí Rel y x
    trans‚Čą : {x y z : Obj} ‚Üí Rel x y ‚Üí Rel y z ‚Üí Rel x z

open ESetoid public

-- ¬ß Endomorphism system: setoid + step.
record EndoSystem : Set1 where
  field
    ES    : ESetoid
    step : Obj ES ‚Üí Obj ES
    step-cong : {x y : Obj ES} ‚Üí Rel ES x y ‚Üí Rel ES (step x) (step y)

open EndoSystem public

-- ¬ß Morphism of endomorphism systems.
record ESHom (X Y : EndoSystem) : Set1 where
  field
    esmap  : Obj (ES X) ‚Üí Obj (ES Y)
    esmap-cong : {x y : Obj (ES X)} ‚Üí Rel (ES X) x y ‚Üí Rel (ES Y) (esmap x) (esmap y)
    escomm : (x : Obj (ES X)) ‚Üí Rel (ES Y) (esmap (step X x)) (step Y (esmap x))

open ESHom public

-- ¬ß Terminal endomorphism system.
record IsTerminal (T : EndoSystem) : Set1 where
  field
    !    : (X : EndoSystem) ‚Üí ESHom X T
    uniq : (X : EndoSystem) ‚Üí (f g : ESHom X T) ‚Üí (x : Obj (ES X)) ‚Üí Rel (ES T) (esmap f x) (esmap g x)

-- ¬ß Initial endomorphism system.
record IsInitial (I : EndoSystem) : Set1 where
  field
    !    : (X : EndoSystem) ‚Üí ESHom I X
    uniq : (X : EndoSystem) ‚Üí (f g : ESHom I X) ‚Üí (x : Obj (ES I)) ‚Üí Rel (ES X) (esmap f x) (esmap g x)

-- ¬ß Law 14J.0: Morphisms commute with n-fold iteration.
law14J-0-hom-iter :
  {X Y : EndoSystem} ‚Üí
  (h : ESHom X Y) ‚Üí
  (n : ‚Ąē) ‚Üí
  (x : Obj (ES X)) ‚Üí
  Rel (ES Y) (esmap h (powEndo n (step X) x)) (powEndo n (step Y) (esmap h x))
law14J-0-hom-iter {X} {Y} h zero x = refl‚Čą (ES Y) (esmap h x)
law14J-0-hom-iter {X} {Y} h (suc n) x =
  trans‚Čą (ES Y)
    (escomm h (powEndo n (step X) x))
    (step-cong Y (law14J-0-hom-iter h n x))
\end{code}

\section{K\texorpdfstring{$_4$}{4} Coupling Infrastructure}
\label{sec:arithmetic-infrastructure:k4-coupling-infrastructure}

The coupling infrastructure provides the combinatorial toolkit for relating two copies of
K$_4$: decidable equality on \texttt{EndoCase}, transpositions (\texttt{swapEndo}), permutations
(\texttt{EndoPerm}), and cross-invariant couplings. The $64$-case table for \texttt{swapEndo}
and its involutivity proof are forced by exhaustive case analysis on three \texttt{EndoCase}
arguments.

\paragraph{Step 1: EndoCase inequality and decidable equality.}
Inequality witnesses and decidable equality on the four-element label set are forced by constructor discrimination.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Inequality on EndoCase.
EndoCase‚Č† : (a b : EndoCase) ‚Üí Set
EndoCase‚Č† a b = a ‚Č° b ‚Üí ‚ä•

case-constL‚Č†case-constR : EndoCase‚Č† case-constL case-constR
case-constL‚Č†case-constR ()

case-constL‚Č†case-id : EndoCase‚Č† case-constL case-id
case-constL‚Č†case-id ()

case-constL‚Č†case-dual : EndoCase‚Č† case-constL case-dual
case-constL‚Č†case-dual ()

case-constR‚Č†case-id : EndoCase‚Č† case-constR case-id
case-constR‚Č†case-id ()

case-constR‚Č†case-dual : EndoCase‚Č† case-constR case-dual
case-constR‚Č†case-dual ()

case-id‚Č†case-dual : EndoCase‚Č† case-id case-dual
case-id‚Č†case-dual ()

-- ¬ß Decidable equality on EndoCase.
EndoCase-decEq : (a b : EndoCase) ‚Üí (a ‚Č° b) ‚äé (EndoCase‚Č† a b)
EndoCase-decEq case-constL case-constL = inj‚āĀ refl
EndoCase-decEq case-constR case-constR = inj‚āĀ refl
EndoCase-decEq case-id     case-id     = inj‚āĀ refl
EndoCase-decEq case-dual   case-dual   = inj‚āĀ refl
EndoCase-decEq case-constL case-constR = inj‚āā case-constL‚Č†case-constR
EndoCase-decEq case-constR case-constL = inj‚āā (őĽ e ‚Üí case-constL‚Č†case-constR (sym e))
EndoCase-decEq case-constL case-id     = inj‚āā case-constL‚Č†case-id
EndoCase-decEq case-id     case-constL = inj‚āā (őĽ e ‚Üí case-constL‚Č†case-id (sym e))
EndoCase-decEq case-constL case-dual   = inj‚āā case-constL‚Č†case-dual
EndoCase-decEq case-dual   case-constL = inj‚āā (őĽ e ‚Üí case-constL‚Č†case-dual (sym e))
EndoCase-decEq case-constR case-id     = inj‚āā case-constR‚Č†case-id
EndoCase-decEq case-id     case-constR = inj‚āā (őĽ e ‚Üí case-constR‚Č†case-id (sym e))
EndoCase-decEq case-constR case-dual   = inj‚āā case-constR‚Č†case-dual
EndoCase-decEq case-dual   case-constR = inj‚āā (őĽ e ‚Üí case-constR‚Č†case-dual (sym e))
EndoCase-decEq case-id     case-dual   = inj‚āā case-id‚Č†case-dual
EndoCase-decEq case-dual   case-id     = inj‚āā (őĽ e ‚Üí case-id‚Č†case-dual (sym e))
\end{code}

\paragraph{Step 2: Transposition on EndoCase.}
The transposition \texttt{swapEndo x y} exchanges \texttt{x} with \texttt{y} and fixes all other labels.
The definition and its involutivity proof are forced by exhaustive $4 \times 4 \times 4 = 64$ case analysis.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Transposition on EndoCase: swaps x‚ÜĒy, fixes others.
swapEndo : EndoCase ‚Üí EndoCase ‚Üí EndoCase ‚Üí EndoCase
swapEndo case-constL case-constL z = z
swapEndo case-constL case-constR case-constL = case-constR
swapEndo case-constL case-constR case-constR = case-constL
swapEndo case-constL case-constR case-id     = case-id
swapEndo case-constL case-constR case-dual   = case-dual
swapEndo case-constL case-id     case-constL = case-id
swapEndo case-constL case-id     case-constR = case-constR
swapEndo case-constL case-id     case-id     = case-constL
swapEndo case-constL case-id     case-dual   = case-dual
swapEndo case-constL case-dual   case-constL = case-dual
swapEndo case-constL case-dual   case-constR = case-constR
swapEndo case-constL case-dual   case-id     = case-id
swapEndo case-constL case-dual   case-dual   = case-constL
swapEndo case-constR case-constL case-constL = case-constR
swapEndo case-constR case-constL case-constR = case-constL
swapEndo case-constR case-constL case-id     = case-id
swapEndo case-constR case-constL case-dual   = case-dual
swapEndo case-constR case-constR z = z
swapEndo case-constR case-id     case-constL = case-constL
swapEndo case-constR case-id     case-constR = case-id
swapEndo case-constR case-id     case-id     = case-constR
swapEndo case-constR case-id     case-dual   = case-dual
swapEndo case-constR case-dual   case-constL = case-constL
swapEndo case-constR case-dual   case-constR = case-dual
swapEndo case-constR case-dual   case-id     = case-id
swapEndo case-constR case-dual   case-dual   = case-constR
swapEndo case-id case-constL case-constL = case-id
swapEndo case-id case-constL case-constR = case-constR
swapEndo case-id case-constL case-id     = case-constL
swapEndo case-id case-constL case-dual   = case-dual
swapEndo case-id case-constR case-constL = case-constL
swapEndo case-id case-constR case-constR = case-id
swapEndo case-id case-constR case-id     = case-constR
swapEndo case-id case-constR case-dual   = case-dual
swapEndo case-id case-id z = z
swapEndo case-id case-dual case-constL = case-constL
swapEndo case-id case-dual case-constR = case-constR
swapEndo case-id case-dual case-id     = case-dual
swapEndo case-id case-dual case-dual   = case-id
swapEndo case-dual case-constL case-constL = case-dual
swapEndo case-dual case-constL case-constR = case-constR
swapEndo case-dual case-constL case-id     = case-id
swapEndo case-dual case-constL case-dual   = case-constL
swapEndo case-dual case-constR case-constL = case-constL
swapEndo case-dual case-constR case-constR = case-dual
swapEndo case-dual case-constR case-id     = case-id
swapEndo case-dual case-constR case-dual   = case-constR
swapEndo case-dual case-id     case-constL = case-constL
swapEndo case-dual case-id     case-constR = case-constR
swapEndo case-dual case-id     case-id     = case-dual
swapEndo case-dual case-id     case-dual   = case-id
swapEndo case-dual case-dual z = z

-- ¬ß swapEndo is an involution (64-case proof).
swapEndo-involutive : (x y z : EndoCase) ‚Üí swapEndo x y (swapEndo x y z) ‚Č° z
swapEndo-involutive case-constL case-constL z = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constL case-constR case-constL = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constL case-constR case-constR = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constL case-constR case-id     = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constL case-constR case-dual   = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constL case-id     case-constL = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constL case-id     case-constR = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constL case-id     case-id     = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constL case-id     case-dual   = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constL case-dual   case-constL = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constL case-dual   case-constR = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constL case-dual   case-id     = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constL case-dual   case-dual   = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constR case-constL case-constL = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constR case-constL case-constR = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constR case-constL case-id     = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constR case-constL case-dual   = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constR case-constR z = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constR case-id     case-constL = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constR case-id     case-constR = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constR case-id     case-id     = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constR case-id     case-dual   = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constR case-dual   case-constL = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constR case-dual   case-constR = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constR case-dual   case-id     = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constR case-dual   case-dual   = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-id case-constL case-constL = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-id case-constL case-constR = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-id case-constL case-id     = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-id case-constL case-dual   = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-id case-constR case-constL = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-id case-constR case-constR = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-id case-constR case-id     = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-id case-constR case-dual   = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-id case-id z = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-id case-dual case-constL = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-id case-dual case-constR = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-id case-dual case-id     = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-id case-dual case-dual   = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-dual case-constL case-constL = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-dual case-constL case-constR = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-dual case-constL case-id     = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-dual case-constL case-dual   = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-dual case-constR case-constL = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-dual case-constR case-constR = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-dual case-constR case-id     = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-dual case-constR case-dual   = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-dual case-id     case-constL = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-dual case-id     case-constR = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-dual case-id     case-id     = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-dual case-id     case-dual   = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-dual case-dual z = refl
\end{code}

\paragraph{Step 3: Transposition sends source to target.}
The boundary behavior of \texttt{swapEndo} is verified: it sends \texttt{x} to \texttt{y} and \texttt{y} back to \texttt{x}, by exhaustive $4 \times 4$ case analysis.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß swapEndo sends x to y.
swapEndo-sends : (x y : EndoCase) ‚Üí swapEndo x y x ‚Č° y
swapEndo-sends case-constL case-constL = refl
swapEndo-sends case-constL case-constR = refl
swapEndo-sends case-constL case-id     = refl
swapEndo-sends case-constL case-dual   = refl
swapEndo-sends case-constR case-constL = refl
swapEndo-sends case-constR case-constR = refl
swapEndo-sends case-constR case-id     = refl
swapEndo-sends case-constR case-dual   = refl
swapEndo-sends case-id     case-constL = refl
swapEndo-sends case-id     case-constR = refl
swapEndo-sends case-id     case-id     = refl
swapEndo-sends case-id     case-dual   = refl
swapEndo-sends case-dual   case-constL = refl
swapEndo-sends case-dual   case-constR = refl
swapEndo-sends case-dual   case-id     = refl
swapEndo-sends case-dual   case-dual   = refl
\end{code}

\paragraph{Step 4: Permutations, couplings, and cross-invariance.}
A permutation on \texttt{EndoCase} is a bijection \texttt{(eto, efrom)} with round-trip proofs.
A coupling relates two copies of \texttt{EndoCase}; cross-invariance requires the coupling to be preserved under independent permutations on each component.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Permutation record on EndoCase.
record EndoPerm : Set where
  field
    eto       : EndoCase ‚Üí EndoCase
    efrom     : EndoCase ‚Üí EndoCase
    eto-efrom : (y : EndoCase) ‚Üí eto (efrom y) ‚Č° y
    efrom-eto : (x : EndoCase) ‚Üí efrom (eto x) ‚Č° x

open EndoPerm public

-- ¬ß Transposition as permutation.
permSwap : (x y : EndoCase) ‚Üí EndoPerm
permSwap x y = record
  { eto = swapEndo x y
  ; efrom = swapEndo x y
  ; eto-efrom = swapEndo-involutive x y
  ; efrom-eto = swapEndo-involutive x y
  }

-- ¬ß Any vertex can be sent to any other by some permutation.
endoPerm-send : (a a' : EndoCase) ‚Üí ő£ EndoPerm (őĽ ŌÉ ‚Üí eto ŌÉ a ‚Č° a')
endoPerm-send a a' = (permSwap a a' , swapEndo-sends a a')

-- ¬ß Coupling: relation between two copies of EndoCase.
Coupling : Set1
Coupling = EndoCase ‚Üí EndoCase ‚Üí Set

-- ¬ß Cross-invariance: coupling respected by independent permutations.
CrossInv : Coupling ‚Üí Set
CrossInv C = (ŌÉ ŌĄ : EndoPerm) ‚Üí (a b : EndoCase) ‚Üí C a b ‚Üí C (eto ŌÉ a) (eto ŌĄ b)

-- ¬ß Heterogeneous transport over a two-argument predicate.
transport2 : {A B : Set} {P : A ‚Üí B ‚Üí Set} {a a' : A} {b b' : B} ‚Üí a ‚Č° a' ‚Üí b ‚Č° b' ‚Üí P a b ‚Üí P a' b'
transport2 {P = P} {a = a} {a' = a'} {b = b} {b' = b'} ea eb pab =
  subst (őĽ a0 ‚Üí P a0 b') ea (subst (őĽ b0 ‚Üí P a b0) eb pab)
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 14F.0: A Single Cross-Invariant Edge Forces All Edges}
\label{law:coupling:edge-forces-all}

If a coupling is cross-invariant and has at least one witness, then every pair is coupled:
the permutation group acts transitively on $\mathrm{EndoCase} \times \mathrm{EndoCase}$.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 14F.0: One edge forces all.
law14F-0-edge-forces-all : (C : Coupling) ‚Üí CrossInv C ‚Üí
  ő£ EndoCase (őĽ a0 ‚Üí ő£ EndoCase (őĽ b0 ‚Üí C a0 b0)) ‚Üí
  (a b : EndoCase) ‚Üí C a b
law14F-0-edge-forces-all C inv (a0 , (b0 , c0)) a b =
  let sa = endoPerm-send a0 a in
  let sb = endoPerm-send b0 b in
  let ŌÉ = fst sa in
  let ŌĄ = fst sb in
  transport2 {P = C} (snd sa) (snd sb) (inv ŌÉ ŌĄ a0 b0 c0)

-- ¬ß Law 14F.1: One missing edge forces no edges.
law14F-1-nonedge-forces-none : (C : Coupling) ‚Üí CrossInv C ‚Üí
  ő£ EndoCase (őĽ a0 ‚Üí ő£ EndoCase (őĽ b0 ‚Üí ¬¨ (C a0 b0))) ‚Üí
  (a b : EndoCase) ‚Üí ¬¨ (C a b)
law14F-1-nonedge-forces-none C inv (a0 , (b0 , n0)) a b cab =
  let sa = endoPerm-send a a0 in
  let sb = endoPerm-send b b0 in
  let ŌÉ = fst sa in
  let ŌĄ = fst sb in
  n0 (transport2 {P = C} (snd sa) (snd sb) (inv ŌÉ ŌĄ a b cab))
\end{code}

\section{K\texorpdfstring{$_4$}{4} Product Graphs}
\label{sec:arithmetic-infrastructure:k4-product-graphs}

Two copies of K$_4$ can be coupled by a cross-relation to form an $8$-vertex product
graph. The edge relation is intra-copy (within the same K$_4$) or inter-copy (via the
coupling).

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Inequality on Two.
Two‚Č† : (i j : Two) ‚Üí Set
Two‚Č† i j = i ‚Č° j ‚Üí ‚ä•

L‚Č†R : Two‚Č† L R
L‚Č†R ()

-- ¬ß Decidable equality on Two.
Two-decEq : (i j : Two) ‚Üí (i ‚Č° j) ‚äé (Two‚Č† i j)
Two-decEq L L = inj‚āĀ refl
Two-decEq R R = inj‚āĀ refl
Two-decEq L R = inj‚āā L‚Č†R
Two-decEq R L = inj‚āā (őĽ e ‚Üí L‚Č†R (sym e))

-- ¬ß Product edge relation.
Edge2 : Coupling ‚Üí (Two √ó EndoCase) ‚Üí (Two √ó EndoCase) ‚Üí Set
Edge2 C (L , a) (L , b) = a ‚Č† b
Edge2 C (R , a) (R , b) = a ‚Č† b
Edge2 C (L , a) (R , b) = C a b
Edge2 C (R , a) (L , b) = C b a

-- ¬ß Product edge symmetry.
edge2-sym : (C : Coupling) ‚Üí {x y : Two √ó EndoCase} ‚Üí Edge2 C x y ‚Üí Edge2 C y x
edge2-sym C {x = (L , a)} {y = (L , b)} e = őĽ eq ‚Üí e (sym eq)
edge2-sym C {x = (R , a)} {y = (R , b)} e = őĽ eq ‚Üí e (sym eq)
edge2-sym C {x = (L , a)} {y = (R , b)} e = e
edge2-sym C {x = (R , a)} {y = (L , b)} e = e

-- ¬ß Product edge irreflexivity.
edge2-irr : (C : Coupling) ‚Üí (x : Two √ó EndoCase) ‚Üí Edge2 C x x ‚Üí ‚ä•
edge2-irr C (L , a) e = e refl
edge2-irr C (R , a) e = e refl

-- ¬ß K‚āĄ √ó 2 graph from coupling.
K4√ó2 : Coupling ‚Üí Graph
K4√ó2 C = record
  { V = Two √ó EndoCase
  ; Edge = Edge2 C
  ; edge-sym = őĽ {a} {b} e ‚Üí edge2-sym C {x = a} {y = b} e
  ; edge-irr = edge2-irr C
  }

-- ¬ß Empty coupling (no cross-edges).
CrossEmpty : Coupling
CrossEmpty _ _ = ‚ä•

-- ¬ß Full coupling (all cross-edges).
CrossFull : Coupling
CrossFull _ _ = ‚ä§
\end{code}

\paragraph{Where we now stand.}
The raw arithmetic substrate is assembled. Finite indices, integers, iteration, couplings, and transport across the surviving K$_4$ cases are now available in exactly the form the graph will require. The document can now stop merely naming the surviving structures and begin imposing stable algebra on them.

\paragraph{What must happen next.}
Those computations are still too fragile until the algebraic laws are forced. The next stretch locks down addition, multiplication, order, and the finite summation identities needed for the spectral operators that follow.

\chapter{Forced Additive Laws}
\label{ch:forced-additive-laws}

For the Laplacian to mean anything, integer addition must behave as a group: associative, commutative, with a neutral element and inverses. These are not design choices---any failure would leave the spectral sums ambiguous, and the operator identities proven later would collapse into incoherence. Each law is forced by induction on $\mathbb{N}$ and structural recursion on the \texttt{normalize‚Ą§} definition. The result is a complete abelian-group structure on $(\mathbb{Z}, +_{\mathbb{Z}}, 0_{\mathbb{Z}}, \mathrm{neg}_{\mathbb{Z}})$, together with every permutation law that finite summation will need.

\section{Natural Addition Laws}
\label{sec:additive-laws:natural-addition}

Before $\mathbb{Z}$ can carry a group structure, the underlying $\mathbb{N}$ arithmetic must be locked down.
Diagonal normalization ($\texttt{normalize‚Ą§}\;n\;n = 0_{\mathbb{Z}}$),
right identity, successor shifting, associativity, and commutativity of $+_{\mathbb{N}}$ are each forced by structural recursion---they leave no parameter free and no alternative definition standing.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Diagonal normalization collapses to zero.
normalizeDiag0‚Ą§ : (n : ‚Ąē) ‚Üí normalize‚Ą§ n n ‚Č° 0‚Ą§
normalizeDiag0‚Ą§ zero = refl
normalizeDiag0‚Ą§ (suc n) = normalizeDiag0‚Ą§ n

-- ¬ß Right identity for +‚Ąē.
+‚Ąē-zero-right : (n : ‚Ąē) ‚Üí n +‚Ąē zero ‚Č° n
+‚Ąē-zero-right zero = refl
+‚Ąē-zero-right (suc n) = cong suc (+‚Ąē-zero-right n)

-- ¬ß Successor shifts right.
+‚Ąē-suc-right : (n m : ‚Ąē) ‚Üí n +‚Ąē suc m ‚Č° suc (n +‚Ąē m)
+‚Ąē-suc-right zero m = refl
+‚Ąē-suc-right (suc n) m = cong suc (+‚Ąē-suc-right n m)

-- ¬ß Associativity of +‚Ąē.
+‚Ąē-assoc : (a b c : ‚Ąē) ‚Üí (a +‚Ąē b) +‚Ąē c ‚Č° a +‚Ąē (b +‚Ąē c)
+‚Ąē-assoc zero b c = refl
+‚Ąē-assoc (suc a) b c = cong suc (+‚Ąē-assoc a b c)

-- ¬ß Commutativity of +‚Ąē.
+‚Ąē-comm : (a b : ‚Ąē) ‚Üí a +‚Ąē b ‚Č° b +‚Ąē a
+‚Ąē-comm zero b = sym (+‚Ąē-zero-right b)
+‚Ąē-comm (suc a) b =
  trans
    refl
    (trans
      (cong suc (+‚Ąē-comm a b))
      (sym (+‚Ąē-suc-right b a)))
\end{code}

\section{Integer Addition Laws}
\label{sec:additive-laws:integer-addition}
Integer addition is defined via pair normalization: $a +_{\mathbb{Z}} b$ adds the positive and negative components pairwise and re-normalizes.
Congruence for \texttt{normalize‚Ą§}, absorption of pair-level addition,
associativity, commutativity, identity, inverse, and cancellation all follow by structural induction on the pair representation.
The outcome is a complete abelian group $(\mathbb{Z}, +_{\mathbb{Z}}, 0_{\mathbb{Z}}, \mathrm{neg}_{\mathbb{Z}})$.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Congruence for normalize‚Ą§.
normalize‚Ą§-cong : {a a' b b' : ‚Ąē} ‚Üí a ‚Č° a' ‚Üí b ‚Č° b' ‚Üí normalize‚Ą§ a b ‚Č° normalize‚Ą§ a' b'
normalize‚Ą§-cong {a} {a'} {b} {b'} pa pb = trans (cong (őĽ t ‚Üí normalize‚Ą§ t b) pa) (cong (normalize‚Ą§ a') pb)

-- ¬ß Normalization absorbs pair-level addition.
normalize-plusRight : (a b c d : ‚Ąē) ‚Üí
  normalize‚Ą§ (pos (toPair‚Ą§ (normalize‚Ą§ a b)) +‚Ąē c) (neg (toPair‚Ą§ (normalize‚Ą§ a b)) +‚Ąē d)
    ‚Č°
  normalize‚Ą§ (a +‚Ąē c) (b +‚Ąē d)
normalize-plusRight zero zero c d = refl
normalize-plusRight (suc a) zero c d = refl
normalize-plusRight zero (suc b) c d = refl
normalize-plusRight (suc a) (suc b) c d = normalize-plusRight a b c d

-- ¬ß Commutativity of +‚Ą§.
+‚Ą§-comm : (x y : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí x +‚Ą§ y ‚Č° y +‚Ą§ x
+‚Ą§-comm x y with toPair‚Ą§ x | toPair‚Ą§ y
... | px | py =
  normalize‚Ą§-cong (+‚Ąē-comm (pos px) (pos py)) (+‚Ąē-comm (neg px) (neg py))

-- ¬ß Associativity of +‚Ą§.
+‚Ą§-assoc : (x y z : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí (x +‚Ą§ y) +‚Ą§ z ‚Č° x +‚Ą§ (y +‚Ą§ z)
+‚Ą§-assoc x y z with toPair‚Ą§ x | toPair‚Ą§ y | toPair‚Ą§ z
... | px | py | pz =
  let ax = pos px in
  let bx = neg px in
  let ay = pos py in
  let by = neg py in
  let az = pos pz in
  let bz = neg pz in
  let Axy = ax +‚Ąē ay in
  let Bxy = bx +‚Ąē by in
  let Ayz = ay +‚Ąē az in
  let Byz = by +‚Ąē bz in

  let lhs‚āÄ = normalize‚Ą§ (pos (toPair‚Ą§ (normalize‚Ą§ Axy Bxy)) +‚Ąē az)
                       (neg (toPair‚Ą§ (normalize‚Ą§ Axy Bxy)) +‚Ąē bz) in
  let lhs‚āĀ = normalize‚Ą§ (Axy +‚Ąē az) (Bxy +‚Ąē bz) in
  let rhs‚āÄ = normalize‚Ą§ (ax +‚Ąē pos (toPair‚Ą§ (normalize‚Ą§ Ayz Byz)))
                       (bx +‚Ąē neg (toPair‚Ą§ (normalize‚Ą§ Ayz Byz))) in
  let rhs‚āĀ = normalize‚Ą§ (pos (toPair‚Ą§ (normalize‚Ą§ Ayz Byz)) +‚Ąē ax)
                       (neg (toPair‚Ą§ (normalize‚Ą§ Ayz Byz)) +‚Ąē bx) in
  let rhs‚āā = normalize‚Ą§ (Ayz +‚Ąē ax) (Byz +‚Ąē bx) in
  let rhs‚āÉ = normalize‚Ą§ (ax +‚Ąē Ayz) (bx +‚Ąē Byz) in

  trans
    (trans
      (cong (őĽ u ‚Üí u) (normalize-plusRight Axy Bxy az bz))
      (normalize‚Ą§-cong (+‚Ąē-assoc ax ay az) (+‚Ąē-assoc bx by bz)))
    (sym
      (trans
        (trans
          (normalize‚Ą§-cong (+‚Ąē-comm ax (pos (toPair‚Ą§ (normalize‚Ą§ Ayz Byz))))
                           (+‚Ąē-comm bx (neg (toPair‚Ą§ (normalize‚Ą§ Ayz Byz)))))
          (normalize-plusRight Ayz Byz ax bx))
        (normalize‚Ą§-cong (+‚Ąē-comm Ayz ax) (+‚Ąē-comm Byz bx))))

-- ¬ß Left identity for +‚Ą§.
+‚Ą§-zero-left : (x : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí 0‚Ą§ +‚Ą§ x ‚Č° x
+‚Ą§-zero-left 0‚Ą§ = refl
+‚Ą§-zero-left (+suc n) = refl
+‚Ą§-zero-left (-suc n) = refl

-- ¬ß Right identity for +‚Ą§.
+‚Ą§-zero-right : (x : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí x +‚Ą§ 0‚Ą§ ‚Č° x
+‚Ą§-zero-right x = trans (+‚Ą§-comm x 0‚Ą§) (+‚Ą§-zero-left x)

-- ¬ß Right inverse.
+‚Ą§-inv-right : (x : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí x +‚Ą§ neg‚Ą§ x ‚Č° 0‚Ą§
+‚Ą§-inv-right 0‚Ą§ = refl
+‚Ą§-inv-right (+suc n) =
  trans
    (cong (őĽ a ‚Üí normalize‚Ą§ a (suc n)) (+‚Ąē-zero-right (suc n)))
    (normalizeDiag0‚Ą§ (suc n))
+‚Ą§-inv-right (-suc n) =
  trans
    (cong (normalize‚Ą§ (suc n)) (+‚Ąē-zero-right (suc n)))
    (normalizeDiag0‚Ą§ (suc n))

-- ¬ß Left inverse.
+‚Ą§-inv-left : (x : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí neg‚Ą§ x +‚Ą§ x ‚Č° 0‚Ą§
+‚Ą§-inv-left x = trans (+‚Ą§-comm (neg‚Ą§ x) x) (+‚Ą§-inv-right x)

-- ¬ß Negation of zero.
neg‚Ą§-zero : neg‚Ą§ 0‚Ą§ ‚Č° 0‚Ą§
neg‚Ą§-zero = refl

-- ¬ß Left cancellation.
+‚Ą§-cancel-left : (a b : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí a +‚Ą§ b ‚Č° a ‚Üí b ‚Č° 0‚Ą§
+‚Ą§-cancel-left a b eq =
  trans
    (sym (+‚Ą§-zero-left b))
    (trans
      (cong (őĽ t ‚Üí t +‚Ą§ b) (sym (+‚Ą§-inv-left a)))
      (trans
        (+‚Ą§-assoc (neg‚Ą§ a) a b)
        (trans
          (cong (őĽ t ‚Üí neg‚Ą§ a +‚Ą§ t) eq)
          (+‚Ą§-inv-left a))))

-- ¬ß Negation zero-test.
neg‚Ą§-zero‚Üízero : (z : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí neg‚Ą§ z ‚Č° 0‚Ą§ ‚Üí z ‚Č° 0‚Ą§
neg‚Ą§-zero‚Üízero 0‚Ą§ _ = refl
neg‚Ą§-zero‚Üízero (+suc n) ()
neg‚Ą§-zero‚Üízero (-suc n) ()
\end{code}

\section{Summation Permutation Laws}
\label{sec:additive-laws:summation-permutations}

Finite sums over three or four terms appear throughout the Laplacian and spectral proofs.
Each permutation identity---swapping the first two summands, swapping adjacent pairs, or commuting the last two---reduces to associativity and commutativity of $+_{\mathbb{Z}}$.
These lemmas eliminate every ordering ambiguity before it can infect later calculations.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Swap first two summands in a three-term sum.
swapHead‚Ą§ : (a b t : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí a +‚Ą§ (b +‚Ą§ t) ‚Č° b +‚Ą§ (a +‚Ą§ t)
swapHead‚Ą§ a b t =
  trans (sym (+‚Ą§-assoc a b t))
        (trans (cong (őĽ s ‚Üí s +‚Ą§ t) (+‚Ą§-comm a b))
               (+‚Ą§-assoc b a t))

sum3‚Ą§-swap01 : (a b c : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí sum3‚Ą§ a b c ‚Č° sum3‚Ą§ b a c
sum3‚Ą§-swap01 a b c = swapHead‚Ą§ a b c

sum4‚Ą§-swap01 : (a b c d : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí sum4‚Ą§ a b c d ‚Č° sum4‚Ą§ b a c d
sum4‚Ą§-swap01 a b c d = swapHead‚Ą§ a b (c +‚Ą§ d)

sum4‚Ą§-swap12 : (a b c d : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí sum4‚Ą§ a b c d ‚Č° sum4‚Ą§ a c b d
sum4‚Ą§-swap12 a b c d = cong (őĽ t ‚Üí a +‚Ą§ t) (sum3‚Ą§-swap01 b c d)

sum4‚Ą§-swap23 : (a b c d : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí sum4‚Ą§ a b c d ‚Č° sum4‚Ą§ a b d c
sum4‚Ą§-swap23 a b c d = cong (őĽ t ‚Üí a +‚Ą§ (b +‚Ą§ t)) (+‚Ą§-comm c d)
\end{code}

\section{Negation Laws}
\label{sec:additive-laws:negation}

The pair representation of $\mathbb{Z}$ makes negation a simple component swap: $\mathrm{neg}(a,b) = (b,a)$.
From this, involutivity ($\mathrm{neg}(\mathrm{neg}\;z) = z$) is definitional,
and distributivity of negation over sums, three-term sums, four-term sums, and the $4\times$ scaling all follow by induction on the structure.
Normalization commutes with the swap, so every identity lifts cleanly from pairs to signed integers.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Swap pair components.
swapPair‚Ąē : Pair‚Ąē ‚Üí Pair‚Ąē
swapPair‚Ąē p = mkPair‚Ąē (neg p) (pos p)

-- ¬ß Negation as pair swap.
toPair-neg‚Ą§ : (z : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí toPair‚Ą§ (neg‚Ą§ z) ‚Č° swapPair‚Ąē (toPair‚Ą§ z)
toPair-neg‚Ą§ 0‚Ą§ = refl
toPair-neg‚Ą§ (+suc n) = refl
toPair-neg‚Ą§ (-suc n) = refl

-- ¬ß Negation is involutive.
neg‚Ą§-involutive : (z : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí neg‚Ą§ (neg‚Ą§ z) ‚Č° z
neg‚Ą§-involutive 0‚Ą§ = refl
neg‚Ą§-involutive (+suc n) = refl
neg‚Ą§-involutive (-suc n) = refl

-- ¬ß Positive component of negation.
pos-toPair-neg‚Ą§ : (z : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí pos (toPair‚Ą§ (neg‚Ą§ z)) ‚Č° neg (toPair‚Ą§ z)
pos-toPair-neg‚Ą§ z = cong pos (toPair-neg‚Ą§ z)

-- ¬ß Negative component of negation.
neg-toPair-neg‚Ą§ : (z : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí neg (toPair‚Ą§ (neg‚Ą§ z)) ‚Č° pos (toPair‚Ą§ z)
neg-toPair-neg‚Ą§ z = cong neg (toPair-neg‚Ą§ z)

-- ¬ß Normalization commutes with negation.
neg-normalize‚Ą§ : (a b : ‚Ąē) ‚Üí neg‚Ą§ (normalize‚Ą§ a b) ‚Č° normalize‚Ą§ b a
neg-normalize‚Ą§ zero zero = refl
neg-normalize‚Ą§ (suc a) zero = refl
neg-normalize‚Ą§ zero (suc b) = refl
neg-normalize‚Ą§ (suc a) (suc b) = neg-normalize‚Ą§ a b

-- ¬ß Negation distributes over componentwise addition.
negAdd-normalizeSwap : (x y : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí
  neg‚Ą§ x +‚Ą§ neg‚Ą§ y ‚Č°
  normalize‚Ą§ (neg (toPair‚Ą§ x) +‚Ąē neg (toPair‚Ą§ y)) (pos (toPair‚Ą§ x) +‚Ąē pos (toPair‚Ą§ y))
negAdd-normalizeSwap x y =
  let A‚āĀ = pos (toPair‚Ą§ (neg‚Ą§ x)) +‚Ąē pos (toPair‚Ą§ (neg‚Ą§ y)) in
  let B‚āĀ = neg (toPair‚Ą§ (neg‚Ą§ x)) +‚Ąē neg (toPair‚Ą§ (neg‚Ą§ y)) in
  let A‚āā = neg (toPair‚Ą§ x) +‚Ąē neg (toPair‚Ą§ y) in
  let B‚āā = pos (toPair‚Ą§ x) +‚Ąē pos (toPair‚Ą§ y) in
  let eqA‚āĀ =
        trans
          (cong (őĽ t ‚Üí t +‚Ąē pos (toPair‚Ą§ (neg‚Ą§ y))) (pos-toPair-neg‚Ą§ x))
          (cong (őĽ t ‚Üí neg (toPair‚Ą§ x) +‚Ąē t) (pos-toPair-neg‚Ą§ y))
      in
  let eqB‚āĀ =
        trans
          (cong (őĽ t ‚Üí t +‚Ąē neg (toPair‚Ą§ (neg‚Ą§ y))) (neg-toPair-neg‚Ą§ x))
          (cong (őĽ t ‚Üí pos (toPair‚Ą§ x) +‚Ąē t) (neg-toPair-neg‚Ą§ y))
      in
  trans (cong (őĽ a ‚Üí normalize‚Ą§ a B‚āĀ) eqA‚āĀ)
        (cong (normalize‚Ą§ A‚āā) eqB‚āĀ)

-- ¬ß Negation distributes over +‚Ą§.
neg-+‚Ą§ : (x y : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí neg‚Ą§ (x +‚Ą§ y) ‚Č° neg‚Ą§ x +‚Ą§ neg‚Ą§ y
neg-+‚Ą§ x y =
  let A = pos (toPair‚Ą§ x) +‚Ąē pos (toPair‚Ą§ y) in
  let B = neg (toPair‚Ą§ x) +‚Ąē neg (toPair‚Ą§ y) in
  trans (neg-normalize‚Ą§ A B) (sym (negAdd-normalizeSwap x y))

-- ¬ß Negation distributes over sum3‚Ą§.
neg-sum3‚Ą§ : (a b c : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí neg‚Ą§ (sum3‚Ą§ a b c) ‚Č° sum3‚Ą§ (neg‚Ą§ a) (neg‚Ą§ b) (neg‚Ą§ c)
neg-sum3‚Ą§ a b c =
  trans (neg-+‚Ą§ a (b +‚Ą§ c))
        (cong (őĽ t ‚Üí neg‚Ą§ a +‚Ą§ t) (neg-+‚Ą§ b c))

-- ¬ß Negation distributes over sum4‚Ą§.
neg-sum4‚Ą§ : (a b c d : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí neg‚Ą§ (sum4‚Ą§ a b c d) ‚Č° sum4‚Ą§ (neg‚Ą§ a) (neg‚Ą§ b) (neg‚Ą§ c) (neg‚Ą§ d)
neg-sum4‚Ą§ a b c d =
  trans
    (neg-+‚Ą§ a (b +‚Ą§ (c +‚Ą§ d)))
    (cong (őĽ t ‚Üí neg‚Ą§ a +‚Ą§ t)
          (trans
            (neg-+‚Ą§ b (c +‚Ą§ d))
            (cong (őĽ t ‚Üí neg‚Ą§ b +‚Ą§ t) (neg-+‚Ą§ c d))))

-- ¬ß Negation distributes over fourTimes‚Ą§.
neg-fourTimes‚Ą§ : (x : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí neg‚Ą§ (fourTimes‚Ą§ x) ‚Č° fourTimes‚Ą§ (neg‚Ą§ x)
neg-fourTimes‚Ą§ x = neg-sum4‚Ą§ x x x x

-- ¬ß Negation distributes over sumFin3‚Ą§.
neg-sumFin3‚Ą§ : (f : Fin3 ‚Üí ‚Ą§) ‚Üí neg‚Ą§ (sumFin3‚Ą§ f) ‚Č° sumFin3‚Ą§ (őĽ k ‚Üí neg‚Ą§ (f k))
neg-sumFin3‚Ą§ f = neg-sum3‚Ą§ (f f0) (f f1) (f f2)
\end{code}

\chapter{Multiplicative Structure and Integer Order}
\label{ch:multiplicative-structure-integer-order}

Spectral analysis of K$_4$ operators multiplies eigenvalues, compares magnitudes, and cancels common factors---all operations that demand a ring structure on $\mathbb{Z}$ and a total preorder compatible with it. Natural multiplication is forced by recursion on the left argument; integer multiplication lifts it through the pair representation; and the preorder $\le_{\mathbb{Z}}$ inherits reflexivity, transitivity, and antisymmetry from $\le$ on $\mathbb{N}$. Together they make $(\mathbb{Z}, +_{\mathbb{Z}}, *_{\mathbb{Z}}, \le_{\mathbb{Z}})$ a commutative ordered ring---the minimal algebraic structure in which the Laplacian's spectral decomposition can be stated.

\section{Natural Multiplication}
\label{sec:multiplicative-structure:natural-multiplication}
