%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
\section{The closure record}
\label{sec:scale-closure:record}
%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ

The closure record packages the three forced constants and the three Planck unit identities into a single witness.

\begin{code}
record ScaleClosure : Set where
  field
    c-forced    : c-FD ‚Č° 1
    hbar-forced : hbar-FD ‚Č° 1
    G-forced    : G-FD ‚Č° 1
    length-1    : planck-length-FD ‚Č° 1
    time-1      : planck-time-FD ‚Č° 1
    mass-1      : planck-mass-FD ‚Č° 1

theorem-scale-closure : ScaleClosure
theorem-scale-closure = record
  { c-forced    = refl
  ; hbar-forced = refl
  ; G-forced    = refl
  ; length-1    = refl
  ; time-1      = refl
  ; mass-1      = refl
  }
\end{code}

%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
\section{No free scale}
\label{sec:scale-closure:no-free-scale}
%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ

Three independent constants saturate three independent dimensions. No free parameter survives.

\begin{code}
independent-constants : ‚Ąē
independent-constants = 3

independent-dimensions : ‚Ąē
independent-dimensions = 3

free-parameters : ‚Ąē
free-parameters = independent-dimensions ‚ął independent-constants

no-free-scale : free-parameters ‚Č° 0
no-free-scale = refl
\end{code}

%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
\section{Normalized scale elimination}
\label{sec:scale-closure:normalized-scale-elimination}
%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ

The closure claim can be sharpened one step further without appealing to decimal SI numerals. Work entirely in normalized units, where the three continuum constants are already written as $c = \hbar = G = 1$. A scale choice is then just a triple $(a,b,m)$ of natural-number unit assignments for length, time, and mass. The bridge equations become
\[
b = a,
\qquad
b = m a^2,
\qquad
mb^2 = a^3.
\]
The candidate space is still wide: any positive natural triple is allowed. The elimination shows that only the unit triple survives.

\begin{code}
IsScaleNonZero : ‚Ąē ‚Üí Set
IsScaleNonZero n = n ‚Č° 0 ‚Üí ‚ä•

record NormalizedScaleChoice : Set where
  field
    length-step    : ‚Ąē
    time-step      : ‚Ąē
    mass-step      : ‚Ąē
    length-nonzero : IsScaleNonZero length-step
    c-lock         : time-step ‚Č° length-step
    hbar-lock      : time-step ‚Č° (mass-step *‚Ąē length-step) *‚Ąē length-step
    G-lock         : (mass-step *‚Ąē time-step) *‚Ąē time-step ‚Č°
                     (length-step *‚Ąē length-step) *‚Ąē length-step
\end{code}

Two small arithmetic lemmas do the work. First: if adding a tail leaves a natural unchanged, that tail must have been zero. Second: for any value $a \ge 2$, neither $a^2 = a$ nor $a^3 = a$ can hold.

\begin{code}
natPlusRightLe : (a : ‚Ąē) ‚Üí (b : ‚Ąē) ‚Üí a ‚Č§ (a +‚Ąē b)
natPlusRightLe zero b = z‚Č§n
natPlusRightLe (suc a) b = s‚Č§s (natPlusRightLe a b)

natPlusCancelSelf : (a : ‚Ąē) ‚Üí (b : ‚Ąē) ‚Üí a +‚Ąē b ‚Č° a ‚Üí b ‚Č° 0
natPlusCancelSelf a b eq with ‚Č§-+‚Ąē-cancelň° a b 0
  (‚Č§-resp-‚Č° ≥ (subst (őĽ t ‚Üí t ‚Č§ a) (sym eq) (‚Č§-refl a)) (sym (+‚Ąē-zero-right a)))
natPlusCancelSelf a zero eq | z‚Č§n = refl
natPlusCancelSelf a (suc b) eq | ()

squareNotSelf : (n : ‚Ąē) ‚Üí ((suc (suc n) *‚Ąē suc (suc n)) ‚Č° suc (suc n)) ‚Üí ‚ä•
squareNotSelf n eq =
  let
    a : ‚Ąē
    a = suc (suc n)

    expand : a *‚Ąē a ‚Č° a +‚Ąē (a *‚Ąē suc n)
    expand = *‚Ąē-suc-right-+‚Ąē a (suc n)

    tail0 : (a *‚Ąē suc n) ‚Č° 0
    tail0 = natPlusCancelSelf a (a *‚Ąē suc n) (trans (sym expand) eq)

    bad : suc n ‚Č° 0
    bad = *‚Ąē-pos-zero‚Üízero (suc n) (suc n) tail0
  in
  suc‚Č†0 bad
  where
    suc‚Č†0 : {k : ‚Ąē} ‚Üí suc k ‚Č° 0 ‚Üí ‚ä•
    suc‚Č†0 ()

squareDominates : (n : ‚Ąē) ‚Üí suc (suc n) ‚Č§ (suc (suc n) *‚Ąē suc (suc n))
squareDominates n =
  let
    a : ‚Ąē
    a = suc (suc n)

    base : suc 0 ‚Č§ a
    base = s‚Č§s z‚Č§n

    step : (suc 0 *‚Ąē a) ‚Č§ (a *‚Ąē a)
    step = ‚Č§-*‚Ąē-mono ≥ base a
  in
  subst (őĽ t ‚Üí t ‚Č§ (a *‚Ąē a)) (*‚Ąē-one-left a) step

cubeNotSelf : (n : ‚Ąē) ‚Üí (((suc (suc n) *‚Ąē suc (suc n)) *‚Ąē suc (suc n)) ‚Č° suc (suc n)) ‚Üí ‚ä•
cubeNotSelf n eq =
  let
    a : ‚Ąē
    a = suc (suc n)

    sq : ‚Ąē
    sq = a *‚Ąē a

    expand : sq *‚Ąē a ‚Č° sq +‚Ąē (sq *‚Ąē suc n)
    expand = *‚Ąē-suc-right-+‚Ąē sq (suc n)

    sq‚Č§a : sq ‚Č§ a
    sq‚Č§a = ‚Č§-resp-‚Č° ≥ (natPlusRightLe sq (sq *‚Ąē suc n)) (trans (sym expand) eq)

    a‚Č§sq : a ‚Č§ sq
    a‚Č§sq = squareDominates n

    sq‚Č°a : sq ‚Č° a
    sq‚Č°a = ‚Č§-antisym sq‚Č§a a‚Č§sq
  in
  squareNotSelf n sq‚Č°a

mass-unit-forced : (m : ‚Ąē) ‚Üí ((m *‚Ąē 1) *‚Ąē 1) ‚Č° 1 ‚Üí m ‚Č° 1
mass-unit-forced zero ()
mass-unit-forced (suc zero) refl = refl
mass-unit-forced (suc (suc n)) ()
\end{code}

The elimination itself is now direct. The three bridge equations collapse to $a^3 = a$, and that excludes every $a \ge 2$. Positivity excludes $a = 0$. So $a = 1$, hence also $b = 1$ and $m = 1$.

\begin{code}
normalized-length-is-1 : (S : NormalizedScaleChoice) ‚Üí NormalizedScaleChoice.length-step S ‚Č° 1
normalized-length-is-1 S
  with NormalizedScaleChoice.length-step S
     | NormalizedScaleChoice.time-step S
     | NormalizedScaleChoice.mass-step S
     | NormalizedScaleChoice.length-nonzero S
     | NormalizedScaleChoice.c-lock S
     | NormalizedScaleChoice.hbar-lock S
     | NormalizedScaleChoice.G-lock S
... | zero          | _ | _ | nz | _  | _  | _  = ‚ä•-elim (nz refl)
... | suc zero      | _ | _ | _  | _  | _  | _  = refl
... | suc (suc n)   | b | m | _  | cl | hl | gl =
  let
    a : ‚Ąē
    a = suc (suc n)

    G-at-a : (m *‚Ąē a) *‚Ąē a ‚Č° (a *‚Ąē a) *‚Ąē a
    G-at-a = trans (sym (cong (őĽ t ‚Üí (m *‚Ąē t) *‚Ąē t) cl)) gl

    cubeEq : ((a *‚Ąē a) *‚Ąē a) ‚Č° a
    cubeEq = trans (sym G-at-a) (trans (sym hl) cl)
  in
  ‚ä•-elim (cubeNotSelf n cubeEq)

normalized-time-is-1 : (S : NormalizedScaleChoice) ‚Üí NormalizedScaleChoice.time-step S ‚Č° 1
normalized-time-is-1 S = trans (NormalizedScaleChoice.c-lock S) (normalized-length-is-1 S)

normalized-mass-is-1 : (S : NormalizedScaleChoice) ‚Üí NormalizedScaleChoice.mass-step S ‚Č° 1
normalized-mass-is-1 S =
  mass-unit-forced
    (NormalizedScaleChoice.mass-step S)
    (trans
      (cong (őĽ t ‚Üí (NormalizedScaleChoice.mass-step S *‚Ąē t) *‚Ąē t)
            (sym (normalized-length-is-1 S)))
      (trans
        (sym (NormalizedScaleChoice.hbar-lock S))
        (normalized-time-is-1 S)))

normalized-scale-witness : NormalizedScaleChoice
normalized-scale-witness = record
  { length-step    = 1
  ; time-step      = 1
  ; mass-step      = 1
  ; length-nonzero = őĽ ()
  ; c-lock         = refl
  ; hbar-lock      = refl
  ; G-lock         = refl
  }
\end{code}

This is the normalized form of the Planck claim. Before any decimal SI calibration enters, the only admissible positive scale triple in the $c = \hbar = G = 1$ regime is already the unit triple. The later SI gate therefore does not introduce new structure. It only names that already-forced triple in external units.

%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
\section{Distinction as measurement kernel}
\label{sec:scale-closure:measurement-kernel}
%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ

At this point the scale triangle is closed. A sharper question now replaces the earlier combinatorial one: what must any would-be physics already contain before it can even speak about measurement? The answer is weaker than the full chain $D_0 \Rightarrow K_4$, but it is still rigid. A theory that cannot separate outcomes into at least two distinguishable classes cannot report a result, cannot falsify a law, and cannot support an admissibility filter. Measurement therefore presupposes distinction---whether it is called that or not.

The formal kernel is exactly the old one in a new role. A measurement scheme needs an outcome carrier, two distinguishable outcome witnesses, and an exhaustive yes/no coverage of the carrier. That is not merely analogous to a distinction. It \emph{is} one.

\begin{code}
record BinaryMeasurement : Set1 where
  field
    State   : Set
    Outcome : Set
    measure : State ‚Üí Outcome
    yes     : Outcome
    no      : Outcome
    yes‚Č†no  : yes ‚Č† no
    total   : (y : Outcome) ‚Üí (y ‚Č° yes) ‚äé (y ‚Č° no)

open BinaryMeasurement public

measurement-outcome-distinction : (M : BinaryMeasurement) ‚Üí Distinction
measurement-outcome-distinction M =
  fromDistinctCovered
    (yes M)
    (no M)
    (yes‚Č†no M)
    (total M)

measurement-outcome-nontrivial : (M : BinaryMeasurement) ‚Üí NonTrivial (Outcome M)
measurement-outcome-nontrivial M =
  law0-5-distinct-pair-forces-nontrivial
    (yes M , (no M , yes‚Č†no M))

measurement-has-distinct-outcomes : (M : BinaryMeasurement) ‚Üí HasDistinctPair (Outcome M)
measurement-has-distinct-outcomes M =
  yes M , (no M , yes‚Č†no M)
\end{code}

These lemmas do not yet say that every physical formalism collapses all the way to $K_4$. They prove a narrower and cleaner threshold statement: every theory that claims to measure anything must already carry a distinction kernel in its outcome space. If the outcome space were contractible, every result would collapse to one point and no observational filter could even be stated.

This is the correct red line. $D_0$ is our name for the fully formalized package that begins from distinction and carries it through elimination. Rival frameworks may refuse that name. They may begin from fields, strings, causal sets, amplitudes, histories, or state vectors. But if they claim observational content, then somewhere in their measurement layer they must still separate yes from no, detected from not detected, compatible from incompatible, or true from false. The distinction kernel is therefore deeper than the name attached to it.

The next chapter does not assume that every framework has already collapsed to the whole FD chain. It assumes only what the lemmas above force: that measurement itself already stands on a distinction. The continuum question can then be asked sharply: once a theory has a distinction kernel and the discrete FD chain has closed to $K_4$, does the passage to decimal physics add freedom, or only expose the next forced quotient?

%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
\section{The SI rescaling gate}
\label{sec:scale-closure:si-gate}
%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ

The closure theorem does not claim that the book directly computes decimal SI numerals. It does something stricter and cleaner: it isolates the only place where continuum calibration may enter. Once $c_{\mathrm{FD}} = \hbar_{\mathrm{FD}} = G_{\mathrm{FD}} = 1$ are forced, an SI interpretation can differ from the native FD system only by three scale factors: one for length, one for time, and one for mass.

Write $a$ for the SI length assigned to one FD edge, $b$ for the SI time assigned to one FD update step, and $m$ for the SI mass assigned to one FD mass unit. Then the SI constants must satisfy the cross-multiplied bridge equations
\[
c_{\mathrm{SI}} \cdot b = a,
\qquad
\hbar_{\mathrm{SI}} \cdot b = m a^2,
\qquad
G_{\mathrm{SI}} \cdot m b^2 = a^3.
\]
These are exactly the dimensional translations of $c = a/b$, $\hbar = ma^2/b$, and $G = a^3/(mb^2)$, with the native FD values already fixed to~$1$.

\begin{code}
LengthScale : Set
LengthScale = ‚Ąö

TimeScale : Set
TimeScale = ‚Ąö

MassScale : Set
MassScale = ‚Ąö

record SIRescaling : Set where
  field
    length-scale : LengthScale
    time-scale   : TimeScale
    mass-scale   : MassScale
    c-SI         : ‚Ąö
    hbar-SI      : ‚Ąö
    G-SI         : ‚Ąö
    c-bridge     : (c-SI *‚Ąö time-scale) ‚ČÉ‚Ąö length-scale
    hbar-bridge  : (hbar-SI *‚Ąö time-scale) ‚ČÉ‚Ąö
                  ((mass-scale *‚Ąö length-scale) *‚Ąö length-scale)
    G-bridge     : ((G-SI *‚Ąö mass-scale) *‚Ąö (time-scale *‚Ąö time-scale)) ‚ČÉ‚Ąö
                  ((length-scale *‚Ąö length-scale) *‚Ąö length-scale)

unit-c-bridge : (1‚Ąö *‚Ąö 1‚Ąö) ‚ČÉ‚Ąö 1‚Ąö
unit-c-bridge = *‚Ąö-one-right 1‚Ąö

unit-hbar-bridge : (1‚Ąö *‚Ąö 1‚Ąö) ‚ČÉ‚Ąö ((1‚Ąö *‚Ąö 1‚Ąö) *‚Ąö 1‚Ąö)
unit-hbar-bridge = ‚ČÉ‚Ąö-sym (*‚Ąö-one-right (1‚Ąö *‚Ąö 1‚Ąö))

unit-G-bridge : ((1‚Ąö *‚Ąö 1‚Ąö) *‚Ąö (1‚Ąö *‚Ąö 1‚Ąö)) ‚ČÉ‚Ąö ((1‚Ąö *‚Ąö 1‚Ąö) *‚Ąö 1‚Ąö)
unit-G-bridge =
  ‚ČÉ‚Ąö-trans
    (*‚Ąö-assoc 1‚Ąö 1‚Ąö (1‚Ąö *‚Ąö 1‚Ąö))
    (‚ČÉ‚Ąö-trans
      (*‚Ąö-one-left (1‚Ąö *‚Ąö (1‚Ąö *‚Ąö 1‚Ąö)))
      (‚ČÉ‚Ąö-sym (*‚Ąö-assoc 1‚Ąö 1‚Ąö 1‚Ąö)))

normalized-planck-rescaling : SIRescaling
normalized-planck-rescaling = record
  { length-scale = 1‚Ąö
  ; time-scale   = 1‚Ąö
  ; mass-scale   = 1‚Ąö
  ; c-SI         = 1‚Ąö
  ; hbar-SI      = 1‚Ąö
  ; G-SI         = 1‚Ąö
  ; c-bridge     = unit-c-bridge
  ; hbar-bridge  = unit-hbar-bridge
  ; G-bridge     = unit-G-bridge
  }

square‚Ąö : ‚Ąö ‚Üí ‚Ąö
square‚Ąö q = q *‚Ąö q

cube-scale‚Ąö : ‚Ąö ‚Üí ‚Ąö ‚Üí ‚Ąö
cube-scale‚Ąö x c = (((x *‚Ąö c) *‚Ąö c) *‚Ąö c)

fifth-scale‚Ąö : ‚Ąö ‚Üí ‚Ąö ‚Üí ‚Ąö
fifth-scale‚Ąö x c = (((((x *‚Ąö c) *‚Ąö c) *‚Ąö c) *‚Ąö c) *‚Ąö c)

record AnalyticPlanckExtraction (R : SIRescaling) : Set where
  field
    planck-length-SI : ‚Ąö
    planck-time-SI   : ‚Ąö
    planck-mass-SI   : ‚Ąö
    length-nonneg    : 0‚Ąö ‚Č§‚Ąö planck-length-SI
    time-nonneg      : 0‚Ąö ‚Č§‚Ąö planck-time-SI
    mass-nonneg      : 0‚Ąö ‚Č§‚Ąö planck-mass-SI
    length-balance   :
      cube-scale‚Ąö (square‚Ąö planck-length-SI) (SIRescaling.c-SI R) ‚ČÉ‚Ąö
      (SIRescaling.hbar-SI R *‚Ąö SIRescaling.G-SI R)
    time-balance     :
      fifth-scale‚Ąö (square‚Ąö planck-time-SI) (SIRescaling.c-SI R) ‚ČÉ‚Ąö
      (SIRescaling.hbar-SI R *‚Ąö SIRescaling.G-SI R)
    mass-balance     :
      (square‚Ąö planck-mass-SI *‚Ąö SIRescaling.G-SI R) ‚ČÉ‚Ąö
      (SIRescaling.hbar-SI R *‚Ąö SIRescaling.c-SI R)

record AnalyticPlanckSolver : Set1 where
  field
    extract-planck : (R : SIRescaling) ‚Üí AnalyticPlanckExtraction R

one‚Ąö-nonneg : 0‚Ąö ‚Č§‚Ąö 1‚Ąö
one‚Ąö-nonneg = <‚Ąö‚Üí‚Č§‚Ąö 0‚Ąö<1‚Ąö

unit-square‚Ąö : square‚Ąö 1‚Ąö ‚ČÉ‚Ąö 1‚Ąö
unit-square‚Ąö = *‚Ąö-one-right 1‚Ąö

unit-cube-scale‚Ąö : cube-scale‚Ąö (square‚Ąö 1‚Ąö) 1‚Ąö ‚ČÉ‚Ąö 1‚Ąö
unit-cube-scale‚Ąö =
  ‚ČÉ‚Ąö-trans
    (*‚Ąö-one-right (((square‚Ąö 1‚Ąö) *‚Ąö 1‚Ąö) *‚Ąö 1‚Ąö))
    (‚ČÉ‚Ąö-trans
      (*‚Ąö-one-right ((square‚Ąö 1‚Ąö) *‚Ąö 1‚Ąö))
      (‚ČÉ‚Ąö-trans
        (*‚Ąö-one-right (square‚Ąö 1‚Ąö))
        unit-square‚Ąö))

unit-fifth-scale‚Ąö : fifth-scale‚Ąö (square‚Ąö 1‚Ąö) 1‚Ąö ‚ČÉ‚Ąö 1‚Ąö
unit-fifth-scale‚Ąö =
  ‚ČÉ‚Ąö-trans
    (*‚Ąö-one-right (((((square‚Ąö 1‚Ąö) *‚Ąö 1‚Ąö) *‚Ąö 1‚Ąö) *‚Ąö 1‚Ąö) *‚Ąö 1‚Ąö))
    (‚ČÉ‚Ąö-trans
    (*‚Ąö-one-right ((((square‚Ąö 1‚Ąö) *‚Ąö 1‚Ąö) *‚Ąö 1‚Ąö) *‚Ąö 1‚Ąö))
      (‚ČÉ‚Ąö-trans
        (*‚Ąö-one-right (((square‚Ąö 1‚Ąö) *‚Ąö 1‚Ąö) *‚Ąö 1‚Ąö))
        (‚ČÉ‚Ąö-trans
          (*‚Ąö-one-right ((square‚Ąö 1‚Ąö) *‚Ąö 1‚Ąö))
          (‚ČÉ‚Ąö-trans
            (*‚Ąö-one-right (square‚Ąö 1‚Ąö))
            unit-square‚Ąö))))

normalized-analytic-planck-extraction : AnalyticPlanckExtraction normalized-planck-rescaling
normalized-analytic-planck-extraction = record
  { planck-length-SI = 1‚Ąö
  ; planck-time-SI   = 1‚Ąö
  ; planck-mass-SI   = 1‚Ąö
  ; length-nonneg    = one‚Ąö-nonneg
  ; time-nonneg      = one‚Ąö-nonneg
  ; mass-nonneg      = one‚Ąö-nonneg
  ; length-balance   =
      ‚ČÉ‚Ąö-trans
        unit-cube-scale‚Ąö
        (‚ČÉ‚Ąö-sym (*‚Ąö-one-right 1‚Ąö))
  ; time-balance     =
      ‚ČÉ‚Ąö-trans
        unit-fifth-scale‚Ąö
        (‚ČÉ‚Ąö-sym (*‚Ąö-one-right 1‚Ąö))
  ; mass-balance     =
      ‚ČÉ‚Ąö-trans
        (‚ČÉ‚Ąö-trans
          (*‚Ąö-one-right (square‚Ąö 1‚Ąö))
          unit-square‚Ąö)
        (‚ČÉ‚Ąö-sym (*‚Ąö-one-right 1‚Ąö))
  }

square‚ĄĚ : ‚ĄĚ ‚Üí ‚ĄĚ
square‚ĄĚ x = x ‚čÖ‚ĄĚ x

record PositiveSquareRoot (x : ‚ĄĚ) : Set where
  field
    sqrt-value  : ‚ĄĚ
    sqrt-nonneg : 0‚ĄĚ ‚Č§‚ĄĚ sqrt-value
    sqrt-square : square‚ĄĚ sqrt-value ‚ČÉ‚ĄĚ x

record RealSquareRootSolver : Set1 where
  field
    sqrt‚Āļ-real : (x : ‚ĄĚ) ‚Üí 0‚ĄĚ ‚Č§‚ĄĚ x ‚Üí PositiveSquareRoot x

unit-square‚ĄĚ : square‚ĄĚ 1‚ĄĚ ‚ČÉ‚ĄĚ 1‚ĄĚ
unit-square‚ĄĚ = ‚čÖ‚ĄĚ-one-right 1‚ĄĚ

unit-positive-square-root : PositiveSquareRoot 1‚ĄĚ
unit-positive-square-root = record
  { sqrt-value  = 1‚ĄĚ
  ; sqrt-nonneg = ‚Č§‚ĄĚ-refl 1‚ĄĚ
  ; sqrt-square = unit-square‚ĄĚ
  }

0<‚Ą§-zero-impossible : 0‚Ą§ <‚Ą§ 0‚Ą§ ‚Üí ‚ä•
0<‚Ą§-zero-impossible (p‚Č§ , p‚Čį) = p‚Čį p‚Č§

0<‚Ąö‚ÜíNonZero‚Ąö : (q : ‚Ąö) ‚Üí 0‚Ąö <‚Ąö q ‚Üí NonZero‚Ąö q
0<‚Ąö‚ÜíNonZero‚Ąö q qpos q0 =
  let
    numPos : 0‚Ą§ <‚Ą§ num q
    numPos = 0‚Ąö<‚Üí0‚Ą§<num q qpos
  in
  0<‚Ą§-zero-impossible (‚Č§‚Ą§-resp-‚Č° ≥ q0 numPos)

record PositiveSIRescaling : Set where
  field
    base      : SIRescaling
    c-positive    : 0‚Ąö <‚Ąö SIRescaling.c-SI base
    hbar-positive : 0‚Ąö <‚Ąö SIRescaling.hbar-SI base
    G-positive    : 0‚Ąö <‚Ąö SIRescaling.G-SI base

c-inverse‚Ąö : PositiveSIRescaling ‚Üí ‚Ąö
c-inverse‚Ąö P = reciprocal‚Āļ (SIRescaling.c-SI (PositiveSIRescaling.base P))
  (PositiveSIRescaling.c-positive P)

cube‚Ąö : ‚Ąö ‚Üí ‚Ąö
cube‚Ąö q = (q *‚Ąö q) *‚Ąö q

fifth‚Ąö : ‚Ąö ‚Üí ‚Ąö
fifth‚Ąö q = (((q *‚Ąö q) *‚Ąö q) *‚Ąö q) *‚Ąö q

length-radicand‚Ąö : PositiveSIRescaling ‚Üí ‚Ąö
length-radicand‚Ąö P =
  (SIRescaling.hbar-SI (PositiveSIRescaling.base P) *‚Ąö
   SIRescaling.G-SI (PositiveSIRescaling.base P)) *‚Ąö
  cube‚Ąö (c-inverse‚Ąö P)

time-radicand‚Ąö : PositiveSIRescaling ‚Üí ‚Ąö
time-radicand‚Ąö P =
  (SIRescaling.hbar-SI (PositiveSIRescaling.base P) *‚Ąö
   SIRescaling.G-SI (PositiveSIRescaling.base P)) *‚Ąö
  fifth‚Ąö (c-inverse‚Ąö P)

mass-radicand‚Ąö : PositiveSIRescaling ‚Üí ‚Ąö
mass-radicand‚Ąö P =
  (SIRescaling.hbar-SI (PositiveSIRescaling.base P) *‚Ąö
   SIRescaling.c-SI (PositiveSIRescaling.base P)) *‚Ąö
  reciprocal‚Āļ (SIRescaling.G-SI (PositiveSIRescaling.base P))
    (PositiveSIRescaling.G-positive P)

length-radicand‚ĄĚ : PositiveSIRescaling ‚Üí ‚ĄĚ
length-radicand‚ĄĚ P = ‚Ąöto‚ĄĚ (length-radicand‚Ąö P)

time-radicand‚ĄĚ : PositiveSIRescaling ‚Üí ‚ĄĚ
time-radicand‚ĄĚ P = ‚Ąöto‚ĄĚ (time-radicand‚Ąö P)

mass-radicand‚ĄĚ : PositiveSIRescaling ‚Üí ‚ĄĚ
mass-radicand‚ĄĚ P = ‚Ąöto‚ĄĚ (mass-radicand‚Ąö P)

normalized-positive-si-rescaling : PositiveSIRescaling
normalized-positive-si-rescaling = record
  { base          = normalized-planck-rescaling
  ; c-positive    = 0‚Ąö<1‚Ąö
  ; hbar-positive = 0‚Ąö<1‚Ąö
  ; G-positive    = 0‚Ąö<1‚Ąö
  }

normalized-c-inverse‚Ąö : c-inverse‚Ąö normalized-positive-si-rescaling ‚ČÉ‚Ąö 1‚Ąö
normalized-c-inverse‚Ąö = *‚Ąö-reciprocal‚Āļ-left 1‚Ąö 0‚Ąö<1‚Ąö

normalized-length-radicand‚Ąö : length-radicand‚Ąö normalized-positive-si-rescaling ‚ČÉ‚Ąö 1‚Ąö
normalized-length-radicand‚Ąö = refl

normalized-time-radicand‚Ąö : time-radicand‚Ąö normalized-positive-si-rescaling ‚ČÉ‚Ąö 1‚Ąö
normalized-time-radicand‚Ąö = refl

normalized-mass-radicand‚Ąö : mass-radicand‚Ąö normalized-positive-si-rescaling ‚ČÉ‚Ąö 1‚Ąö
normalized-mass-radicand‚Ąö = refl

‚Ąöto‚ĄĚ-resp-‚ČÉ‚Ąö : {p q : ‚Ąö} ‚Üí p ‚ČÉ‚Ąö q ‚Üí ‚Ąöto‚ĄĚ p ‚ČÉ‚ĄĚ ‚Ąöto‚ĄĚ q
‚Ąöto‚ĄĚ-resp-‚ČÉ‚Ąö {p} {q} pq = record
  { conv0 = őĽ őĶ őĶpos ‚Üí
      zero , (őĽ n _ ‚Üí
        let
          d‚ČÉ0 : dist‚Ąö p q ‚ČÉ‚Ąö 0‚Ąö
          d‚ČÉ0 = dist‚Ąö-‚ČÉ0 pq

          d‚Č§0 : dist‚Ąö p q ‚Č§‚Ąö 0‚Ąö
          d‚Č§0 = ‚ČÉ‚Ąö‚Üí‚Č§‚Ąöň° {dist‚Ąö p q} {0‚Ąö} d‚ČÉ0
        in
        ‚Č§<‚Ąö‚Üí<‚Ąö {dist‚Ąö p q} {0‚Ąö} {őĶ} d‚Č§0 őĶpos)
  }

‚Ąöto‚ĄĚ-mono-‚Č§ : {p q : ‚Ąö} ‚Üí p ‚Č§‚Ąö q ‚Üí ‚Ąöto‚ĄĚ p ‚Č§‚ĄĚ ‚Ąöto‚ĄĚ q
‚Ąöto‚ĄĚ-mono-‚Č§ {p} {q} p‚Č§q = record
  { leReal = őĽ őĶ őĶpos ‚Üí
      zero , (őĽ n _ ‚Üí
        let
          q‚Č§q+őĶ : q ‚Č§‚Ąö (q +‚Ąö őĶ)
          q‚Č§q+őĶ = ‚Č§‚Ąö-add-nonneg-right q őĶ (<‚Ąö‚Üí‚Č§‚Ąö őĶpos)
        in
        ‚Č§‚Ąö-trans p‚Č§q q‚Č§q+őĶ)
  }

‚Ąöto‚ĄĚ-nonneg : {q : ‚Ąö} ‚Üí 0‚Ąö ‚Č§‚Ąö q ‚Üí 0‚ĄĚ ‚Č§‚ĄĚ ‚Ąöto‚ĄĚ q
‚Ąöto‚ĄĚ-nonneg {q} qnonneg = ‚Ąöto‚ĄĚ-mono-‚Č§ {0‚Ąö} {q} qnonneg

0‚Č§‚Ąö-*‚Ąö : (p q : ‚Ąö) ‚Üí 0‚Ąö ‚Č§‚Ąö p ‚Üí 0‚Ąö ‚Č§‚Ąö q ‚Üí 0‚Ąö ‚Č§‚Ąö (p *‚Ąö q)
0‚Č§‚Ąö-*‚Ąö p q pnonneg qnonneg =
  let
    scaled : (0‚Ąö *‚Ąö q) ‚Č§‚Ąö (p *‚Ąö q)
    scaled = ‚Č§‚Ąö-mul-nonneg-right 0‚Ąö p q pnonneg qnonneg

    zero‚Č§scaled : 0‚Ąö ‚Č§‚Ąö (0‚Ąö *‚Ąö q)
    zero‚Č§scaled = ‚ČÉ‚Ąö‚Üí‚Č§‚Ąö ≥ (*‚Ąö-zero-left q)
  in
  ‚Č§‚Ąö-trans zero‚Č§scaled scaled

cube‚Ąö-nonneg : (q : ‚Ąö) ‚Üí 0‚Ąö ‚Č§‚Ąö q ‚Üí 0‚Ąö ‚Č§‚Ąö cube‚Ąö q
cube‚Ąö-nonneg q qnonneg =
  0‚Č§‚Ąö-*‚Ąö (q *‚Ąö q) q (0‚Č§‚Ąö-*‚Ąö q q qnonneg qnonneg) qnonneg

fifth‚Ąö-nonneg : (q : ‚Ąö) ‚Üí 0‚Ąö ‚Č§‚Ąö q ‚Üí 0‚Ąö ‚Č§‚Ąö fifth‚Ąö q
fifth‚Ąö-nonneg q qnonneg =
  0‚Č§‚Ąö-*‚Ąö ((((q *‚Ąö q) *‚Ąö q) *‚Ąö q)) q
    (0‚Č§‚Ąö-*‚Ąö (((q *‚Ąö q) *‚Ąö q)) q
      (0‚Č§‚Ąö-*‚Ąö (q *‚Ąö q) q (0‚Č§‚Ąö-*‚Ąö q q qnonneg qnonneg) qnonneg)
      qnonneg)
    qnonneg

length-radicand‚Ąö-nonneg : (P : PositiveSIRescaling) ‚Üí 0‚Ąö ‚Č§‚Ąö length-radicand‚Ąö P
length-radicand‚Ąö-nonneg P =
  let
    hbarNonneg : 0‚Ąö ‚Č§‚Ąö SIRescaling.hbar-SI (PositiveSIRescaling.base P)
    hbarNonneg = <‚Ąö‚Üí‚Č§‚Ąö (PositiveSIRescaling.hbar-positive P)

    GNonneg : 0‚Ąö ‚Č§‚Ąö SIRescaling.G-SI (PositiveSIRescaling.base P)
    GNonneg = <‚Ąö‚Üí‚Č§‚Ąö (PositiveSIRescaling.G-positive P)

    cinvNonneg : 0‚Ąö ‚Č§‚Ąö c-inverse‚Ąö P
    cinvNonneg = <‚Ąö‚Üí‚Č§‚Ąö (reciprocal‚Āļ-pos (SIRescaling.c-SI (PositiveSIRescaling.base P))
      (PositiveSIRescaling.c-positive P))
  in
  0‚Č§‚Ąö-*‚Ąö
    (SIRescaling.hbar-SI (PositiveSIRescaling.base P) *‚Ąö SIRescaling.G-SI (PositiveSIRescaling.base P))
    (cube‚Ąö (c-inverse‚Ąö P))
    (0‚Č§‚Ąö-*‚Ąö
      (SIRescaling.hbar-SI (PositiveSIRescaling.base P))
      (SIRescaling.G-SI (PositiveSIRescaling.base P))
      hbarNonneg GNonneg)
    (cube‚Ąö-nonneg (c-inverse‚Ąö P) cinvNonneg)

time-radicand‚Ąö-nonneg : (P : PositiveSIRescaling) ‚Üí 0‚Ąö ‚Č§‚Ąö time-radicand‚Ąö P
time-radicand‚Ąö-nonneg P =
  let
    hbarNonneg : 0‚Ąö ‚Č§‚Ąö SIRescaling.hbar-SI (PositiveSIRescaling.base P)
    hbarNonneg = <‚Ąö‚Üí‚Č§‚Ąö (PositiveSIRescaling.hbar-positive P)

    GNonneg : 0‚Ąö ‚Č§‚Ąö SIRescaling.G-SI (PositiveSIRescaling.base P)
    GNonneg = <‚Ąö‚Üí‚Č§‚Ąö (PositiveSIRescaling.G-positive P)

    cinvNonneg : 0‚Ąö ‚Č§‚Ąö c-inverse‚Ąö P
    cinvNonneg = <‚Ąö‚Üí‚Č§‚Ąö (reciprocal‚Āļ-pos (SIRescaling.c-SI (PositiveSIRescaling.base P))
      (PositiveSIRescaling.c-positive P))
  in
  0‚Č§‚Ąö-*‚Ąö
    (SIRescaling.hbar-SI (PositiveSIRescaling.base P) *‚Ąö SIRescaling.G-SI (PositiveSIRescaling.base P))
    (fifth‚Ąö (c-inverse‚Ąö P))
    (0‚Č§‚Ąö-*‚Ąö
      (SIRescaling.hbar-SI (PositiveSIRescaling.base P))
      (SIRescaling.G-SI (PositiveSIRescaling.base P))
      hbarNonneg GNonneg)
    (fifth‚Ąö-nonneg (c-inverse‚Ąö P) cinvNonneg)

mass-radicand‚Ąö-nonneg : (P : PositiveSIRescaling) ‚Üí 0‚Ąö ‚Č§‚Ąö mass-radicand‚Ąö P
mass-radicand‚Ąö-nonneg P =
  let
    hbarNonneg : 0‚Ąö ‚Č§‚Ąö SIRescaling.hbar-SI (PositiveSIRescaling.base P)
    hbarNonneg = <‚Ąö‚Üí‚Č§‚Ąö (PositiveSIRescaling.hbar-positive P)

    cNonneg : 0‚Ąö ‚Č§‚Ąö SIRescaling.c-SI (PositiveSIRescaling.base P)
    cNonneg = <‚Ąö‚Üí‚Č§‚Ąö (PositiveSIRescaling.c-positive P)

    GinvNonneg : 0‚Ąö ‚Č§‚Ąö reciprocal‚Āļ (SIRescaling.G-SI (PositiveSIRescaling.base P))
      (PositiveSIRescaling.G-positive P)
    GinvNonneg = <‚Ąö‚Üí‚Č§‚Ąö (reciprocal‚Āļ-pos (SIRescaling.G-SI (PositiveSIRescaling.base P))
      (PositiveSIRescaling.G-positive P))
  in
  0‚Č§‚Ąö-*‚Ąö
    (SIRescaling.hbar-SI (PositiveSIRescaling.base P) *‚Ąö SIRescaling.c-SI (PositiveSIRescaling.base P))
    (reciprocal‚Āļ (SIRescaling.G-SI (PositiveSIRescaling.base P))
      (PositiveSIRescaling.G-positive P))
    (0‚Č§‚Ąö-*‚Ąö
      (SIRescaling.hbar-SI (PositiveSIRescaling.base P))
      (SIRescaling.c-SI (PositiveSIRescaling.base P))
      hbarNonneg cNonneg)
    GinvNonneg

length-radicand‚ĄĚ-nonneg : (P : PositiveSIRescaling) ‚Üí 0‚ĄĚ ‚Č§‚ĄĚ length-radicand‚ĄĚ P
length-radicand‚ĄĚ-nonneg P = ‚Ąöto‚ĄĚ-nonneg (length-radicand‚Ąö-nonneg P)

time-radicand‚ĄĚ-nonneg : (P : PositiveSIRescaling) ‚Üí 0‚ĄĚ ‚Č§‚ĄĚ time-radicand‚ĄĚ P
time-radicand‚ĄĚ-nonneg P = ‚Ąöto‚ĄĚ-nonneg (time-radicand‚Ąö-nonneg P)

mass-radicand‚ĄĚ-nonneg : (P : PositiveSIRescaling) ‚Üí 0‚ĄĚ ‚Č§‚ĄĚ mass-radicand‚ĄĚ P
mass-radicand‚ĄĚ-nonneg P = ‚Ąöto‚ĄĚ-nonneg (mass-radicand‚Ąö-nonneg P)

record RealAnalyticPlanckExtraction (P : PositiveSIRescaling) : Set where
  field
    planck-length-‚ĄĚ : ‚ĄĚ
    planck-time-‚ĄĚ   : ‚ĄĚ
    planck-mass-‚ĄĚ   : ‚ĄĚ
    length-nonneg-‚ĄĚ : 0‚ĄĚ ‚Č§‚ĄĚ planck-length-‚ĄĚ
    time-nonneg-‚ĄĚ   : 0‚ĄĚ ‚Č§‚ĄĚ planck-time-‚ĄĚ
    mass-nonneg-‚ĄĚ   : 0‚ĄĚ ‚Č§‚ĄĚ planck-mass-‚ĄĚ
    length-square-‚ĄĚ : square‚ĄĚ planck-length-‚ĄĚ ‚ČÉ‚ĄĚ length-radicand‚ĄĚ P
    time-square-‚ĄĚ   : square‚ĄĚ planck-time-‚ĄĚ ‚ČÉ‚ĄĚ time-radicand‚ĄĚ P
    mass-square-‚ĄĚ   : square‚ĄĚ planck-mass-‚ĄĚ ‚ČÉ‚ĄĚ mass-radicand‚ĄĚ P

record RealAnalyticPlanckSolver : Set1 where
  field
    extract-planck-‚ĄĚ : (P : PositiveSIRescaling) ‚Üí RealAnalyticPlanckExtraction P

record PlanckRadicandRoots (P : PositiveSIRescaling) : Set where
  field
    length-root : PositiveSquareRoot (length-radicand‚ĄĚ P)
    time-root   : PositiveSquareRoot (time-radicand‚ĄĚ P)
    mass-root   : PositiveSquareRoot (mass-radicand‚ĄĚ P)

record SpecializedPlanckRootSolver : Set1 where
  field
    solve-planck-radicands : (P : PositiveSIRescaling) ‚Üí PlanckRadicandRoots P

normalized-length-radicand‚ĄĚ : length-radicand‚ĄĚ normalized-positive-si-rescaling ‚ČÉ‚ĄĚ 1‚ĄĚ
normalized-length-radicand‚ĄĚ =
  ‚ČÉ‚ĄĚ-sym (‚Ąöto‚ĄĚ-resp-‚ČÉ‚Ąö normalized-length-radicand‚Ąö)

normalized-time-radicand‚ĄĚ : time-radicand‚ĄĚ normalized-positive-si-rescaling ‚ČÉ‚ĄĚ 1‚ĄĚ
normalized-time-radicand‚ĄĚ =
  ‚ČÉ‚ĄĚ-sym (‚Ąöto‚ĄĚ-resp-‚ČÉ‚Ąö normalized-time-radicand‚Ąö)

normalized-mass-radicand‚ĄĚ : mass-radicand‚ĄĚ normalized-positive-si-rescaling ‚ČÉ‚ĄĚ 1‚ĄĚ
normalized-mass-radicand‚ĄĚ =
  ‚ČÉ‚ĄĚ-sym (‚Ąöto‚ĄĚ-resp-‚ČÉ‚Ąö normalized-mass-radicand‚Ąö)

normalized-planck-radicand-roots : PlanckRadicandRoots normalized-positive-si-rescaling
normalized-planck-radicand-roots = record
  { length-root = record
      { sqrt-value  = 1‚ĄĚ
      ; sqrt-nonneg = ‚Č§‚ĄĚ-refl 1‚ĄĚ
      ; sqrt-square = ‚ČÉ‚ĄĚ-trans unit-square‚ĄĚ (‚ČÉ‚ĄĚ-sym normalized-length-radicand‚ĄĚ)
      }
  ; time-root = record
      { sqrt-value  = 1‚ĄĚ
      ; sqrt-nonneg = ‚Č§‚ĄĚ-refl 1‚ĄĚ
      ; sqrt-square = ‚ČÉ‚ĄĚ-trans unit-square‚ĄĚ (‚ČÉ‚ĄĚ-sym normalized-time-radicand‚ĄĚ)
      }
  ; mass-root = record
      { sqrt-value  = 1‚ĄĚ
      ; sqrt-nonneg = ‚Č§‚ĄĚ-refl 1‚ĄĚ
      ; sqrt-square = ‚ČÉ‚ĄĚ-trans unit-square‚ĄĚ (‚ČÉ‚ĄĚ-sym normalized-mass-radicand‚ĄĚ)
      }
  }

assemble-real-analytic-planck-solver : RealSquareRootSolver ‚Üí RealAnalyticPlanckSolver
assemble-real-analytic-planck-solver sqrtSolver = record
  { extract-planck-‚ĄĚ = őĽ P ‚Üí
      let
        lengthRoot : PositiveSquareRoot (length-radicand‚ĄĚ P)
        lengthRoot = RealSquareRootSolver.sqrt‚Āļ-real sqrtSolver (length-radicand‚ĄĚ P) (length-radicand‚ĄĚ-nonneg P)

        timeRoot : PositiveSquareRoot (time-radicand‚ĄĚ P)
        timeRoot = RealSquareRootSolver.sqrt‚Āļ-real sqrtSolver (time-radicand‚ĄĚ P) (time-radicand‚ĄĚ-nonneg P)

        massRoot : PositiveSquareRoot (mass-radicand‚ĄĚ P)
        massRoot = RealSquareRootSolver.sqrt‚Āļ-real sqrtSolver (mass-radicand‚ĄĚ P) (mass-radicand‚ĄĚ-nonneg P)
      in
      record
        { planck-length-‚ĄĚ = PositiveSquareRoot.sqrt-value lengthRoot
        ; planck-time-‚ĄĚ   = PositiveSquareRoot.sqrt-value timeRoot
        ; planck-mass-‚ĄĚ   = PositiveSquareRoot.sqrt-value massRoot
        ; length-nonneg-‚ĄĚ = PositiveSquareRoot.sqrt-nonneg lengthRoot
        ; time-nonneg-‚ĄĚ   = PositiveSquareRoot.sqrt-nonneg timeRoot
        ; mass-nonneg-‚ĄĚ   = PositiveSquareRoot.sqrt-nonneg massRoot
        ; length-square-‚ĄĚ = PositiveSquareRoot.sqrt-square lengthRoot
        ; time-square-‚ĄĚ   = PositiveSquareRoot.sqrt-square timeRoot
        ; mass-square-‚ĄĚ   = PositiveSquareRoot.sqrt-square massRoot
        }
  }

assemble-specialized-planck-solver : SpecializedPlanckRootSolver ‚Üí RealAnalyticPlanckSolver
assemble-specialized-planck-solver rootSolver = record
  { extract-planck-‚ĄĚ = őĽ P ‚Üí
      let
        roots : PlanckRadicandRoots P
        roots = SpecializedPlanckRootSolver.solve-planck-radicands rootSolver P

        lengthRoot : PositiveSquareRoot (length-radicand‚ĄĚ P)
        lengthRoot = PlanckRadicandRoots.length-root roots

        timeRoot : PositiveSquareRoot (time-radicand‚ĄĚ P)
        timeRoot = PlanckRadicandRoots.time-root roots

        massRoot : PositiveSquareRoot (mass-radicand‚ĄĚ P)
        massRoot = PlanckRadicandRoots.mass-root roots
      in
      record
        { planck-length-‚ĄĚ = PositiveSquareRoot.sqrt-value lengthRoot
        ; planck-time-‚ĄĚ   = PositiveSquareRoot.sqrt-value timeRoot
        ; planck-mass-‚ĄĚ   = PositiveSquareRoot.sqrt-value massRoot
        ; length-nonneg-‚ĄĚ = PositiveSquareRoot.sqrt-nonneg lengthRoot
        ; time-nonneg-‚ĄĚ   = PositiveSquareRoot.sqrt-nonneg timeRoot
        ; mass-nonneg-‚ĄĚ   = PositiveSquareRoot.sqrt-nonneg massRoot
        ; length-square-‚ĄĚ = PositiveSquareRoot.sqrt-square lengthRoot
        ; time-square-‚ĄĚ   = PositiveSquareRoot.sqrt-square timeRoot
        ; mass-square-‚ĄĚ   = PositiveSquareRoot.sqrt-square massRoot
        }
  }

\end{code}

This record is the whole gate from FD to SI. No fourth free parameter is permitted. All empirical calibration is concentrated in the choice of a single rescaling triple $(a,b,m)$ satisfying these three equations. In physical language, the solution is the Planck calibration: once the measured continuum values of $c$, $\hbar$, and $G$ are fixed, the three native FD units are identified with the Planck length, Planck time, and Planck mass.

The code above now contains the normalized witness explicitly: if the continuum constants are already expressed in Planck units, so that $c_{\mathrm{SI}} = \hbar_{\mathrm{SI}} = G_{\mathrm{SI}} = 1$, then the canonical rescaling is the unit triple $(a,b,m) = (1,1,1)$. That is the formal content of \texttt{normalized-planck-rescaling}.

The book now also fixes the exact shape of the remaining analytic problem. The record \texttt{AnalyticPlanckExtraction} expresses the three Planck formulas using multiplication alone:
\[
\ell_P^2 c^3 = \hbar G,
\qquad
t_P^2 c^5 = \hbar G,
\qquad
m_P^2 G = \hbar c.
\]
So the missing endgame is no longer vague. A full decimal-to-Planck normalization theorem is precisely a term of \texttt{AnalyticPlanckSolver}. The normalized case is already realized by \texttt{normalized-analytic-planck-extraction}: when the continuum constants are themselves normalized to $1$, the analytic witnesses for $(\ell_P,t_P,m_P)$ are again the unit triple.

The next missing solver layer is now fixed just as explicitly. A general square-root theory on the positive real sector is precisely a term of \texttt{RealSquareRootSolver}, and the normalized seed case is already witnessed by \texttt{unit-positive-square-root}. So the analytic staircase is now split into two clean interfaces rather than one vague gap: inversion on $\mathbb{Q}$, then square-root extraction on $\mathbb{R}$, then the final assembly of the Planck solver.

One correction of target space is now explicit. For arbitrary measured SI data, the Planck scales need not be rational. So the genuinely general endpoint cannot live in $\mathbb{Q}$ alone. The book therefore now contains \texttt{RealAnalyticPlanckExtraction}: three nonnegative real outputs whose squares reproduce the three already-defined Planck radicands. In this stricter sense, \texttt{AnalyticPlanckExtraction} is the rational balance ledger, while \texttt{RealAnalyticPlanckSolver} is the honest general target for arbitrary continuum data.

The remaining gap can also be stated in a weaker and more focused form. Instead of demanding a square-root solver for all nonnegative reals, it already suffices to provide square roots for the three Planck radicands attached to a positive SI rescaling. That weaker interface is exactly \texttt{SpecializedPlanckRootSolver}, and the function \texttt{assemble-specialized-planck-solver} shows that nothing else is missing on the assembly side.

What is 
\emph{not yet} formalized is the final analytic step from arbitrary measured decimal SI values to that normalized witness by explicit inversion and square-root formulas. The present book has forced multiplication, order, and Cauchy completion, but it has not yet built a general square-root / reciprocal solver at this late chapter boundary. So the correct claim at this stage is precise: the SI gate is fully identified, the normalized Planck calibration is witnessed, and the absence of any fourth scale parameter is proved; the explicit analytic extraction of $(\ell_P,t_P,m_P)$ from measured SI numerals remains future infrastructure rather than hidden assumption.

\subsubsection*{Threshold To Physics}

At this point the discrete chain has already delivered its verdict. The carrier has been forced into a two-class distinction. The two-class distinction has been forced into the unique $K_4$ graph. The graph has been forced into a rigid invariant ledger. And the observable principle has stripped away the last semantic slack by ruling out every quantity that depends on presentation, labeling, or proof artefacts.

What survives this pressure is no longer a loose collection of combinatorial curiosities. It is a closed discrete record: $V = 4$, $E = 6$, $d = 3$, $\chi = 2$, $\kappa = 8$, the Clifford count $16$, the compactification stratum $17$, and the rigid coupling value $\alpha^{-1} = 137$. Nothing in this ledger has been fitted. Every entry is already the survivor of an elimination chain.

This is the last point at which the work is purely discrete. The next question cannot be whether different integers would do just as well. That question has already collapsed at the theorem level. The only live question is harsher: when experiment reports decimal structure rather than bare integers, does the continuum introduce freedom, or does it merely expose the next forced quotient of the same graph?

\textbf{Constraint:} Before physics begins, the discrete stage must already be closed. If any invariant or observable were still negotiable here, every later numerical match would be contaminated by hidden freedom.

\textbf{Construction:} The preceding parts force exactly two ingredients: the invariant ledger of $K_4$, and the admissibility criterion for observables on that ledger.

\textbf{Consequence:} The physics chapter does not begin by choosing formulas. It begins with a sealed discrete record and asks whether the passage to measured continuum values is itself forced.


%‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź
\chapter{The Discrete-Continuum Bridge}
\label{ch:discrete-continuum-bridge}
%‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź

The discrete record is closed, but experiment is not reported as a ledger of bare integers. Physics is measured in a smooth continuum. The proton-to-electron mass ratio is not $1836$ but $1836.15267$, and the fine-structure constant is not $1/137$ but $1/137.036$. The question is therefore no longer which discrete invariants survive. That question is settled. The sharper question is whether the decimal structure is an independent source of freedom, or the next forced quotient of the same $K_4$ data.

The chapter proceeds in three stages, each with a distinct epistemic status:

\begin{enumerate}
\item \textbf{Primary graph invariants.} The raw data of $K_4$---$V$, $E$, $d$, $\chi$, the spectrum, $\kappa$, and $\mathrm{Aut}(K_4) \cong S_4$---are forced by the classification theorems of the preceding parts. These are the hardest results in the chain: no freedom remains.

\item \textbf{Forced observables.} Not every function of these invariants is physically admissible. Part~IV established that only observables which are presentation-independent, automorphism-invariant, and canonically factorable qualify. At the primitive global level, this admissible sector collapses to four classes only: boundary, correlator, observer, and channels. This chapter then evaluates and combines those primitive observables with the rigid ledger invariants of $K_4$ to classify the derived integers $137$, $1836$, $11$, $72$, and $576$.

\item \textbf{Canonical correction.} The discrete-to-continuum transition is not a new source of freedom but a canonical quotient: the forced loop observable supplies the numerator; the canonical interaction volume at each energy scale supplies the denominator. The resulting rational corrections---$11/72$, $11/576$---are the unique bridge between the discrete graph and the measured continuum.
\end{enumerate}

If any of these stages introduced a free parameter, the chain would break. The integer values would become numerological coincidences and the decimal places fitted afterthoughts. The strength of the argument is that no parameter enters at any stage: primary invariants are forced, observables are classified, and the correction is canonical. Interpretation enters only after those three theorem-level stages are closed.

%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
\section{The admissibility filter}
\label{sec:discrete-continuum:admissibility-filter}
%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ

Part~IV already shattered the idea that every graph function deserves physical status. Most quantities collapse at the threshold. They depend on labeling, on presentation, or on proof packaging. If that noise were allowed to cross into the physics chapter, every later number would lose its force. Nature therefore permits only those quantities that survive a stricter tribunal: they must ignore presentation, survive the full symmetry group, and descend through the unique canonical observable map.

Part~IV proved the Observable Factorization Theorem (\hyperref[law:observables:factor-through-base]{Laws~9.3--9.4}): every admissible observable on the indexed drift ledger factors through a unique base computation, and that factorization is unique up to canonical identification. What counts as a ``physical quantity'' is therefore not a matter of definition but of elimination:

\begin{enumerate}
\item \textbf{Presentation invariance.} The observable must not depend on which labeling of the carrier is chosen. Any two presentations that are total, sound, and injective yield the same value (\hyperref[law:classification:distinction-to-class]{Law~1.17}).

\item \textbf{Automorphism invariance.} The value must be fixed under the full automorphism group of the underlying structure. For $K_4$, that group is $\mathrm{Aut}(K_4) \cong S_4$: only $S_4$-invariant combinations of the graph data qualify.

\item \textbf{Canonical factorization.} The observable must factor through the canonical base computation $\texttt{canonObs}$ (\hyperref[law:observables:canonObs-sound]{Law~9.10}). Observables that fail to factor are proof-dependent artefacts, not structural quantities.
\end{enumerate}

The integers derived below are not ``nice numbers from a graph.'' At the primitive global level, only four observable classes survive at all; everything else entering the physics chapter is obtained by evaluating those classes, the local observable layer, and the already forced ledger invariants on the canonical object $K_4$. The admissibility filter therefore converts the question ``why this number?'' into the sharper question ``which surviving observable class or forced invariant chain leaves it behind?'' The answer is that elimination leaves no room for a second survivor.

\textbf{Constraint:} A quantity can enter physics only if it is invariant under presentation, invariant under $\mathrm{Aut}(K_4)$, and canonically factored through the unique observable base. Any weaker criterion would let proof artefacts masquerade as observables.

\textbf{Construction:} The observable factorization theorem supplies exactly one admissibility filter: presentation invariance, automorphism invariance, and canonical factorization. On the fixed $K_4$ ledger this filter leaves a finite primitive observable basis, and the later numerical quantities arise only as evaluations or derived combinations of that basis with already forced invariants.

\textbf{Consequence:} The numbers carried into the loop and mass sectors are not selected formulas. Their primitive observable inputs are already fixed, and every later derived value is constrained by that fixed basis together with the invariant ledger, so excluded alternatives collapse before any numerical comparison with experiment begins.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß The universal loop numerator: sum of all primitive loop parameters.
loop-numerator : ‚Ąē
loop-numerator = edgeCountK4 + degree-K4 + eulerChar-computed
\end{code}

%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
\section{Tree-level mass formulae}
\label{sec:discrete-continuum:tree-level-mass-formulae}
%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ

Before any correction can be applied, the bare values must exist. They are not hypotheses; they are computable functions of the invariants already fixed.

The proton-to-electron mass ratio is built from the Euler characteristic $\chi$, the vertex degree $d$, and the Fermat stratum $F_2$. Two combinatorially distinct decompositions yield the same integer, locked together by the identity $\chi \cdot d = E$.

A direct question must be faced: why the product $\chi^2 \cdot d^3 \cdot F_2$ and not, say, $V^2 \times E \times d = 288$? The answer is not immediate logical necessity but classification: $1836 = 2^2 \times 3^3 \times 17$, and among the $K_4$ invariants the factor $17 = F_2$ is prime and appears nowhere else. The remaining factor $108 = \chi^2 d^3 = E^2 d$ admits exactly two monomial factorizations over $\{V, E, d, \chi\}$, both of minimal degree. Their agreement ($\chi^2 d^3 = E^2 d$ via the identity $\chi d = E$) is a non-trivial structural constraint. Whether this classification constitutes \emph{necessity} or merely \emph{distinction} is addressed in the classification section below.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Tree-level proton-to-electron mass ratio from K‚āĄ invariants
proton-mass-bare : ‚Ąē
proton-mass-bare =
  (eulerChar-computed * eulerChar-computed)
  * (degree-K4 * degree-K4 * degree-K4)
  * F‚āā

-- ¬ß Law: proton bare mass is exactly 1836
law-proton-bare-1836 : proton-mass-bare ‚Č° 1836
law-proton-bare-1836 = refl

-- ¬ß Alternative factorization: d √ó E¬≤ √ó F‚āā
proton-mass-alt : ‚Ąē
proton-mass-alt = degree-K4 * (edgeCountK4 * edgeCountK4) * F‚āā

-- ¬ß Both factorizations agree
law-proton-alt-1836 : proton-mass-alt ‚Č° 1836
law-proton-alt-1836 = refl

law-proton-factorizations-agree : proton-mass-bare ‚Č° proton-mass-alt
law-proton-factorizations-agree = refl

-- ¬ß The identity that connects them: Ōá ¬∑ d = E
law-chi-times-d-is-E : eulerChar-computed * degree-K4 ‚Č° edgeCountK4
law-chi-times-d-is-E = refl

-- ¬ß Tree-level muon-to-electron mass ratio from K‚āĄ invariants
muon-mass-bare : ‚Ąē
muon-mass-bare = (degree-K4 * degree-K4) * (edgeCountK4 + F‚āā)

-- ¬ß Law: muon bare mass ratio is exactly 207
law-muon-bare-207 : muon-mass-bare ‚Č° 207
law-muon-bare-207 = refl

-- ¬ß Tau-to-muon ratio: the Fermat stratum alone
tau-muon-bare : ‚Ąē
tau-muon-bare = F‚āā

-- ¬ß Law: tau/muon bare ratio is 17
law-tau-muon-bare-17 : tau-muon-bare ‚Č° 17
law-tau-muon-bare-17 = refl
\end{code}

%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
\section{The universal loop numerator}
\label{sec:discrete-continuum:universal-loop-numerator}
%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ

When a discrete graph is projected into a continuum, interactions that were sharp graph traversals become smeared across the embedding space. The smearing generates corrections. The question is not ``what number shall we put in the numerator?'' but ``which observable is forced by the admissibility filter in the loop sector?''

On $K_4$, the primitive structural parameters for a graph interaction are the edge count ($E = 6$), the vertex degree ($d = 3$), and the Euler characteristic ($\chi = 2$). A loop observable is a $\{0,1\}$-linear combination of these---a sum of a subset. The admissibility constraints are:

\begin{enumerate}
\item \textbf{Irreducibility.} The correction fraction $N/D$ must be irreducible: $\gcd(N,\, V \!\cdot\! E \!\cdot\! d) = 1$. Common factors would indicate that the observable double-counts structure already present in the interaction volume.

\item \textbf{Completeness.} The observable must use the full irreducible loop basis, not a proper subset. This requirement is not an additional axiom: it is derived below from the $K_4$ identity $\chi \cdot d = E$ and the role of $E$ in the interaction volume.

\item \textbf{Cross-scale consistency.} The same numerator must appear at every energy scale. If different scales required different numerators, the loop observable would depend on the embedding, not on the graph.
\end{enumerate}

The classification section below proves exhaustively that among all $\{0,1\}$-linear combinations of $\{E, d, \chi\}$, exactly two are coprime to the QCD denominator $72$: the partial sum $d + \chi = 5$ and the full sum $E + d + \chi = 11$. The partial sum fails completeness (it omits the edge sector). The full sum is the unique admissible observable. Cross-scale consistency then provides a confirmation rather than an additional elimination: the partial $d + \chi = 5$ also happens to be coprime to the EW denominator $576$, so the filters that eliminate it are irreducibility at QCD scale (passing) and completeness (failing). Only $11$ passes all three.

\textbf{Constraint:} The loop observable must be admissible: irreducible, complete, and cross-scale consistent.

\textbf{Construction:} $E + d + \chi = 6 + 3 + 2 = 11$ is the unique $\{0,1\}$-linear combination satisfying all three constraints. The elimination sequence is: Filter~1 reduces 8 candidates to 2; Filter~2 eliminates the incomplete partial sum; Filter~3 confirms the result.

\textbf{Consequence:} $11$ is not proposed. It is forced by elimination: every alternative fails completeness or irreducibility.

\subsubsection*{Candidate space and denominators}

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law: loop numerator is exactly 11
law-loop-num-11 : loop-numerator ‚Č° 11
law-loop-num-11 = refl

-- ¬ß Decomposition: the three structural contributions
law-loop-num-decomposition :
  loop-numerator ‚Č° 6 + 3 + 2
law-loop-num-decomposition = refl

-- ¬ß Denominator definitions for the classification proof; the narrative discussion follows below.

-- ¬ß Loop denominator at QCD (hadron) scale
loop-denom-QCD : ‚Ąē
loop-denom-QCD = vertexCountK4 * edgeCountK4 * degree-K4

-- ¬ß Loop denominator at electroweak scale
loop-denom-EW : ‚Ąē
loop-denom-EW = loop-denom-QCD * őļ-discrete

\end{code}

\subsubsection*{Exhaustive candidate enumeration}

The search space is finite, so the argument is clearer when it proceeds in the same order as the earlier census sections: enumerate first, then eliminate by filters.

\begin{code}

-- ¬ß Exhaustive loop classification over the 2¬≥ {0,1}-linear combinations of {E, d, Ōá}.

-- ¬ß The eight candidates and their values:
loop-cand-000 : ‚Ąē           -- empty set
loop-cand-000 = 0
loop-cand-001 : ‚Ąē           -- Ōá only
loop-cand-001 = eulerChar-computed
loop-cand-010 : ‚Ąē           -- d only
loop-cand-010 = degree-K4
loop-cand-011 : ‚Ąē           -- d + Ōá
loop-cand-011 = degree-K4 + eulerChar-computed
loop-cand-100 : ‚Ąē           -- E only
loop-cand-100 = edgeCountK4
loop-cand-101 : ‚Ąē           -- E + Ōá
loop-cand-101 = edgeCountK4 + eulerChar-computed
loop-cand-110 : ‚Ąē           -- E + d
loop-cand-110 = edgeCountK4 + degree-K4
loop-cand-111 : ‚Ąē           -- E + d + Ōá
loop-cand-111 = edgeCountK4 + degree-K4 + eulerChar-computed

-- ¬ß Values
law-cand-000 : loop-cand-000 ‚Č° 0
law-cand-000 = refl
law-cand-001 : loop-cand-001 ‚Č° 2
law-cand-001 = refl
law-cand-010 : loop-cand-010 ‚Č° 3
law-cand-010 = refl
law-cand-011 : loop-cand-011 ‚Č° 5
law-cand-011 = refl
law-cand-100 : loop-cand-100 ‚Č° 6
law-cand-100 = refl
law-cand-101 : loop-cand-101 ‚Č° 8
law-cand-101 = refl
law-cand-110 : loop-cand-110 ‚Č° 9
law-cand-110 = refl
law-cand-111 : loop-cand-111 ‚Č° 11
law-cand-111 = refl

\end{code}

\subsubsection*{Filter 1: irreducibility}

This first filter removes every candidate that shares a factor with the QCD interaction volume. Only the two coprime survivors are allowed to reach the structural test.

\begin{code}

-- ¬ß Filter 1: irreducibility with 72 eliminates 0, 2, 3, 6, 8, and 9.
law-elim-000 : gcd loop-cand-001 loop-denom-QCD ‚Č° 2   -- Ōá=2 shares factor 2
law-elim-000 = refl
law-elim-010 : gcd loop-cand-010 loop-denom-QCD ‚Č° 3   -- d=3 shares factor 3
law-elim-010 = refl
law-elim-100 : gcd loop-cand-100 loop-denom-QCD ‚Č° 6   -- E=6 shares factor 6
law-elim-100 = refl
law-elim-101 : gcd loop-cand-101 loop-denom-QCD ‚Č° 8   -- E+Ōá=8 shares factor 8
law-elim-101 = refl
law-elim-110 : gcd loop-cand-110 loop-denom-QCD ‚Č° 9   -- E+d=9 shares factor 9
law-elim-110 = refl

-- ¬ß Pass irreducibility: d+Ōá=5 and E+d+Ōá=11
law-pass-011 : gcd loop-cand-011 loop-denom-QCD ‚Č° 1   -- d+Ōá=5 coprime ‚úď
law-pass-011 = refl
law-pass-111 : gcd loop-cand-111 loop-denom-QCD ‚Č° 1   -- E+d+Ōá=11 coprime ‚úď
law-pass-111 = refl

\end{code}

\subsubsection*{Filter 2: completeness}

The second filter now has an explicit internal shape: first the direct witness that $d + \chi$ omits the edge sector, then the number-theoretic argument showing that the omitted sector cannot be reconstructed from the partial sum.

\begin{code}

-- ¬ß Filter 2: completeness requires all three basis elements, so d+Ōá=5 is missing the E-sector.
law-partial-omits-E : loop-cand-011 + edgeCountK4 ‚Č° loop-cand-111
law-partial-omits-E = refl

-- ¬ß Equivalently: the full candidate is the partial plus the missing E-sector.
law-full-is-partial-plus-E : loop-cand-111 ‚Č° edgeCountK4 + loop-cand-011
law-full-is-partial-plus-E = refl

-- ¬ß After Filter 2: d+Ōá=5 is eliminated. Only E+d+Ōá=11 remains.

-- ¬ß Structural independence: E is multiplicatively independent of d+Ōá, so the edge stratum cannot be recovered by rescaling.
law-E-structurally-independent :
  gcd edgeCountK4 (degree-K4 + eulerChar-computed) ‚Č° 1
law-E-structurally-independent = refl  -- gcd(6, 5) = 1

-- ¬ß E = 6 spans the full {2,3} prime base inside the loop volume 72 = 2¬≥¬∑3¬≤.
law-E-spans-full-base : gcd edgeCountK4 (eulerChar-computed * degree-K4) ‚Č° 6
law-E-spans-full-base = refl  -- gcd(6, 6) = 6

-- ¬ß d = 3 contributes only the 3-stratum
law-d-in-3-stratum : gcd degree-K4 (eulerChar-computed * degree-K4) ‚Č° 3
law-d-in-3-stratum = refl  -- gcd(3, 6) = 3

-- ¬ß Ōá = 2 contributes only the 2-stratum
law-chi-in-2-stratum : gcd eulerChar-computed (eulerChar-computed * degree-K4) ‚Č° 2
law-chi-in-2-stratum = refl  -- gcd(2, 6) = 2

-- ¬ß The partial sum d+Ōá = 5 escapes the {2,3} stratum, leaving the E-stratum unaccounted for; only E+d+Ōá = 11 covers all three.
law-dchi-escapes-stratum :
  gcd (degree-K4 + eulerChar-computed) (eulerChar-computed * degree-K4) ‚Č° 1
law-dchi-escapes-stratum = refl  -- gcd(5, 6) = 1
\end{code}

\subsubsection*{Derivation of the completeness criterion}

The completeness requirement is not an axiom imposed from outside. It follows from three structural properties already established:

\begin{enumerate}
\item \textbf{Multiplicative identity.} The three loop basis elements satisfy $\chi \cdot d = E$ (\texttt{law-chi-times-d-is-E}). This is the unique non-trivial multiplicative relation among $\{E, d, \chi\}$: the edge count is the product of the Euler characteristic and the degree.

\item \textbf{Denominator factorization.} The interaction volume $D = V \cdot E \cdot d = 72$ factors through $E$. Substituting $E = \chi d$ yields $D = V \cdot \chi \cdot d^2$: the denominator already encodes both $\chi$ and $d$ multiplicatively, through the edge channel.

\item \textbf{Sum--product coprimality.} The partial sum $d + \chi = 5$ and the product $\chi \cdot d = E = 6$ are coprime: $\gcd(5, 6) = 1$ (\texttt{law-dchi-escapes-stratum}). Since $\chi$ and $d$ are themselves coprime ($\gcd(2, 3) = 1$), their sum and product share no factor---a number-theoretic necessity for coprime inputs.
\end{enumerate}

The consequence: the partial numerator $d + \chi$ provides the \emph{additive} combination of $\chi$ and $d$, but the denominator encodes the \emph{multiplicative} combination $\chi \cdot d = E$. These two projections of the same generators are coprime and therefore structurally disconnected. A correction fraction with $d + \chi$ in the numerator and $\chi \cdot d$ in the denominator treats two multiplicatively linked channels as if they were independent---the edge contribution to the volume has no counterpart in the numerator.

Including $E$ directly restores the connection: the full sum $E + d + \chi = 11$ references all three generators and its coprimality with $D = 72$ is verified. The completeness criterion is thus a consequence of the $K_4$ identity $\chi \cdot d = E$ together with the irreducibility constraint.

\subsubsection*{Formal completeness witness}

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Completeness derivation from Ōá¬∑d = E and the interaction-volume structure.
record CompletenessDerivation : Set where
  field
    -- Step 1: the multiplicative relation links all three generators
    relation            : eulerChar-computed * degree-K4 ‚Č° edgeCountK4
    -- The two generators Ōá and d are coprime
    generators-coprime  : gcd eulerChar-computed degree-K4 ‚Č° 1
    -- Step 2: the volume rewrites via Ōá¬∑d = E as D = V¬∑Ōá¬∑d¬≤
    volume-via-relation : (vertexCountK4 * eulerChar-computed) * (degree-K4 * degree-K4) ‚Č° loop-denom-QCD
    -- Step 3: sum and product of coprime generators are coprime
    sum-product-coprime : gcd (degree-K4 + eulerChar-computed) (eulerChar-computed * degree-K4) ‚Č° 1
    -- The partial numerator d+Ōá is coprime to E ‚ÄĒ structurally disconnected
    partial-blind-to-E  : gcd loop-cand-011 edgeCountK4 ‚Č° 1
    -- The full sum includes E alongside d+Ōá
    full-witnesses-E    : loop-cand-111 ‚Č° edgeCountK4 + loop-cand-011
    -- The full sum passes irreducibility
    full-irreducible    : gcd loop-cand-111 loop-denom-QCD ‚Č° 1

theorem-completeness-derivation : CompletenessDerivation
theorem-completeness-derivation = record
  { relation            = refl
  ; generators-coprime  = refl
  ; volume-via-relation = refl
  ; sum-product-coprime = refl
  ; partial-blind-to-E  = refl
  ; full-witnesses-E    = refl
  ; full-irreducible    = refl
  }
\end{code}

\subsubsection*{Filter 3 and final packaging}

The last stage does not introduce a new eliminator. It confirms that the same numerator survives when the denominator is enlarged by $\kappa$, and then packages the elimination result into a single record.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Filter 3: cross-scale consistency confirms that the same observable 11 survives at EW scale; it does not newly eliminate d+Ōá.
law-partial-EW  : gcd loop-cand-011 loop-denom-EW ‚Č° 1   -- 5 coprime to 576 too
law-partial-EW  = refl
law-pass-111-EW : gcd loop-cand-111 loop-denom-EW ‚Č° 1   -- 11 coprime to 576 ‚úď
law-pass-111-EW = refl

-- ¬ß Loop classification record certifying the unique admissible loop observable.
record LoopClassification : Set where
  field
    -- The forced value
    forced-value        : loop-numerator ‚Č° 11
    forced-from-K4      : loop-numerator ‚Č° edgeCountK4 + degree-K4 + eulerChar-computed

    -- Irreducibility at both scales
    irreducible-QCD     : gcd loop-numerator loop-denom-QCD ‚Č° 1
    irreducible-EW      : gcd loop-numerator loop-denom-EW ‚Č° 1

    -- All other candidates eliminated by irreducibility
    elim-chi            : gcd eulerChar-computed loop-denom-QCD ‚Č° 2
    elim-d              : gcd degree-K4 loop-denom-QCD ‚Č° 3
    elim-E              : gcd edgeCountK4 loop-denom-QCD ‚Č° 6
    elim-E-chi          : gcd (edgeCountK4 + eulerChar-computed) loop-denom-QCD ‚Č° 8
    elim-E-d            : gcd (edgeCountK4 + degree-K4) loop-denom-QCD ‚Č° 9

    -- The only other coprime candidate (d+Ōá=5) fails completeness
    partial-coprime     : gcd (degree-K4 + eulerChar-computed) loop-denom-QCD ‚Č° 1
    partial-incomplete  : degree-K4 + eulerChar-computed ‚Č° 5  -- omits E
    -- Formal completeness witness: the missing contribution is exactly E
    partial-omits-E     : loop-cand-011 + edgeCountK4 ‚Č° loop-cand-111

    -- Cross-scale confirmation: 5 also passes EW, so Filter 3 is not the eliminator
    partial-also-EW     : gcd loop-cand-011 loop-denom-EW ‚Č° 1

-- ¬ß Proof: every alternative is eliminated
theorem-loop-classification : LoopClassification
theorem-loop-classification = record
  { forced-value        = refl
  ; forced-from-K4      = refl
  ; irreducible-QCD     = refl
  ; irreducible-EW      = refl
  ; elim-chi            = refl
  ; elim-d              = refl
  ; elim-E              = refl
  ; elim-E-chi          = refl
  ; elim-E-d            = refl
  ; partial-coprime     = refl
  ; partial-incomplete  = refl
  ; partial-omits-E     = refl
  ; partial-also-EW     = refl
  }
\end{code}

%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
\section{Canonical interaction volumes}
\label{sec:discrete-continuum:canonical-interaction-volumes}
%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ

The forced observable provides the numerator. The denominator is not chosen---it is the canonical interaction volume at the relevant energy scale: the product of all independent graph coordinates that participate in the smearing.

At the QCD (hadron) scale, the interaction volume is the product of the three vertex-level graph coordinates: $V \times E \times d = 4 \times 6 \times 3 = 72$. Each factor is a primary $K_4$ invariant; their product counts the independent local configurations of the graph.

At the electroweak scale, the spectral width $\kappa$ opens an additional degree of freedom. The interaction volume becomes $V \times E \times d \times \kappa = 72 \times 8 = 576$. The factor $\kappa = 2(d{+}1) = 8$ is the same spectral constant already fixed in the preceding chapter. The scale ratio between regimes is a graph invariant, not a parameter.

\textbf{Constraint:} The denominator at each scale must be the canonical product of all graph coordinates active at that scale. No subset, no superset.

\textbf{Construction:} QCD volume $= V \cdot E \cdot d = 72$. Electroweak volume $= V \cdot E \cdot d \cdot \kappa = 576$. Their ratio is $\kappa = 8$.

\textbf{Consequence:} The denominators are determined by the same forcing principle as the numerator. They are not fit parameters but canonical products of forced invariants. The QCD and electroweak scales are related by $\kappa$, which also governs the spectral width and the CHSH bound---a single invariant linking three independent roles.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Canonical volume laws (definitions are above, in the loop classification block)

-- ¬ß Law: QCD denominator is exactly 72
law-loop-denom-QCD-72 : loop-denom-QCD ‚Č° 72
law-loop-denom-QCD-72 = refl

-- ¬ß Law: EW denominator is exactly 576
law-loop-denom-EW-576 : loop-denom-EW ‚Č° 576
law-loop-denom-EW-576 = refl

-- ¬ß The scale factor between QCD and EW is őļ
law-EW-scales-by-kappa : loop-denom-EW ‚Č° loop-denom-QCD * őļ-discrete
law-EW-scales-by-kappa = refl

-- ¬ß The RG slope denominator: 2őĪ = 274
rg-slope-denom : ‚Ąē
rg-slope-denom = 2 * őĪ-bare-K4

-- ¬ß Law: RG slope denominator is 274
law-rg-slope-274 : rg-slope-denom ‚Č° 274
law-rg-slope-274 = refl

-- ¬ß QCD denominator decomposes into named invariants
law-denom-QCD-from-K4 : loop-denom-QCD ‚Č° vertexCountK4 * edgeCountK4 * degree-K4
law-denom-QCD-from-K4 = refl

-- ¬ß RG slope decomposes into 2őĪ
law-rg-from-alpha : rg-slope-denom ‚Č° 2 * őĪ-bare-K4
law-rg-from-alpha = refl
\end{code}

%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
\section{Proton mass correction}
\label{sec:discrete-continuum:proton-mass-correction}
%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ

The measured proton-to-electron mass ratio is $1836.15267$ (CODATA 2022). The bare $K_4$ value is $1836$. The fractional part $0.15267$ is the QCD-scale correction forced by the loop observable:

$$
\text{correction} = \frac{\text{forced numerator}}{\text{QCD volume}} = \frac{E + d + \chi}{V \times E \times d} = \frac{11}{72} = 0.1527\overline{7}
$$

No parameter is chosen. The numerator is forced by elimination. The denominator is the canonical QCD interaction volume. The quotient is the canonical correction.

\textbf{Constraint:} The correction must use the QCD-scale volume (no spectral width factor $\kappa$, since the proton is a hadron).

\textbf{Construction:} $11/72 = 0.15277\ldots$ The corrected value is $1836 + 11/72 = 1836.1527\overline{7}$.

\textbf{Consequence:} The measured value $1836.15267$ agrees to six significant figures. The residual $|0.15278 - 0.15267| = 0.00011$ is of order $1/576$---exactly the next-order correction from the electroweak volume.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Proton loop correction numerator (same as universal)
proton-loop-num : ‚Ąē
proton-loop-num = loop-numerator

-- ¬ß Proton loop correction denominator (QCD scale)
proton-loop-den : ‚Ąē
proton-loop-den = loop-denom-QCD

-- ¬ß Proton correction as rational: 11/72
proton-loop : ‚Ąö
proton-loop = (+suc 10) / (‚Ąē-to-‚Ąē‚Āļ 71)     -- 11/72

-- ¬ß Corrected proton mass ratio as rational: 1836 + 11/72
proton-corrected : ‚Ąö
proton-corrected = (+suc 1835) / one‚Āļ +‚Ąö proton-loop

-- ¬ß The numerator is forced
law-proton-loop-num : proton-loop-num ‚Č° 11
law-proton-loop-num = refl

-- ¬ß The denominator is forced
law-proton-loop-den : proton-loop-den ‚Č° 72
law-proton-loop-den = refl

-- ¬ß Cross-check: numerator decomposes into named invariants
law-proton-loop-from-K4 :
  proton-loop-num ‚Č° edgeCountK4 + degree-K4 + eulerChar-computed
law-proton-loop-from-K4 = refl

-- ¬ß Cross-check: denominator decomposes into named invariants
law-proton-denom-from-K4 :
  proton-loop-den ‚Č° vertexCountK4 * edgeCountK4 * degree-K4
law-proton-denom-from-K4 = refl
\end{code}

%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
\section{Electroweak correction}
\label{sec:discrete-continuum:electroweak-correction}
%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ

The Weinberg mixing angle defines the ratio of electroweak coupling strengths. The tree-level value from $K_4$ is $\sin^2\theta_W = \chi/\kappa = 2/8 = 0.25$. The measured value is $\sin^2\theta_W = 0.2312$ (PDG 2024).

The correction is the electroweak-scale application of the same forced observable:

$$
\delta_{\text{EW}} = \frac{\text{forced numerator}}{\text{EW volume}} = \frac{E + d + \chi}{V \times E \times d \times \kappa} = \frac{11}{576} \approx 0.0191
$$

The corrected value is $0.25 - 11/576 = 0.2309$, within $0.1\%$ of the measured value.

\textbf{Constraint:} The electroweak correction must use the full canonical interaction volume including the spectral width $\kappa$.

\textbf{Construction:} $11/576 = 0.01909\overline{72}$. The corrected Weinberg angle is $\chi/\kappa - 11/576 = 2/8 - 11/576$.

\textbf{Consequence:} The same forced observable that corrects the proton mass also corrects the Weinberg angle. Only the canonical volume changes, by the factor $\kappa$. This is not cross-validation in the sense of an independent check: it is a structural consequence. The observable is forced. The volumes are canonical. The same quotient must appear at every scale---and it does.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Weinberg tree-level: Ōá/őļ
weinberg-tree-num : ‚Ąē
weinberg-tree-num = eulerChar-computed

weinberg-tree-den : ‚Ąē
weinberg-tree-den = őļ-discrete

-- ¬ß Law: tree-level Weinberg angle numerator/denominator
law-weinberg-tree : weinberg-tree-num ‚Č° 2
law-weinberg-tree = refl

law-weinberg-denom : weinberg-tree-den ‚Č° 8
law-weinberg-denom = refl

-- ¬ß Electroweak loop correction: 11/576
ew-loop : ‚Ąö
ew-loop = (+suc 10) / (‚Ąē-to-‚Ąē‚Āļ 575)     -- 11/576

-- ¬ß Weinberg tree-level as rational: 2/8
weinberg-tree : ‚Ąö
weinberg-tree = (+suc 1) / (‚Ąē-to-‚Ąē‚Āļ 7)  -- 2/8

-- ¬ß Corrected Weinberg angle: 2/8 ‚ąí 11/576
weinberg-corrected : ‚Ąö
weinberg-corrected = weinberg-tree -‚Ąö ew-loop

-- ¬ß The EW loop uses the same numerator as the proton loop
law-ew-same-numerator : loop-numerator ‚Č° proton-loop-num
law-ew-same-numerator = refl

-- ¬ß The EW denominator scales from QCD by őļ
law-ew-denom-from-QCD : loop-denom-EW ‚Č° proton-loop-den * őļ-discrete
law-ew-denom-from-QCD = refl
\end{code}



%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
\section{The correction chain}
\label{sec:discrete-continuum:correction-chain}
%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ

The corrections are not added by hand. They arise from a chain of canonical maps, each forced by the elimination of alternatives:

\begin{enumerate}
\item \textbf{Graph.} $K_4$ is the unique fixpoint of the classification-and-elimination process (Parts~I--III). No other graph passes the filter.

\item \textbf{Observable.} Among all functions on the drift ledger, only those that are presentation-independent, automorphism-invariant, and base-factorable qualify as admissible observables (Part~IV). Every admissible observable reduces to its canonical form via $\texttt{canonObs}$. In the concrete $K_4$ physics layer, the primitive global sector then collapses further to four classes only: boundary, correlator, observer, and channels.

\item \textbf{Loop observable.} Among all $\{0,1\}$-linear loop observables over $\{E, d, \chi\}$, exactly one satisfies irreducibility, completeness, and cross-scale consistency: $E + d + \chi = 11$. This is not a fifth primitive global observable but a uniquely surviving derived observable built from the forced invariant ledger. Every alternative is eliminated.

\item \textbf{Canonical volume.} The interaction volume at each scale is the product of all active graph coordinates: $V \!\cdot\! E \!\cdot\! d = 72$ at QCD, $V \!\cdot\! E \!\cdot\! d \!\cdot\! \kappa = 576$ at the electroweak scale. These are derived ledger volumes, not additional primitive observables. No subset and no superset gives a canonically complete volume.

\item \textbf{Correction.} The discrete-to-continuum bridge is the canonical quotient: forced numerator / canonical volume. This is the unique map from the discrete graph to the rational correction, and it uses only forced ingredients at every step.
\end{enumerate}

Each link in this chain is a canonical map. No link introduces a free parameter, and no step quietly enlarges the primitive observable basis. The chain is therefore itself canonical---and the rational corrections are its output. The next theorem does not add a new ingredient. It packages this already established chain into a single uniqueness statement about the discrete--continuum passage itself.

Figure~\ref{fig:observable-classes-to-corrections} pulls the same chain slightly closer to the eye of the physicist. The four primitive observable classes are not yet the corrections. They are the knobs that survive after every fake knob has been removed. Once those four remain, the loop numerator and the channel volume are no longer adjustable pieces. They lock together and the two correction fractions fall out.

\begin{figure}[h]
\centering
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=Latex, node distance=2.5cm]
  \node[concept] (b) {boundary};
  \node[concept, right=1.7cm of b] (c) {correlator};
  \node[concept, right=1.7cm of c] (o) {observer};
  \node[concept, right=1.7cm of o] (ch) {channels};

  \node[derives, below=1.8cm of $(b)!0.5!(c)$] (num) {loop numerator\\$11$};
  \node[derives, below=1.8cm of $(o)!0.5!(ch)$] (vol) {canonical volume\\$72,\;576$};

  \node[concept, below=2.1cm of $(num)!0.5!(vol)$] (corr) {corrections\\$11/72,\;11/576$};

  \draw[flow] (b.south) -- ($(num.north)+(-0.55,0)$);
  \draw[flow] (c.south) -- ($(num.north)+(0.55,0)$);
  \draw[flow] (o.south) -- ($(vol.north)+(-0.55,0)$);
  \draw[flow] (ch.south) -- ($(vol.north)+(0.55,0)$);
  \draw[flow] (num) -- node[label, left] {forced quotient} (corr);
  \draw[flow] (vol) -- node[label, right] {forced quotient} (corr);
\end{tikzpicture}
\caption{From primitive observable classes to continuum corrections. Once the surviving knobs are fixed, the correction fractions are no longer tuned but read off.}
\label{fig:observable-classes-to-corrections}
\end{figure}

\subsubsection*{Admissible real-valued observables and quotient presentation}

The continuum cannot be allowed to enter as an anonymous decimal haze. Part~IV already forced admissible observables to collapse to canonical base content. Part~V now also forces the rational quotient data to remain globally visible: every admissible rational observable carries unique pointwise quotient fields, and any reduced witness is unique whenever it exists. If these ingredients are not explicitly tied together here, the reader still sees only the final fractions and is left to reconstruct the closure by hand.

This chapter therefore no longer has to invent a quotient theory locally. It only fixes the real-valued closure of the already forced rational core. A bridge observable is not an arbitrary function into $\mathbb{R}$. It is a real quantity whose surviving content already comes from an admissible rational core and reaches the continuum only through the forced closure map $\mathbb{Q} \to \mathbb{R}$. The only extra pressure specific to physics is irreducibility: common factors would mean that the correction is still repeating structure already counted in the canonical volume.

\textbf{Constraint:} A continuum quantity may not introduce fresh observational freedom beyond the admissible and canonically factored content already surviving on the discrete side. If it did, the bridge would cease to be a closure and become a new source of parameters.

\textbf{Construction:} Reuse the global class of admissible rational observables from Part~V, add the corresponding real-valued codomain, and admit only those real observables that are exactly the $\mathbb{Q}$-to-$\mathbb{R}$ closure of an admissible rational core. Quotient presentation is therefore inherited rather than postulated; irreducibility is then imposed where a correction fraction is claimed.

\textbf{Consequence:} The discrete--continuum map no longer appears as a jump from integers to decimals. It becomes locally visible as a forced chain: admissible observable, canonical rational quotient, irreducibility filter, real closure. The correction theorem below can then speak about uniqueness without hiding where numerator and denominator come from.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Phase 1: Admit only real observables closed from rational ones
record ‚ĄĚŌČ : SetŌČ where
  constructor wrap‚ĄĚ
  field
    lower‚ĄĚ : ‚ĄĚ

open ‚ĄĚŌČ public

wrap‚Ąöto‚ĄĚ : ‚ĄöŌČ ‚Üí ‚ĄĚŌČ
wrap‚Ąöto‚ĄĚ q = wrap‚ĄĚ (‚Ąöto‚ĄĚ (lower‚Ąö q))

AdmissibleRealObservable : SetŌČ
AdmissibleRealObservable = AdmissibleObservable ‚ĄĚŌČ

-- ¬ß A bridge-real observable is a real observable induced from an admissible rational core.
record QuotientPresentedRealObservable : SetŌČ where
  field
    rational-core : AdmissibleRationalObservable
    real-value    : AdmissibleRealObservable
    closes-rationally :
      (x : DriftState‚Ąē) ‚Üí
      lower‚ĄĚ (obs real-value x) ‚ČÉ‚ĄĚ wrap‚Ąöto‚ĄĚ (obs rational-core x) .lower‚ĄĚ

-- ¬ß Phase 2: Fix the rational correction data first

-- ¬ß The two bridge corrections are already irreducible at the rational layer.
proton-loop-irreducible : IrreducibleQuotient proton-loop
proton-loop-irreducible = refl

ew-loop-irreducible : IrreducibleQuotient ew-loop
ew-loop-irreducible = refl

-- ¬ß Both bridge corrections have nonnegative numerators.
proton-loop-nonnegative : 0‚Ą§ ‚Č§‚Ą§ num proton-loop
proton-loop-nonnegative = tt

ew-loop-nonnegative : 0‚Ą§ ‚Č§‚Ą§ num ew-loop
ew-loop-nonnegative = tt

-- ¬ß Canonical reduced witness for a positive correction quotient.
proton-loop-reduced : ReducedRationalWitness proton-loop
proton-loop-reduced =
  irreducible-nonnegative-witness proton-loop proton-loop-irreducible proton-loop-nonnegative

ew-loop-reduced : ReducedRationalWitness ew-loop
ew-loop-reduced =
  irreducible-nonnegative-witness ew-loop ew-loop-irreducible ew-loop-nonnegative

-- ¬ß Canonical quotient-level reduced forms for the two bridge corrections.
proton-loop-quotient-form : ReducedRationalForm proton-loop
proton-loop-quotient-form =
  canonical-reduction-form-on-irreducible-nonnegative
    proton-loop proton-loop-irreducible proton-loop-nonnegative

ew-loop-quotient-form : ReducedRationalForm ew-loop
ew-loop-quotient-form =
  canonical-reduction-form-on-irreducible-nonnegative
    ew-loop ew-loop-irreducible ew-loop-nonnegative

proton-loop-reduced-unique :
  (r : ReducedRationalWitness proton-loop) ‚Üí
  ReducedRationalWitness.red-num r ‚Č° 11
  √ó ReducedRationalWitness.red-den r ‚Č° ‚Ąē-to-‚Ąē‚Āļ 71
proton-loop-reduced-unique r =
  let pair = reduced-rational-witness-unique r proton-loop-reduced in
  fst pair , snd pair

ew-loop-reduced-unique :
  (r : ReducedRationalWitness ew-loop) ‚Üí
  ReducedRationalWitness.red-num r ‚Č° 11
  √ó ReducedRationalWitness.red-den r ‚Č° ‚Ąē-to-‚Ąē‚Āļ 575
ew-loop-reduced-unique r =
  let pair = reduced-rational-witness-unique r ew-loop-reduced in
  fst pair , snd pair

proton-loop-quotient-form-forced :
  ReducedRationalForm.form-num proton-loop-quotient-form ‚Č° 11
  √ó ReducedRationalForm.form-den proton-loop-quotient-form ‚Č° ‚Ąē-to-‚Ąē‚Āļ 71
proton-loop-quotient-form-forced =
  let
    cand =
      canonical-reduction-on-irreducible-nonnegative
        proton-loop proton-loop-irreducible proton-loop-nonnegative
    red = proton-loop-reduced-unique proton-loop-reduced
  in
  trans (fst (snd cand)) (fst red)
  , trans (snd (snd cand)) (snd red)

-- ¬ß Phase 3: Read off the forced reduced normal forms

ew-loop-quotient-form-forced :
  ReducedRationalForm.form-num ew-loop-quotient-form ‚Č° 11
  √ó ReducedRationalForm.form-den ew-loop-quotient-form ‚Č° ‚Ąē-to-‚Ąē‚Āļ 575
ew-loop-quotient-form-forced =
  let
    cand =
      canonical-reduction-on-irreducible-nonnegative
        ew-loop ew-loop-irreducible ew-loop-nonnegative
    red = ew-loop-reduced-unique ew-loop-reduced
  in
  trans (fst (snd cand)) (fst red)
  , trans (snd (snd cand)) (snd red)
\end{code}

\subsubsection*{Theorem: Uniqueness of the Discrete--Continuum Map}

At this point the bridge can no longer be described merely as a successful recipe. If the passage from the discrete ledger to the continuum correction admitted two inequivalent realizations, the entire chapter would lose force at the last moment. The graph could still be rigid, the admissibility filter could still be sharp, and yet the measured correction would depend on representational wording. That fork is exactly what the preceding elimination chain has already destroyed.

Nothing new is introduced here. The theorem only hardens what the chapter has already forced in pieces. The admissible observable content is first collapsed to a canonical rational core. That core already carries quotient fields. In the correction sector the numerator is then classified uniquely, the scale fixes the canonical denominator uniquely, and irreducibility blocks any common-factor slack.
