Once those ingredients are fixed, the real closure cannot branch without reintroducing a parameter the chapter has already eliminated.

\textbf{Constraint:} A discrete--continuum map is admissible only if it uses admissible observable content, passes through the canonical rational quotient layer, and then closes into $\mathbb{R}$ without adding new structure. In the correction sector this means: unique surviving loop numerator, unique canonical interaction volume, and irreducibility of the resulting fraction.

\textbf{Construction:} Package the bridge as a scale-indexed map with two cases only: the QCD correction $11/72$ and the electroweak correction $11/576$. The numerator is shared because the loop observable is unique. The denominator changes only by the already forced spectral factor $\kappa$. The quotient fields are then fixed by the rational values themselves, and irreducibility is verified inside the correction sector.

The canonical reduced witnesses below make this visible in the sharpest local form the chapter currently supports: each correction is not only a rational quotient, but a quotient already written in its surviving reduced presentation.

\textbf{Consequence:} The discrete--continuum passage does not fork. At every scale treated in this chapter, the bridge is fixed by the unique numerator together with the canonical volume, and the real layer contributes only closure, not new freedom. The specific corrections $11/72$ and $11/576$ are therefore not chosen outputs. They are the unique irreducible rational quotients forced by the discrete record at the treated scales.

\phantomsection\label{law:discrete-continuum:map-unique}

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß The discrete-continuum bridge is indexed only by the active scale.
data CorrectionScale : Set where
  qcd-scale : CorrectionScale
  ew-scale  : CorrectionScale

-- ¬ß The canonical interaction volume at each scale.
canonical-volume-at : CorrectionScale ‚Üí ‚Ąē
canonical-volume-at qcd-scale = loop-denom-QCD
canonical-volume-at ew-scale  = loop-denom-EW

-- ¬ß The bridge outputs the forced rational correction at each scale.
discrete-continuum-map : CorrectionScale ‚Üí ‚Ąö
discrete-continuum-map qcd-scale = proton-loop
discrete-continuum-map ew-scale  = ew-loop

-- ¬ß The bridge is unique because both numerator and canonical volume are already fixed.
record DiscreteContinuumMapUniqueness : Set where
  field
    unique-loop-numerator : (s : CorrectionScale) ‚Üí loop-numerator ‚Č° 11
    qcd-volume-is-canonical : canonical-volume-at qcd-scale ‚Č° 72
    ew-volume-is-canonical : canonical-volume-at ew-scale ‚Č° 576
    qcd-quotient-is-irreducible : IrreducibleQuotient proton-loop
    ew-quotient-is-irreducible : IrreducibleQuotient ew-loop
    qcd-reduced-form : ReducedRationalWitness proton-loop
    ew-reduced-form : ReducedRationalWitness ew-loop
    qcd-quotient-normal-form : ReducedRationalForm proton-loop
    ew-quotient-normal-form : ReducedRationalForm ew-loop
    qcd-map-uses-forced-data :
      proton-loop-num ‚Č° loop-numerator √ó proton-loop-den ‚Č° canonical-volume-at qcd-scale
    ew-map-uses-forced-data :
      loop-numerator ‚Č° 11 √ó loop-denom-EW ‚Č° canonical-volume-at ew-scale
    qcd-quotient-fields-unique :
      (n‚āĀ n‚āā : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí (d‚āĀ d‚āā : ‚Ąē‚Āļ) ‚Üí
      proton-loop ‚Č° (n‚āĀ / d‚āĀ) ‚Üí
      proton-loop ‚Č° (n‚āā / d‚āā) ‚Üí
      (n‚āĀ ‚Č° n‚āā) √ó (d‚āĀ ‚Č° d‚āā)
    ew-quotient-fields-unique :
      (n‚āĀ n‚āā : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí (d‚āĀ d‚āā : ‚Ąē‚Āļ) ‚Üí
      ew-loop ‚Č° (n‚āĀ / d‚āĀ) ‚Üí
      ew-loop ‚Č° (n‚āā / d‚āā) ‚Üí
      (n‚āĀ ‚Č° n‚āā) √ó (d‚āĀ ‚Č° d‚āā)
    qcd-reduced-form-unique :
      (r : ReducedRationalWitness proton-loop) ‚Üí
      ReducedRationalWitness.red-num r ‚Č° 11
      √ó ReducedRationalWitness.red-den r ‚Č° ‚Ąē-to-‚Ąē‚Āļ 71
    ew-reduced-form-unique :
      (r : ReducedRationalWitness ew-loop) ‚Üí
      ReducedRationalWitness.red-num r ‚Č° 11
      √ó ReducedRationalWitness.red-den r ‚Č° ‚Ąē-to-‚Ąē‚Āļ 575
    qcd-quotient-normal-form-forced :
      ReducedRationalForm.form-num qcd-quotient-normal-form ‚Č° 11
      √ó ReducedRationalForm.form-den qcd-quotient-normal-form ‚Č° ‚Ąē-to-‚Ąē‚Āļ 71
    ew-quotient-normal-form-forced :
      ReducedRationalForm.form-num ew-quotient-normal-form ‚Č° 11
      √ó ReducedRationalForm.form-den ew-quotient-normal-form ‚Č° ‚Ąē-to-‚Ąē‚Āļ 575
    qcd-map-is-unique : discrete-continuum-map qcd-scale ‚ČÉ‚Ąö proton-loop
    ew-map-is-unique : discrete-continuum-map ew-scale ‚ČÉ‚Ąö ew-loop

theorem-discrete-continuum-map-unique : DiscreteContinuumMapUniqueness
theorem-discrete-continuum-map-unique = record
  { unique-loop-numerator = őĽ where
      qcd-scale ‚Üí refl
      ew-scale  ‚Üí refl
  ; qcd-volume-is-canonical = refl
  ; ew-volume-is-canonical = refl
  ; qcd-quotient-is-irreducible = proton-loop-irreducible
  ; ew-quotient-is-irreducible = ew-loop-irreducible
  ; qcd-reduced-form = proton-loop-reduced
  ; ew-reduced-form = ew-loop-reduced
  ; qcd-quotient-normal-form = proton-loop-quotient-form
  ; ew-quotient-normal-form = ew-loop-quotient-form
  ; qcd-map-uses-forced-data = refl , refl
  ; ew-map-uses-forced-data = refl , refl
  ; qcd-quotient-fields-unique = rational-fields-unique proton-loop
  ; ew-quotient-fields-unique = rational-fields-unique ew-loop
  ; qcd-reduced-form-unique = proton-loop-reduced-unique
  ; ew-reduced-form-unique = ew-loop-reduced-unique
  ; qcd-quotient-normal-form-forced = proton-loop-quotient-form-forced
  ; ew-quotient-normal-form-forced = ew-loop-quotient-form-forced
  ; qcd-map-is-unique = ‚ČÉ‚Ąö-refl proton-loop
  ; ew-map-is-unique = ‚ČÉ‚Ąö-refl ew-loop
  }
\end{code}

The theorem and the following diagram say the same thing at two different levels: the code packages the bridge as a unique scale-indexed map, and the diagram makes visible which forced inputs leave that map with no room to branch.

The following diagram summarizes the five links:

\begin{figure}[h]
\centering
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=Latex, node distance=2.8cm]
  \node[concept] (G)  {$K_4$\\{\small graph}};
  \node[concept, right of=G]  (O) {obs\\{\small admissible}};
  \node[concept, right of=O]  (L) {$11$\\{\small loop}};
  \node[concept, right of=L]  (V) {$72,\,576$\\{\small volume}};
  \node[derives, right of=V]  (C) {$\tfrac{11}{72},\,\tfrac{11}{576}$\\{\small correction}};
  \draw[flow] (G) -- node[label, above] {Parts I--III} (O);
  \draw[flow] (O) -- node[label, above] {Part IV} (L);
  \draw[flow] (L) -- node[label, above] {$V\!\cdot\!E\!\cdot\!d$} (V);
  \draw[flow] (V) -- node[label, above] {quotient} (C);
\end{tikzpicture}
\caption{The correction chain.  Each arrow is a canonical map forced by elimination.}
\label{fig:correction-chain}
\end{figure}

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{llccc}
\toprule
\textbf{Derived quantity} & \textbf{Bare} & \textbf{Numerator} & \textbf{Derived volume} & \textbf{Correction} \\
\midrule
Proton mass ratio & $1836$  & $11$ & $72 = V \!\cdot\! E \!\cdot\! d$ & $+11/72$ \\
Weinberg angle    & $0.25$  & $11$ & $576 = V \!\cdot\! E \!\cdot\! d \!\cdot\! \kappa$ & $-11/576$ \\
RG slope          & ---     & $1$  & $274 = 2\alpha^{-1}$ & $1/274$ \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

The table now reads as a corollary of the theorem rather than as an isolated summary. Proton and electroweak corrections share the same forced loop numerator~$11$ because the loop sector survives elimination only once and is then evaluated at two canonical volumes. The RG slope belongs to a different derived channel: its numerator is fixed at~$1$, while its denominator is forced by the already classified coupling value $\alpha^{-1} = 137$. In each case, the continuum correction is downstream of already fixed discrete data.

%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
\section{Classification of Derived Sectors}
\label{sec:discrete-continuum:classification-derived-sectors}
%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ

The preceding sections fixed the admissibility filter and the primitive observable basis. But the physics chapter does not end at that basis. It must still show how the derived sectors built from the ledger survive elimination. This section performs that harsher census: not of new primitive observables, but of the derived candidate spaces for coupling, mass, loop numerator, and correction volume. In each case the task is the same. A finite sector is exposed, weaker survivors are shattered, and exactly one derived quantity remains.

\subsection{Classification of \texorpdfstring{$\alpha^{-1} = 137$}{őĪ‚ĀĽ¬Ļ = 137}}
\label{sec:discrete-continuum:classification-alpha-137}

The value $\alpha^{-1} = 137$ does not enter here as a chosen formula. By the stage at which it is classified, the construction is already closed in the only places where hidden freedom could have entered: the graph is fixed as $K_4$, the invariant ledger $(V,E,d,\chi,\kappa)$ is fixed, and the admissibility filter has already eliminated presentation-dependent, non-canonical, and proof-sensitive quantities. The remaining task is therefore not to pick a coupling value, but to expose the unique survivor in the derived coupling sector.

\textbf{Constraint:} The coupling value must remain inside the admissible derived sector determined by the earlier filter. Any candidate must be invariant under $\mathrm{Aut}(K_4)$, factor through the canonical invariant basis already fixed, and stay within the graph-forced local complexity bound. If one relaxes any of these conditions, the value is no longer derived from the surviving structure but from reintroduced representational slack.

\textbf{Construction:} Classification first eliminates every single monomial in the invariant basis as a possible source of $137$, then classifies all degree-$\le 3$ admissible two-term completions. Exactly one decomposition survives: $128 + 9 = V^3\chi + d^2 = V^2\kappa + d^2$.

\textbf{Consequence:} The value is not fitted to experiment and not selected by taste. It is the unique survivor of the admissibility constraints in the bounded derived coupling sector. Any alternative value would require either non-invariant structure, non-canonical observables, or restored representational freedom, and each of those has already been eliminated upstream.

The coupling constant $\alpha^{-1} = 137$ is prime. No single monomial in the $K_4$ invariants $\{V{=}4,\, E{=}6,\, d{=}3,\, \chi{=}2,\, \kappa{=}8\}$ of degree $\le 3$ evaluates to~$137$, because all such monomials factor through $2$, $3$, or both. The value is therefore not a coincidence of a single structural factor; it requires at least two terms.

Among all pairs $(M_1, M_2)$ where each $M_i$ is a monomial of degree $\le 3$ in the $K_4$ invariants, the decomposition $M_1 + M_2 = 137$ has exactly one solution: $128 + 9$. The first term $128 = V^3 \chi = V^2 \kappa$ combines spectral scaling with topology; the second term $9 = d^2$ is the geometric square of the vertex degree. No other pair of degree-$\le 3$ monomials sums to $137$.

\subsubsection*{Prime exclusion and the surviving pair}

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Classification: 137 is not a monomial in K‚āĄ invariants
law-137-coprime-to-V : gcd 137 vertexCountK4 ‚Č° 1
law-137-coprime-to-V = refl

law-137-coprime-to-E : gcd 137 edgeCountK4 ‚Č° 1
law-137-coprime-to-E = refl

law-137-coprime-to-d : gcd 137 degree-K4 ‚Č° 1
law-137-coprime-to-d = refl

law-137-coprime-to-chi : gcd 137 eulerChar-computed ‚Č° 1
law-137-coprime-to-chi = refl

law-137-coprime-to-kappa : gcd 137 őļ-discrete ‚Č° 1
law-137-coprime-to-kappa = refl

-- ¬ß The spectral-topological bulk term: V¬≥Ōá = 128
bulk-spectral : ‚Ąē
bulk-spectral = (vertexCountK4 ^ 3) * eulerChar-computed

law-bulk-128 : bulk-spectral ‚Č° 128
law-bulk-128 = refl

-- ¬ß Alternative expression: V¬≤őļ = 128 (same value, different route)
law-bulk-alt : (vertexCountK4 ^ 2) * őļ-discrete ‚Č° 128
law-bulk-alt = refl

-- ¬ß The geometric degree term: d¬≤ = 9
geometric-sq : ‚Ąē
geometric-sq = degree-K4 * degree-K4

law-geometric-9 : geometric-sq ‚Č° 9
law-geometric-9 = refl

-- ¬ß The unique binomial decomposition: 128 + 9 = 137
law-coupling-decomposition : bulk-spectral + geometric-sq ‚Č° 137
law-coupling-decomposition = refl

\end{code}

\subsubsection*{Degree bound and two-term necessity}

The remaining laws explain why the search stops at degree three and why a single monomial cannot hit the prime value $137$. This keeps the bound as a structural statement rather than a hidden search parameter.

The justification for the degree bound is structural, not merely numerical. One cannot simply say that every monomial of degree at least four already exceeds $137$: that statement is false in raw arithmetic, since expressions such as $\chi^4 = 16$, $\chi^4 d = 48$, $\chi^4 E = 96$, and $\chi^4 V = 64$ remain below $137$. The real point is different. The graph itself fixes the natural local complexity scale: $K_4$ has degree $d = 3$, and this same value appears as the simplex depth used in the coupling law. Monomials of degree at most three therefore exhaust the primitive local observables naturally generated by the graph's own interaction depth. Going beyond degree three does not reveal a new local layer of $K_4$; it merely iterates already available invariants into higher-order composites.

This matters for the epistemic status of the argument. At the standard set by the beginning of the book, elimination must always act on an explicitly delimited class of alternatives. That is exactly what happens here: the sector under classification is the class of primitive local observables on $K_4$, and the graph's own degree fixes that class at complexity $\le 3$. Within that class, the arithmetic classification becomes sharp. The only admissible two-term completion of the prime value $137$ is $128 + 9 = V^3\chi + d^2$. Higher-degree terms are therefore not being ruled out as meaningless formal expressions in general; they are being ruled out from this particular elimination problem because they lie outside the primitive observable sector being classified.

This cutoff cannot remain a verbal convention. If the primitive sector were left implicit, the bound $\deg \le d$ could be mistaken for a search parameter chosen after $137$ was already visible. The graph itself blocks that escape. The simplex depth is the local interaction depth of $K_4$, and \hyperref[law:k4:simplex-degree-match]{Law~16B.5} identifies that depth with the graph degree. Primitive observables therefore stop exactly where iteration begins.

\textbf{Constraint:} The admissible local sector must be delimited by graph structure alone. A bound introduced only to hit $137$ would destroy the elimination claim.

\textbf{Construction:} A primitive observable is represented by a monomial in the invariant basis $\{V,E,d,\chi,\kappa\}$ together with a proof that its total degree does not exceed $\texttt{simplex-degree}$. Since $\texttt{simplex-degree} \equiv d$, the cutoff is read directly from $K_4$.

\textbf{Consequence:} Degree-$4$ terms such as $\chi^4$ are not forbidden as expressions. They are classified as iterated composites outside the primitive sector. The $\alpha^{-1}$ decomposition survives this filter: $V^2\kappa$ has degree $3$, and $d^2$ has degree $2$.

\begin{code}

-- ¬ß A local observable monomial is specified by exponents on the K‚āĄ invariant basis.
record ObservableMonomial : Set where
  constructor mono
  field
    exp-V : ‚Ąē
    exp-E : ‚Ąē
    exp-d : ‚Ąē
    exp-Ōá : ‚Ąē
    exp-őļ : ‚Ąē

open ObservableMonomial public

-- ¬ß Total monomial degree counts local complexity in the invariant basis.
monomial-degree : ObservableMonomial ‚Üí ‚Ąē
monomial-degree m =
  exp-V m + exp-E m + exp-d m + exp-Ōá m + exp-őļ m

-- ¬ß Evaluation sends an exponent profile to its K‚āĄ integer value.
eval-monomial : ObservableMonomial ‚Üí ‚Ąē
eval-monomial m =
  (((vertexCountK4 ^ exp-V m)
    * (edgeCountK4 ^ exp-E m))
    * (degree-K4 ^ exp-d m)
    * (eulerChar-computed ^ exp-Ōá m))
    * (őļ-discrete ^ exp-őļ m)

-- ¬ß Primitive observables are exactly the monomials whose degree stays within simplex depth.
PrimitiveLocalMonomial : ObservableMonomial ‚Üí Set
PrimitiveLocalMonomial m = monomial-degree m ‚Č§ simplex-degree

-- ¬ß The bulk term is primitive as V¬≤¬∑őļ, which is degree 3.
bulk-primitive-monomial : ObservableMonomial
bulk-primitive-monomial = mono 2 0 0 0 1

-- ¬ß The geometric completion is the degree-square monomial d¬≤.
degree-square-monomial : ObservableMonomial
degree-square-monomial = mono 0 0 2 0 0

-- ¬ß Ōá‚Āī is the first explicit example beyond the primitive cutoff.
quartic-chi-monomial : ObservableMonomial
quartic-chi-monomial = mono 0 0 0 4 0

-- ¬ß Anything above the cutoff is not primitive.
primitive-vs-iterated-impossible :
  {m : ObservableMonomial} ‚Üí
  suc simplex-degree ‚Č§ monomial-degree m ‚Üí
  PrimitiveLocalMonomial m ‚Üí
  ‚ä•
primitive-vs-iterated-impossible {m} high low =
  suc‚Č§-impossible simplex-degree (‚Č§-trans high low)

-- ¬ß The primitive observable sector is fixed by the graph's local depth.
record PrimitiveObservableSector : Set1 where
  field
    cutoff-from-graph : simplex-degree ‚Č° degree-K4
    cutoff-is-structural : simplex-degree ‚Č° 3
    admissible : ObservableMonomial ‚Üí Set
    admissible-exactly-bounded :
      (m : ObservableMonomial) ‚Üí
      (admissible m ‚Üí monomial-degree m ‚Č§ simplex-degree)
      √ó (monomial-degree m ‚Č§ simplex-degree ‚Üí admissible m)
    above-cutoff-is-iterated :
      (m : ObservableMonomial) ‚Üí
      suc simplex-degree ‚Č§ monomial-degree m ‚Üí
      ¬¨ (admissible m)
    bulk-is-primitive : admissible bulk-primitive-monomial
    bulk-evaluates-to-128 : eval-monomial bulk-primitive-monomial ‚Č° 128
    degree-square-is-primitive : admissible degree-square-monomial
    degree-square-evaluates-to-9 : eval-monomial degree-square-monomial ‚Č° 9
    quartic-chi-begins-iteration :
      suc simplex-degree ‚Č§ monomial-degree quartic-chi-monomial
    quartic-chi-is-not-primitive : ¬¨ (admissible quartic-chi-monomial)

theorem-primitive-observable-sector : PrimitiveObservableSector
theorem-primitive-observable-sector = record
  { cutoff-from-graph = sym law16B-5-degree-match
  ; cutoff-is-structural = law14C-1-degree
  ; admissible = PrimitiveLocalMonomial
    ; admissible-exactly-bounded = őĽ m ‚Üí (őĽ adm ‚Üí adm) , (őĽ bound ‚Üí bound)
  ; above-cutoff-is-iterated =
      őĽ m high adm ‚Üí primitive-vs-iterated-impossible {m = m} high adm
  ; bulk-is-primitive = s‚Č§s (s‚Č§s (s‚Č§s z‚Č§n))
  ; bulk-evaluates-to-128 = refl
  ; degree-square-is-primitive = s‚Č§s (s‚Č§s z‚Č§n)
  ; degree-square-evaluates-to-9 = refl
  ; quartic-chi-begins-iteration = s‚Č§s (s‚Č§s (s‚Č§s (s‚Č§s z‚Č§n)))
  ; quartic-chi-is-not-primitive =
      őĽ adm ‚Üí
        primitive-vs-iterated-impossible {m = quartic-chi-monomial}
          (s‚Č§s (s‚Č§s (s‚Č§s (s‚Č§s z‚Č§n))))
          adm
  }

-- ¬ß Only 128 pairs with 9 to reach 137 within the degree-‚Č§3 sector.

-- ¬ß Structural derivation: the degree bound d = simplex-degree = 3 is the spectral depth of K4, so V^d¬∑Ōá + d¬≤ is evaluated exactly at that depth.
law-alpha-exponent-is-simplex-degree :
  alpha-inverse ‚Č°   (simplex-vertices ^ simplex-degree) * simplex-chi
                  + simplex-degree * simplex-degree
law-alpha-exponent-is-simplex-degree = refl

-- ¬ß Structural reason for two terms: all K4 monomials are {2,3}-smooth, but 137 is coprime to 6, so no single monomial can equal it.
law-alpha-exceeds-monomial-stratum :
  gcd alpha-inverse (eulerChar-computed * degree-K4) ‚Č° 1
law-alpha-exceeds-monomial-stratum = refl  -- gcd(137, 6) = 1

-- ¬ß The completion 137 ‚ął 128 = 9 = d¬≤ is the unique degree-‚Č§3 monomial that fits.
law-completion-unique : alpha-inverse ‚ął bulk-spectral ‚Č° geometric-sq
law-completion-unique = refl  -- 137 ‚ął 128 = 9 = d¬≤
\end{code}



\subsection{Epistemic status}
\label{sec:discrete-continuum:epistemic-status}

The classification results above establish the following census:

\begin{enumerate}
\item \textbf{$\alpha^{-1} = 137$: forced by elimination.} Once the graph, invariant ledger, and primitive cutoff are fixed, the coupling sector contains exactly one admissible survivor. Among all two-term degree-$\le 3$ completions, only $V^d \chi + d^2 = 128 + 9$ reaches the prime value. No alternative passes the filter.

\item \textbf{Loop numerator $11$: forced by elimination.} The loop sector is finite and explicit. Among all $\{0,1\}$-linear combinations of $\{E, d, \chi\}$, exactly one satisfies irreducibility ($\gcd = 1$ with~$72$), completeness, and cross-scale consistency. The partial candidate $d + \chi = 5$ collapses on completeness. Only $E + d + \chi = 11$ survives.

\item \textbf{Denominators $72$, $576$, $274$: derived.} These do not require a separate survivor hunt. They are canonical products of quantities already forced upstream: $V \!\cdot\! E \!\cdot\! d$, $V \!\cdot\! E \!\cdot\! d \!\cdot\! \kappa$, $2\alpha^{-1}$. Once the factors survive, the products survive with them.

\item \textbf{Proton bare mass $1836$: forced by elimination after reduction.} The mass sector separates compactification content from local graph content. The compactified spinor survivor is already fixed at $F_2 = 17$, and the remaining cofactor reduces through $\chi d = E$ to the primitive edge channel. There, among all degree-$\le 3$ monomials in $\{E,d\}$, exactly one evaluates to $108$, namely $E^2 d$. The full product $F_2 \cdot E^2 d = 1836$ is therefore forced.

\item \textbf{Muon bare mass $207$: forced by elimination.} The muon sector survives only if its two channels remain distinct: geometric square and mixed compactification sum. Among the degree-only candidates $1,d,d^2,d^3$, exactly one evaluates to $9$. Among the additive candidates $0,E,F_2,E+F_2$, exactly one evaluates to $23$. Their product is therefore forced as $9 \cdot 23 = 207$.

\item \textbf{Tau/muon ratio $17$: forced by compactification elimination.} The terminal ratio sits in the compactification channel itself. That channel carries only two candidates: the raw spinor count $16$ and its one-point compactification $17$. Only one survives the terminal value filter. The survivor is $F_2 = 17$.
\end{enumerate}

The correction chain is now closed for $\alpha^{-1}$, for the loop numerator, for the denominators, and for the bare proton, muon, and tau sectors. What changes from one mass sector to the next is not the standard of proof, but the channel through which the surviving structure is allowed to speak. In the proton sector the graph first sheds its topological clothing and reveals an edge-channel core. In the muon sector the story splits into two visibly different motions: one geometric, one compactificatory, and the value survives only when those motions are kept separate until the end. In the tau sector even that split disappears: only the compactification residue is left on the table. None of these moves is an escape hatch. Each is forced by the way the graph freezes some degrees of freedom and leaves exactly one live channel behind.



\subsubsection*{Mass-sector admissibility sector}

The proton value $1836$ cannot be allowed to enter as a decorative product assembled after the fact. By the time the mass sector is reached, the same eliminative discipline that fixed $\alpha^{-1}$ is already in force here as well: new compactification content must be separated from local graph content, and whatever survives locally must survive inside an explicitly delimited admissibility sector. Physically, the reader should picture a two-stage filter. First, the genuinely new compactification content is isolated so that it cannot be smuggled back into local graph arithmetic. Then the local residue is forced to show its face inside the primitive channel already carved out by the graph. If that separation fails, the proton factorization collapses into bookkeeping.

What the graph leaves standing is a two-layer channel and nothing else. One layer contributes the genuinely new compactification survivor $F_2 = 17$. The other contributes a local cofactor that must already be present in the primitive observable sector once the topological route has been reduced through $\chi d = E$. In more concrete terms: one hand carries the compactification prime, the other hand carries the smallest local cofactor the graph cannot eliminate. The task is therefore not to decorate $1836$ with factors, but to expose the only factorization that survives this split.

\textbf{Constraint:} The mass observable must separate genuinely new compactification content from local graph content. If these layers are mixed, the factorization ceases to be structural.

\textbf{Construction:} The new factor is $F_2 = \mathrm{suc}(\mathrm{clifford\text{-}dimension})$. The local cofactor sector consists of primitive monomials whose reduced value is the edge-channel cofactor $E^2 d = 108$. The higher-degree presentation $\chi^2 d^3$ is retained only as a derivable route into that reduced cofactor.

\textbf{Consequence:} The proton formula is no longer a floating product. It lies in an explicit admissibility sector: compactification prime times reduced primitive cofactor. Any rival factorization would have to blur this separation, reintroduce non-primitive local structure, or abandon the compactification channel already fixed upstream. The reduced cofactor is then classified exhaustively below.

\begin{code}

-- ¬ß Mass-sector admissibility: include F‚āā = 17, require a minimal-degree cofactor decomposition, and require dual spectral agreement.

-- ¬ß (1) F‚āā shares no prime with the base invariant product V¬∑E¬∑d¬∑Ōá = 144
law-F2-coprime-to-base :
  gcd F‚āā (vertexCountK4 * edgeCountK4 * degree-K4 * eulerChar-computed) ‚Č° 1
law-F2-coprime-to-base = refl   -- gcd(17, 144) = 1

-- ¬ß (2) Proton mass factors as F‚āā √ó 108
law-proton-F2-times-108 : proton-mass-bare ‚Č° F‚āā * 108
law-proton-F2-times-108 = refl  -- 1836 = 17 √ó 108

-- ¬ß (2) First path: Ōá¬≤¬∑d¬≥ = 108 (topological √ó degree)
law-cofactor-chi-sq-d-cube :
  (eulerChar-computed * eulerChar-computed)
  * (degree-K4 * degree-K4 * degree-K4) ‚Č° 108
law-cofactor-chi-sq-d-cube = refl  -- 4 √ó 27 = 108

-- ¬ß (2) Second path: E¬≤¬∑d = 108 (edge-squared √ó degree)
law-cofactor-E-sq-d :
  (edgeCountK4 * edgeCountK4) * degree-K4 ‚Č° 108
law-cofactor-E-sq-d = refl       -- 36 √ó 3 = 108

-- ¬ß (3) Spectral duality: both paths yield the same cofactor (via Ōá¬∑d = E)
law-dual-path-identity :
  (eulerChar-computed * eulerChar-computed) * (degree-K4 * degree-K4 * degree-K4)
  ‚Č° (edgeCountK4 * edgeCountK4) * degree-K4
law-dual-path-identity = refl    -- Ōá¬≤d¬≥ = E¬≤d via Ōád = E

-- ¬ß Degree minimality: E¬≤ = 36 < 108, so the cofactor forces degree at least 3 and both minimal paths use exactly degree 3.
law-degree2-maximum : edgeCountK4 * edgeCountK4 ‚Č° 36
law-degree2-maximum = refl       -- E¬≤ = 36 < 108

-- ¬ß Reduced primitive cofactor: E¬≤¬∑d sits exactly at the simplex cutoff.
edge-square-degree-monomial : ObservableMonomial
edge-square-degree-monomial = mono 0 2 1 0 0

-- ¬ß Topological presentation of the same cofactor before reduction via Ōá¬∑d = E.
topological-cofactor-monomial : ObservableMonomial
topological-cofactor-monomial = mono 0 0 3 2 0

-- ¬ß Reduced mass cofactors are exactly primitive monomials with the canonical value 108.
ReducedMassCofactor : ObservableMonomial ‚Üí Set
ReducedMassCofactor m = PrimitiveLocalMonomial m √ó (eval-monomial m ‚Č° 108)

-- ¬ß The mass observable sector separates the compactification factor from the reduced local cofactor.
record MassObservableSector : Set1 where
  field
    prime-factor-from-compactification : F‚āā ‚Č° suc clifford-dimension
    prime-factor-is-17 : F‚āā ‚Č° 17
    prime-factor-coprime-to-base :
      gcd F‚āā (vertexCountK4 * edgeCountK4 * degree-K4 * eulerChar-computed) ‚Č° 1
    reduced-cofactor : ObservableMonomial ‚Üí Set
    reduced-cofactor-exactly-primitive-108 :
      (m : ObservableMonomial) ‚Üí
      (reduced-cofactor m ‚Üí PrimitiveLocalMonomial m √ó (eval-monomial m ‚Č° 108))
      √ó ((PrimitiveLocalMonomial m √ó (eval-monomial m ‚Č° 108)) ‚Üí reduced-cofactor m)
    edge-channel-is-admissible : reduced-cofactor edge-square-degree-monomial
    edge-channel-is-primitive : PrimitiveLocalMonomial edge-square-degree-monomial
    edge-channel-is-108 : eval-monomial edge-square-degree-monomial ‚Č° 108
    topological-route-is-108 : eval-monomial topological-cofactor-monomial ‚Č° 108
    topological-route-reduces :
      eval-monomial topological-cofactor-monomial
      ‚Č° eval-monomial edge-square-degree-monomial
    topological-route-is-iterated : ¬¨ (PrimitiveLocalMonomial topological-cofactor-monomial)
    proton-mass-from-sector :
      proton-mass-bare ‚Č°
      F‚āā * eval-monomial edge-square-degree-monomial

theorem-mass-observable-sector : MassObservableSector
theorem-mass-observable-sector = record
  { prime-factor-from-compactification = refl
  ; prime-factor-is-17 = law16B-12-F2-is-17
  ; prime-factor-coprime-to-base = law-F2-coprime-to-base
  ; reduced-cofactor = ReducedMassCofactor
  ; reduced-cofactor-exactly-primitive-108 =
      őĽ m ‚Üí (őĽ adm ‚Üí adm) , (őĽ adm ‚Üí adm)
  ; edge-channel-is-admissible = (s‚Č§s (s‚Č§s (s‚Č§s z‚Č§n))) , refl
  ; edge-channel-is-primitive = s‚Č§s (s‚Č§s (s‚Č§s z‚Č§n))
  ; edge-channel-is-108 = refl
  ; topological-route-is-108 = refl
  ; topological-route-reduces = refl
  ; topological-route-is-iterated =
      őĽ adm ‚Üí
        primitive-vs-iterated-impossible {m = topological-cofactor-monomial}
          (s‚Č§s (s‚Č§s (s‚Č§s (s‚Č§s z‚Č§n))))
          adm
  ; proton-mass-from-sector = refl
  }

\end{code}

\subsubsection*{Reduced edge-channel elimination}

Reduction cannot stop at a slogan either. Once the cofactor has been pushed through $\chi d = E$, no hidden reservoir of alternatives remains. The surviving local channel is finite and exposed. There are only ten monomials in the edge channel $\{E,d\}$ of total degree at most three. If more than one of them hit $108$, the proton cofactor would remain ambiguous. If exactly one survives, the ambiguity is shattered and the edge channel closes.

\textbf{Constraint:} The reduced cofactor must live in the primitive edge channel and remain within the graph-forced cutoff $\deg \le d = 3$.

\textbf{Construction:} Enumerate all degree-$\le 3$ edge-channel monomials, evaluate each on $K_4$, and classify the unique survivor at value $108$.

\textbf{Consequence:} $E^2 d$ is not a convenient rewrite of the cofactor. It is the only reduced primitive edge-channel monomial that survives the value filter. Every weaker alternative is eliminated inside the finite case space itself. The proton mass is therefore forced as $F_2 \cdot E^2 d = 17 \cdot 108 = 1836$.

\begin{code}

-- ¬ß Phase 1: Enumerate the reduced edge-channel case space

-- ¬ß Edge-channel monomials keep only the reduced local basis {E,d}.
record EdgeChannelMonomial : Set where
  constructor edge-mono
  field
    exp-edge : ‚Ąē
    exp-degree : ‚Ąē

open EdgeChannelMonomial public

-- ¬ß Total degree in the reduced edge channel.
edge-channel-degree : EdgeChannelMonomial ‚Üí ‚Ąē
edge-channel-degree m = exp-edge m + exp-degree m

-- ¬ß Primitive reduced monomials are bounded by simplex depth.
PrimitiveEdgeChannelMonomial : EdgeChannelMonomial ‚Üí Set
PrimitiveEdgeChannelMonomial m = edge-channel-degree m ‚Č§ simplex-degree

-- ¬ß Evaluation of reduced edge-channel monomials on K‚āĄ.
eval-edge-channel : EdgeChannelMonomial ‚Üí ‚Ąē
eval-edge-channel m =
  (edgeCountK4 ^ exp-edge m) * (degree-K4 ^ exp-degree m)

-- ¬ß The ten degree-‚Č§3 edge-channel candidates.
data ReducedEdgeCase : Set where
  mass-case-1   : ReducedEdgeCase
  mass-case-d   : ReducedEdgeCase
  mass-case-E   : ReducedEdgeCase
  mass-case-d2  : ReducedEdgeCase
  mass-case-Ed  : ReducedEdgeCase
  mass-case-E2  : ReducedEdgeCase
  mass-case-d3  : ReducedEdgeCase
  mass-case-Ed2 : ReducedEdgeCase
  mass-case-E2d : ReducedEdgeCase
  mass-case-E3  : ReducedEdgeCase

interpret-reduced-edge-case : ReducedEdgeCase ‚Üí EdgeChannelMonomial
interpret-reduced-edge-case mass-case-1   = edge-mono 0 0
interpret-reduced-edge-case mass-case-d   = edge-mono 0 1
interpret-reduced-edge-case mass-case-E   = edge-mono 1 0
interpret-reduced-edge-case mass-case-d2  = edge-mono 0 2
interpret-reduced-edge-case mass-case-Ed  = edge-mono 1 1
interpret-reduced-edge-case mass-case-E2  = edge-mono 2 0
interpret-reduced-edge-case mass-case-d3  = edge-mono 0 3
interpret-reduced-edge-case mass-case-Ed2 = edge-mono 1 2
interpret-reduced-edge-case mass-case-E2d = edge-mono 2 1
interpret-reduced-edge-case mass-case-E3  = edge-mono 3 0

eval-reduced-edge-case : ReducedEdgeCase ‚Üí ‚Ąē
eval-reduced-edge-case c = eval-edge-channel (interpret-reduced-edge-case c)

ReducedEdgePrimitive : ReducedEdgeCase ‚Üí Set
ReducedEdgePrimitive c =
  PrimitiveEdgeChannelMonomial (interpret-reduced-edge-case c)

-- ¬ß Every candidate lies inside the reduced primitive cutoff.
reduced-edge-case-primitive :
  (c : ReducedEdgeCase) ‚Üí
  ReducedEdgePrimitive c
reduced-edge-case-primitive mass-case-1   = z‚Č§n
reduced-edge-case-primitive mass-case-d   = s‚Č§s z‚Č§n
reduced-edge-case-primitive mass-case-E   = s‚Č§s z‚Č§n
reduced-edge-case-primitive mass-case-d2  = s‚Č§s (s‚Č§s z‚Č§n)
reduced-edge-case-primitive mass-case-Ed  = s‚Č§s (s‚Č§s z‚Č§n)
reduced-edge-case-primitive mass-case-E2  = s‚Č§s (s‚Č§s z‚Č§n)
reduced-edge-case-primitive mass-case-d3  = s‚Č§s (s‚Č§s (s‚Č§s z‚Č§n))
reduced-edge-case-primitive mass-case-Ed2 = s‚Č§s (s‚Č§s (s‚Č§s z‚Č§n))
reduced-edge-case-primitive mass-case-E2d = s‚Č§s (s‚Č§s (s‚Č§s z‚Č§n))
reduced-edge-case-primitive mass-case-E3  = s‚Č§s (s‚Č§s (s‚Č§s z‚Č§n))

-- ¬ß Phase 2: Evaluate every candidate numerically

-- ¬ß The reduced candidate values are fixed numerically.
law-reduced-edge-1 : eval-reduced-edge-case mass-case-1 ‚Č° 1
law-reduced-edge-1 = refl

law-reduced-edge-d : eval-reduced-edge-case mass-case-d ‚Č° 3
law-reduced-edge-d = refl

law-reduced-edge-E : eval-reduced-edge-case mass-case-E ‚Č° 6
law-reduced-edge-E = refl

law-reduced-edge-d2 : eval-reduced-edge-case mass-case-d2 ‚Č° 9
law-reduced-edge-d2 = refl

law-reduced-edge-Ed : eval-reduced-edge-case mass-case-Ed ‚Č° 18
law-reduced-edge-Ed = refl

law-reduced-edge-E2 : eval-reduced-edge-case mass-case-E2 ‚Č° 36
law-reduced-edge-E2 = refl

law-reduced-edge-d3 : eval-reduced-edge-case mass-case-d3 ‚Č° 27
law-reduced-edge-d3 = refl

law-reduced-edge-Ed2 : eval-reduced-edge-case mass-case-Ed2 ‚Č° 54
law-reduced-edge-Ed2 = refl

law-reduced-edge-E2d : eval-reduced-edge-case mass-case-E2d ‚Č° 108
law-reduced-edge-E2d = refl

law-reduced-edge-E3 : eval-reduced-edge-case mass-case-E3 ‚Č° 216
law-reduced-edge-E3 = refl

-- ¬ß Exactly one reduced primitive edge-channel monomial evaluates to 108.
reduced-edge-108-survivor-unique :
  (c : ReducedEdgeCase) ‚Üí
  eval-reduced-edge-case c ‚Č° 108 ‚Üí
  c ‚Č° mass-case-E2d
reduced-edge-108-survivor-unique mass-case-1 ()
reduced-edge-108-survivor-unique mass-case-d ()
reduced-edge-108-survivor-unique mass-case-E ()
reduced-edge-108-survivor-unique mass-case-d2 ()
reduced-edge-108-survivor-unique mass-case-Ed ()
reduced-edge-108-survivor-unique mass-case-E2 ()
reduced-edge-108-survivor-unique mass-case-d3 ()
reduced-edge-108-survivor-unique mass-case-Ed2 ()
reduced-edge-108-survivor-unique mass-case-E2d refl = refl
reduced-edge-108-survivor-unique mass-case-E3 ()

-- ¬ß Classification record for the reduced edge-channel cofactor.
record ReducedEdgeCofactorClassification : Set where
  field
    bounded-case-space :
      (c : ReducedEdgeCase) ‚Üí
      PrimitiveEdgeChannelMonomial (interpret-reduced-edge-case c)
    unique-108-case :
      (c : ReducedEdgeCase) ‚Üí
      eval-reduced-edge-case c ‚Č° 108 ‚Üí
      c ‚Č° mass-case-E2d
    survivor-is-108 : eval-reduced-edge-case mass-case-E2d ‚Č° 108
    proton-cofactor-is-survivor :
      edge-mono 2 1 ‚Č° interpret-reduced-edge-case mass-case-E2d
    proton-mass-is-forced : proton-mass-bare ‚Č° F‚āā * eval-reduced-edge-case mass-case-E2d

theorem-reduced-edge-cofactor-classification : ReducedEdgeCofactorClassification
theorem-reduced-edge-cofactor-classification = record
  { bounded-case-space = reduced-edge-case-primitive
  ; unique-108-case = reduced-edge-108-survivor-unique
  ; survivor-is-108 = refl
  ; proton-cofactor-is-survivor = refl
  ; proton-mass-is-forced = refl
  }

\end{code}

\subsubsection*{Muon mixed-channel elimination}

The muon sector cannot be left at the level of a suggestive product either. Once the proton has been forced, the next pressure point is obvious: is $207 = d^2(E+F_2)$ equally unavoidable, or is this the first place where convenience re-enters? The graph blocks that escape by forcing another finite sector. This time the structure does not collapse into a single multiplicative channel immediately. Instead it visibly splits into two pieces that do different jobs. The geometric contribution measures pure local repetition along the degree axis. The compactification contribution measures the extra residue that appears only when the edge channel and the compactified spinor channel are allowed to meet additively. Both spaces are small enough to classify exhaustively, so nothing can hide behind informal plausibility.

\textbf{Constraint:} The muon observable must keep the local geometric square distinct from the compactification correction. Repeating either channel would double-count structure and destroy the decomposition.

\textbf{Construction:} Enumerate the degree-only candidates $1,d,d^2,d^3$ and the mixed additive candidates $0,E,F_2,E+F_2$. Exactly one candidate in the first list evaluates to $9$, and exactly one candidate in the second evaluates to $23$.

\textbf{Consequence:} The product $d^2(E+F_2)$ is not a guess. It is the only product assembled from the unique geometric-square survivor and the unique mixed additive survivor. Any alternative muon factorization would have to alter one of those two finite survivor classes, and neither class has any room left. The muon bare mass is therefore forced as $207$.

\begin{code}

-- ¬ß Phase 1: Scan the pure degree channel

-- ¬ß Pure degree-channel candidates for the muon geometric factor.
data MuonDegreeCase : Set where
  muon-degree-1  : MuonDegreeCase
  muon-degree-d  : MuonDegreeCase
  muon-degree-d2 : MuonDegreeCase
  muon-degree-d3 : MuonDegreeCase

eval-muon-degree-case : MuonDegreeCase ‚Üí ‚Ąē
eval-muon-degree-case muon-degree-1  = 1
eval-muon-degree-case muon-degree-d  = degree-K4
eval-muon-degree-case muon-degree-d2 = degree-K4 * degree-K4
eval-muon-degree-case muon-degree-d3 = degree-K4 * degree-K4 * degree-K4

law-muon-degree-1 : eval-muon-degree-case muon-degree-1 ‚Č° 1
law-muon-degree-1 = refl

law-muon-degree-d : eval-muon-degree-case muon-degree-d ‚Č° 3
law-muon-degree-d = refl

law-muon-degree-d2 : eval-muon-degree-case muon-degree-d2 ‚Č° 9
law-muon-degree-d2 = refl

law-muon-degree-d3 : eval-muon-degree-case muon-degree-d3 ‚Č° 27
law-muon-degree-d3 = refl

muon-degree-9-survivor-unique :
  (c : MuonDegreeCase) ‚Üí
  eval-muon-degree-case c ‚Č° 9 ‚Üí
  c ‚Č° muon-degree-d2
muon-degree-9-survivor-unique muon-degree-1 ()
muon-degree-9-survivor-unique muon-degree-d ()
muon-degree-9-survivor-unique muon-degree-d2 refl = refl
muon-degree-9-survivor-unique muon-degree-d3 ()

-- ¬ß Phase 2: Scan the mixed compactification channel

-- ¬ß Mixed additive candidates for the compactification-edge factor.
data MuonMixedCase : Set where
  muon-mix-0    : MuonMixedCase
  muon-mix-E    : MuonMixedCase
  muon-mix-F2   : MuonMixedCase
  muon-mix-EF2  : MuonMixedCase

eval-muon-mixed-case : MuonMixedCase ‚Üí ‚Ąē
eval-muon-mixed-case muon-mix-0   = 0
eval-muon-mixed-case muon-mix-E   = edgeCountK4
eval-muon-mixed-case muon-mix-F2  = F‚āā
eval-muon-mixed-case muon-mix-EF2 = edgeCountK4 + F‚āā

law-muon-mix-0 : eval-muon-mixed-case muon-mix-0 ‚Č° 0
law-muon-mix-0 = refl

law-muon-mix-E : eval-muon-mixed-case muon-mix-E ‚Č° 6
law-muon-mix-E = refl

law-muon-mix-F2 : eval-muon-mixed-case muon-mix-F2 ‚Č° 17
law-muon-mix-F2 = refl

law-muon-mix-EF2 : eval-muon-mixed-case muon-mix-EF2 ‚Č° 23
law-muon-mix-EF2 = refl

muon-mixed-23-survivor-unique :
  (c : MuonMixedCase) ‚Üí
  eval-muon-mixed-case c ‚Č° 23 ‚Üí
  c ‚Č° muon-mix-EF2
muon-mixed-23-survivor-unique muon-mix-0 ()
muon-mixed-23-survivor-unique muon-mix-E ()
muon-mixed-23-survivor-unique muon-mix-F2 ()
muon-mixed-23-survivor-unique muon-mix-EF2 refl = refl

-- ¬ß Phase 3: Multiply the two unique survivors

-- ¬ß Classification record for the muon bare mass factorization.
record MuonBareClassification : Set where
  field
    unique-degree-9 :
      (c : MuonDegreeCase) ‚Üí
      eval-muon-degree-case c ‚Č° 9 ‚Üí
      c ‚Č° muon-degree-d2
    unique-mixed-23 :
      (c : MuonMixedCase) ‚Üí
      eval-muon-mixed-case c ‚Č° 23 ‚Üí
      c ‚Č° muon-mix-EF2
    degree-survivor-is-9 : eval-muon-degree-case muon-degree-d2 ‚Č° 9
    mixed-survivor-is-23 : eval-muon-mixed-case muon-mix-EF2 ‚Č° 23
    muon-factorization-forced :
      muon-mass-bare ‚Č° eval-muon-degree-case muon-degree-d2 * eval-muon-mixed-case muon-mix-EF2
    muon-bare-is-forced : muon-mass-bare ‚Č° 207

theorem-muon-bare-classification : MuonBareClassification
theorem-muon-bare-classification = record
  { unique-degree-9 = muon-degree-9-survivor-unique
  ; unique-mixed-23 = muon-mixed-23-survivor-unique
  ; degree-survivor-is-9 = refl
  ; mixed-survivor-is-23 = refl
  ; muon-factorization-forced = refl
  ; muon-bare-is-forced = refl
  }

\end{code}

\subsubsection*{Tau compactification elimination}

The tau sector is smaller, but it cannot be treated more loosely. Once the compactification channel has appeared in both proton and muon formulas, the terminal ratio $\tau/\mu = 17$ must survive by the same standard or the chain weakens at its end. Here the candidate space is minimal, and that is exactly why the section matters. All geometric clutter has already burned away. Nothing remains but the compactification channel itself: the raw spinor count and its one-point compactification. If both remained viable, the terminal ratio would still carry unresolved slack. If only one survives, the compactification channel closes without residue.

\textbf{Constraint:} The terminal lepton ratio must come entirely from the compactification channel, with no leftover geometric factor.

\textbf{Construction:} Enumerate the two compactification candidates: the raw spinor count $\mathrm{Cl}(\mathbb{R}^4)$ dimension $= 16$, and its one-point compactification $F_2 = 17$.

\textbf{Consequence:} The tau/muon ratio is not merely equal to $F_2$. It is the unique survivor of the compactification channel. The alternative value $16$ is not approximately wrong or philosophically weaker; it is eliminated inside the only candidate space available. The terminal ratio is therefore forced as $17$.

\begin{code}

-- ¬ß Compactification-channel candidates for the terminal tau/muon ratio.
data TauCompactificationCase : Set where
  tau-raw-spinor     : TauCompactificationCase
  tau-compactified   : TauCompactificationCase

eval-tau-compactification : TauCompactificationCase ‚Üí ‚Ąē
eval-tau-compactification tau-raw-spinor   = clifford-dimension
eval-tau-compactification tau-compactified = F‚āā

law-tau-raw-spinor-16 : eval-tau-compactification tau-raw-spinor ‚Č° 16
law-tau-raw-spinor-16 = refl

law-tau-compactified-17 : eval-tau-compactification tau-compactified ‚Č° 17
law-tau-compactified-17 = refl

tau-17-survivor-unique :
  (c : TauCompactificationCase) ‚Üí
  eval-tau-compactification c ‚Č° 17 ‚Üí
  c ‚Č° tau-compactified
tau-17-survivor-unique tau-raw-spinor ()
tau-17-survivor-unique tau-compactified refl = refl

-- ¬ß Classification record for the terminal compactification survivor.
record TauBareClassification : Set where
  field
    compactification-adds-one : F‚āā ‚Č° suc clifford-dimension
    raw-spinor-is-16 : eval-tau-compactification tau-raw-spinor ‚Č° 16
    compactified-spinor-is-17 : eval-tau-compactification tau-compactified ‚Č° 17
    unique-17-case :
      (c : TauCompactificationCase) ‚Üí
      eval-tau-compactification c ‚Č° 17 ‚Üí
      c ‚Č° tau-compactified
    tau-ratio-is-forced : tau-muon-bare ‚Č° eval-tau-compactification tau-compactified
    tau-ratio-is-17 : tau-muon-bare ‚Č° 17

theorem-tau-bare-classification : TauBareClassification
theorem-tau-bare-classification = record
  { compactification-adds-one = refl
  ; raw-spinor-is-16 = refl
  ; compactified-spinor-is-17 = refl
  ; unique-17-case = tau-17-survivor-unique
  ; tau-ratio-is-forced = refl
  ; tau-ratio-is-17 = refl
  }


\end{code}

%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
\section{Physics Presupposes Distinction}
\label{sec:discrete-continuum:physics-presupposes-distinction}
%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ

The final asymmetry can no longer remain implicit. Up to this point the book has forced the physical record from the invariant spine of $K_4$. A weaker reader could still try to reverse the dependence and say that physics is the deeper object, while $K_4$ is only one successful encoding of it. That escape collapses at the type level.

A physical record here is not a free-standing bag of measured numbers. It is threaded together by identity witnesses: the anchor tying dimension to $K_4$, the chain laws transporting that anchor through every other field, and the closure proofs showing that rival admissible records collapse to the same object. If identity is removed, these fields are not weakened. They become uninhabitable. Physics therefore does not generate distinction. It consumes the logical infrastructure distinction has already forced.

\textbf{Constraint:} An admissible physical record already contains equality data. The anchor and chain constraints of \texttt{K4PhysRep} presuppose the identity type, and the identity type presupposes the distinction-theoretic base established at the beginning of the book. If distinction were denied, the physics record would not become deeper; it would become uninhabitable.

\textbf{Construction:} Extract the record's own equality witness and use it to state the direction of dependence in two forms. First, every inhabitant of \texttt{K4PhysRep} yields a distinction witness. Second, assuming $\neg\,\mathit{Distinction}$ annihilates every purported physical record, so physics without distinction collapses to $\bot$.

\textbf{Consequence:} The dependency arrow cannot be reversed. Physics is an invariant of $K_4$, but $K_4$ is not reconstructed from a prior physics layer. The physical record already lives inside the logical world distinction opened. Physics as fundament is therefore eliminated by self-presupposition.

This does not close the book by withdrawing from physics into logic. It closes the directional ambiguity that would otherwise remain at the threshold. The route from distinction to $K_4$ to the physical record is now fixed in one direction only. What remains is not another internal theorem, but the outward question that this fixed direction leaves behind: what follows once physics has been shown to depend on distinction rather than ground it?

\phantomsection\label{law:discrete-continuum:physics-presupposes-distinction}
\phantomsection\label{law:discrete-continuum:physics-without-distinction-uninhabitable}

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Any admissible K‚āĄ physics record already consumes distinction.
physics-presupposes-distinction : (p : K4PhysRep) ‚Üí Distinction
physics-presupposes-distinction p with K4PhysRep.anchor p
... | refl = Two-distinction

-- ¬ß Denying distinction makes the physics record uninhabitable.
physics-without-distinction-uninhabitable : ¬¨ Distinction ‚Üí K4PhysRep ‚Üí ‚ä•
physics-without-distinction-uninhabitable noDistinction p with K4PhysRep.anchor p
... | refl = noDistinction Two-distinction

-- ¬ß Every physical record therefore implies the irrefutability of distinction.
physics-presupposes-distinction-irrefutable : (p : K4PhysRep) ‚Üí ¬¨¬¨ Distinction
physics-presupposes-distinction-irrefutable p noDistinction =
  physics-without-distinction-uninhabitable noDistinction p
\end{code}

\chapter{The Invitation}
\label{ch:invitation}

The chain has now been closed in the only direction that survives scrutiny. Distinction forces the two-class carrier; the carrier forces $K_4$; $K_4$ forces the invariant ledger; and the admissible physical record is shown, in the end, to presuppose the very distinction from which the chain began. What remains is therefore not a request for lenience. It is a ledger of the surviving debts. The proof no longer waits for completion, but it does leave obligations behind: comparison with rival physical explanations, sharpening of the mathematical form, and a philosophical account of what kind of necessity has been uncovered.

The invitation is the place where those debts are named without reopening the chain. It states, for each audience in turn, where resistance is still possible, where the real pressure now falls, and what kind of response would count as serious.

\section{To Physicists}
\label{sec:invitation:to-physicists}

This manuscript does not ask for deference to formalism, and it does
not claim a new empire of prediction. It makes a narrower and harsher
claim. Starting from distinction, and permitting only structures that
survive explicit elimination, one is driven to a graph, to an invariant
ledger, and then to a small cluster of computed quantities that can be
compared with physics. Those comparisons are now on the table in their
final form.

After the last asymmetry has been made explicit, the central physical
question is no longer whether physics secretly grounds the chain. That
path has been closed. The live question is whether the surviving match
to measured structure is accidental, derivative, or telling us something
real about how much of physics is already fixed before phenomenology
begins.

If the match is accidental, the standard of reply is clear. One must
produce another structure with comparable rigidity and no hidden freedom
that reaches the same constants with equal precision. If the match is
not accidental, the burden changes. One must explain why elimination on
such a small discrete spine reaches as far into the measured world as it
does.

The physical debt is therefore comparative rather than decorative. The
chain is not asking for applause. It is asking whether any equally rigid
route reaches the same measured structure, or whether this one is
already telling us that more of physics is fixed before model-building
begins than the usual story admits.

\section{To Mathematicians}
\label{sec:invitation:to-mathematicians}

The formal chain is closed, but closure is not the same as exhaustion.
What survives here is strong enough to be interrogated from several
directions at once: proof-theoretic, categorical, spectral, and
geometric. The invitation is therefore not to repair a missing proof,
but to decide whether the surviving structure admits a sharper and more
illuminating formulation.

Several pressure points are immediate:

\begin{itemize}
\item $K_4$ uniqueness is proven by exhaustive classification
  (\texttt{theorem-4-unique-fixpoint}).  Can the proof be sharpened to a
  single categorical universal property?
\item The fold establishes algebraic stability for finite lattices.  Can
  category theory (e.g.\ topos-theoretic methods) extend this to a
  sheaf-theoretic statement?
\item What is the relationship between the drift operad and existing
  operadic structures in mathematical physics (e.g.\ Kontsevich formality)?
\end{itemize}

The mathematics is self-contained. The open work is not to rescue the
argument from incompleteness, but to determine whether the same survivor
can be characterized in a form that is even more rigid, more general, or
more transparent.

This is the largest technical debt left by the manuscript. The theorem
chain stands, but its strongest surviving form may still be ahead of us:
a presentation in which classification becomes inevitability in an even
sharper sense than exhaustive proof can display.

\section{To Philosophers}
\label{sec:invitation:to-philosophers}

Every epoch produces a new observer and a new key to intelligibility.
One age trusts geometry. Another trusts experiment. Another trusts
symmetry, or information, or computation. This work belongs to a later
observer: one who asks what remains when every explanatory surplus is
forced to justify itself and only formal survival is allowed to stay.

That shift matters. The manuscript does not begin from world, mind, or
measurement and then ascend toward abstraction. It begins from the bare
fact of distinction and asks what can survive collapse. If physical
structure reappears at the end of that descent, then the philosophical
problem is no longer how mathematics is applied from outside. It is how
far the order of the world is already exposed by the conditions of
stable form.

\begin{itemize}
\item If physical constants are determined by logic, what does this say
  about the nature of physical law?
\item What is the ontological status of a mathematical structure that
  computes the parameters of the universe?
\item What becomes of scientific realism if successive traditions are
  not competitors but partial witnesses of the same invariant order?
\end{itemize}

The philosophical debt is therefore neither ornament nor rescue. It is
to say what has happened when a structure reached by elimination behaves
less like a formal toy and more like an exposed layer of the world. If
that question is avoided, the proof remains intact, but its meaning is
left underdescribed.

Taken together, these are the debts that survive the end of the proof:
empirical comparison, mathematical sharpening, and philosophical
interpretation. None of them reopens the chain from distinction to
$K_4$ to the physical record. They begin only after that chain has been
forced. The conclusion therefore does not add one more theorem. It names
what kind of survivor has been left in our hands.

\chapter{Conclusion}
\label{ch:conclusion}

We began with a mark on a blank page---the simplest possible act:
separating something from nothing. Nothing in the chain that followed
was allowed to stand by taste, elegance, or convenience. The question
was harsher from the beginning: what remains if every unstable
alternative is forced to fall away?

By the end, even the final temptation to reverse the chain had been
eliminated. The physical record did not stand behind distinction as a
deeper source from which $K_4$ could be rebuilt. It stood downstream,
already inhabiting the equality and distinction structure it might have
claimed to explain. The dependence ran one way only.

Through the entire chain, the method stayed the same. Not invention,
but elimination. Not ornament, but survival. Equality had to survive.
Negation had to survive. Classification had to survive. Reachability,
presentation, fixpoint, graph, arithmetic, spectrum, observable: each
was admitted only where contradiction failed to destroy it.

What survived was small. A distinction. Two sides. Four cases. One
graph. A narrow arithmetic. A rigid spectrum. A short list of
invariants. That is why the result is unsettling. It does not arrive
as a grand architecture chosen for elegance. It arrives as residue.

No triumph should be claimed here. This work does not say that the
physicists were wrong and the logicians were right, or that the
mathematicians were early and the philosophers confused. It says
something more difficult: that they may all have been watching the same
constraint from different entrances.

Each epoch brings forth a new observer and a new key to knowledge. One
measures. One classifies. One formalizes. One asks what symmetry will
allow. One asks what computation will certify. This book belongs to
that last lineage. It asks a single question and refuses to relax it:
what if nothing remains except distinction, consistency, invariance,
and the structures they force?

Then almost everything disappears.

No new empire of prediction is announced here. No license is claimed
to outrank experiment. The modest claim is the stronger one: we do not
need to show more than that the great observations and achievements of
their respective traditions may be locally right together. Brouwer's
witness, Martin-L√∂f's discipline, Euler's count, Cayley's symmetry,
Noether's invariance, Curie's asymmetry, Dirac's severity, Feynman's
consistency of paths---none is overturned here. They survive.

What now survives with them is a threefold obligation. The empirical
question is comparative: whether any equally rigid route reaches the
same measured structure without concealed freedom. The mathematical
question is sharpening: whether the survivor can be recast in a form
still more inevitable than exhaustive classification. The philosophical
question is interpretive: what kind of necessity has been exposed if a
structure forced by elimination reaches so far into the world we
measure.

If this manuscript has earned any right to continue beyond its last
code line, it is because it leaves us with a cleaner question than the
one with which it began. Why should the survivor of formal elimination
resemble the measured world at all? Why should a structure forced by
logic meet the constants of physics where they are sharpest?

Those are not repairs to a broken argument. They are the proper debts of
one that has reached its limit without collapsing. The proof ends where
comparison, sharpening, and interpretation begin.

That is not a failure of the book. It is the honest end of the journey.

The code stops here. The investigation does not.

\textit{Quod erat demonstrandum.}

\end{document}
